variables from its enclosing scope, even after the scope exits"},{"id":"c","text":"A sealed class that wraps a delegate"},{"id":"d","text":"A lambda expression that is immediately invoked upon Single() returns default."},{"id":"b","text":"First() throws InvalidOperationException if empty; FirstOrDefault() returns default(T) if empty."},{"id":"c","text":"First() returns null if empty; FirstOrDefault() throws an exception."},{"id":"d","text":"Both First() and FirstOrDefault() throw an exception if the sequence is empty."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q3","text":"What does the Aggregate overload with a seed value do in LINQ?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It starts accumulation from the seed value instead of the first element, applying the accumulator to each subsequent element."},{"id":"b","text":"It groups elements starting from a specific index."},{"id":"c","text":"It sets the initial size of the result collection."},{"id":"d","text":"It specifies the default value if the sequence is empty."}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q4","text":"What problem can arise when capturing a loop variable inside a lambda that runs after the loop?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The lambda cannot be compiled because loop variables are not in scope."},{"id":"b","text":"All lambdas capture the same variable reference, so when executed after the loop, they all see the final loop value rather than the value at capture time."},{"id":"c","text":"The lambda creates a defensive copy of the loop variable automatically."},{"id":"d","text":"The loop variable is treated as a const inside the lambda."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q5","text":"How does partial application differ from currying in C# functional patterns?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Partial application and currying are the same concept."},{"id":"b","text":"Partial application fixes some arguments of a function returning a new function for the remaining arguments; currying transforms an n-argument function into n chained single-argument functions."},{"id":"c","text":"Currying fixes one argument; partial application requires fixing all arguments."},{"id":"d","text":"Partial application applies a function to a collection; currying applies it to a single value."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q6","text":"What is an anonymous method in C# and how does it differ from a lambda?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Anonymous methods use the 'delegate' keyword and cannot capture variables; lambdas can."},{"id":"b","text":"An anonymous method is declared with the 'delegate' keyword and a block body; a lambda uses '=>' syntax. Both can capture variables, but lambdas also support expression bodies and can be converted to expression trees."},{"id":"c","text":"Anonymous methods are faster than lambdas at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"Lambdas are only usable with LINQ; anonymous methods work everywhere."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q7","text":"What is the standard event pattern in C# and what is the signature of EventHandler<TEventArgs>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"EventHandler<TEventArgs> has signature: void Handler(TEventArgs e)."},{"id":"b","text":"EventHandler<TEventArgs> has signature: void Handler(object sender, TEventArgs e), following the standard pattern where sender is the event source and TEventArgs derives from EventArgs."},{"id":"c","text":"EventHandler<TEventArgs> returns TEventArgs and takes no parameters."},{"id":"d","text":"EventHandler<TEventArgs> is a generic class, not a delegate."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q8","text":"How do you create a custom EventArgs class to pass data with an event?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Inherit from Delegate and add properties for the event data."},{"id":"b","text":"Inherit from EventArgs and add properties for the data; use EventHandler<YourEventArgs> as the event's delegate type."},{"id":"c","text":"Implement IEventArgs interface with the required properties."},{"id":"d","text":"Pass a Dictionary<string,object> directly to the EventHandler."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q9","text":"What is a Memory<T> slice and how is it created from an existing Memory<T>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Memory<T> does not support slicing."},{"id":"b","text":"A Memory<T> slice is a view over a sub-range, created via memory.Slice(start) or memory.Slice(start, length), pointing to the same underlying data without copying."},{"id":"c","text":"Slicing a Memory<T> creates a new array with copied elements."},{"id":"d","text":"You create a Memory<T> slice using Array.Copy()"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q10","text":"What is the performance advantage of using typeof(T).GetProperty().GetValue() vs a compiled expression for repeated property reads via reflection?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Reflection GetValue is always faster due to JIT caching."},{"id":"b","text":"Compiling an Expression<Func<T,TProp>> into a delegate is significantly faster for repeated calls because it avoids per-call reflection overhead, while raw reflection involves boxing and metadata lookup each time."},{"id":"c","text":"Both have identical performance."},{"id":"d","text":"Dynamic keyword is always faster than both."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q11","text":"What does [Conditional(\"DEBUG\")] attribute do when applied to a method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It makes the method run only on debug builds of the runtime."},{"id":"b","text":"It causes the compiler to omit all call sites to that method when the specified symbol (e.g. DEBUG) is not defined, effectively making calls no-ops without removing the method itself."},{"id":"c","text":"It adds a breakpoint to every call of the method."},{"id":"d","text":"It converts the method to an inline call."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q12","text":"What is an Expression Tree in C# and how does it differ from a compiled lambda?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An expression tree and a compiled lambda are identical at runtime."},{"id":"b","text":"An expression tree (Expression<Func<T>>) represents code as data (a tree of nodes) that can be inspected and translated (e.g., to SQL); a compiled lambda is executable IL code."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression trees are used only for event handling."},{"id":"d","text":"Expression trees can only represent boolean predicates."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q13","text":"How do LINQ query providers like Entity Framework use expression trees?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They compile expression trees into IL and execute them in-process."},{"id":"b","text":"They traverse the expression tree to translate it into another language (such as SQL), enabling execution on a remote data source rather than in-memory."},{"id":"c","text":"They use expression trees solely for caching query results."},{"id":"d","text":"They convert expression trees into delegate chains automatically."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q14","text":"What is the Action<> delegate type in C# and how does it differ from Func<>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Action<> can return a value; Func<> cannot."},{"id":"b","text":"Action<> is a delegate that takes zero or more parameters and returns void; Func<> takes zero or more parameters and always returns a value (the last type parameter is the return type)."},{"id":"c","text":"Action<> is only used for event handlers; Func<> is for LINQ."},{"id":"d","text":"They are identical except for naming conventions."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q15","text":"How do you prevent a memory leak when a long-lived object subscribes to an event on a short-lived object?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Use static events to avoid instance references."},{"id":"b","text":"Unsubscribe the event handler (using -=) when the subscriber is done, or use the weak event pattern so the publisher does not prevent the subscriber from being garbage collected."},{"id":"c","text":"Mark the subscriber class as sealed to prevent GC issues."},{"id":"d","text":"Call GC.Collect() after each event firing."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q16","text":"How can extension methods be used to build a fluent API for configuration?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Extension methods cannot be used in fluent APIs because they are static."},{"id":"b","text":"Each extension method takes the configuration object as 'this' parameter, modifies it, and returns it, allowing chained calls like: config.SetHost(...).SetPort(...).EnableTLS()."},{"id":"c","text":"Fluent APIs require interface default implementations, not extension methods."},{"id":"d","text":"Extension methods in fluent APIs must return void."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q17","text":"What is the difference between Expression.Lambda() and Expression.Call() in the System.Linq.Expressions API?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression.Lambda() creates a method call node; Expression.Call() creates a lambda node."},{"id":"b","text":"Expression.Lambda() creates a LambdaExpression that wraps a body and parameters into a callable delegate shape; Expression.Call() creates a node representing a method call within an expression tree."},{"id":"c","text":"They are interchangeable."},{"id":"d","text":"Expression.Call() can only call static methods."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q18","text":"What does the 'this' modifier do in an extension method parameter, and what class must contain the extension method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It makes the parameter optional; any class can contain extension methods."},{"id":"b","text":"It designates the parameter as the type being extended, allowing the method to be called as an instance method; the extension method must be in a non-generic, non-nested static class."},{"id":"c","text":"It marks the parameter as a ref parameter."},{"id":"d","text":"It allows the extension method to access private members of the extended type."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q19","text":"In the context of custom event args, why should TEventArgs inherit from EventArgs rather than being a plain class?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The compiler requires EventArgs inheritance for all event delegates."},{"id":"b","text":"It follows the .NET event design guideline, enables compatibility with EventHandler<TEventArgs>, and allows the event args to be extended with additional properties while maintaining a consistent pattern."},{"id":"c","text":"EventArgs provides automatic thread-safety for event data."},{"id":"d","text":"Only EventArgs subclasses can be passed to event handlers."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q20","text":"What does the LINQ Distinct() method use to determine equality between elements?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It always uses reference equality."},{"id":"b","text":"It uses the default equality comparer for the element type (EqualityComparer<T>.Default), or an IEqualityComparer<T> overload if provided."},{"id":"c","text":"It uses the == operator only."},{"id":"d","text":"It uses GetHashCode() but ignores Equals()."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q21","text":"What does a ref struct guarantee that a regular struct does not?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A ref struct is always allocated on the heap."},{"id":"b","text":"A ref struct is guaranteed to live only on the stack; it cannot be boxed, stored in arrays, assigned to interfaces, or used as generic type arguments, providing safety guarantees for stack-only lifetime."},{"id":"c","text":"A ref struct cannot contain any reference-type fields."},{"id":"d","text":"A ref struct is thread-safe by default."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q22","text":"How would you use reflection to determine if a type implements a specific interface?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"typeof(MyType).HasInterface(typeof(IMyInterface))"},{"id":"b","text":"typeof(IMyInterface).IsAssignableFrom(typeof(MyType)) or typeof(MyType).GetInterfaces().Contains(typeof(IMyInterface))"},{"id":"c","text":"MyType is IMyInterface (this is not reflection)"},{"id":"d","text":"Assembly.Implements(typeof(MyType), typeof(IMyInterface))"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q23","text":"How can you build and compile an expression tree that represents the lambda x => x * 2?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression.Lambda(x => x * 2).Compile()"},{"id":"b","text":"var param = Expression.Parameter(typeof(int), \"x\"); var body = Expression.Multiply(param, Expression.Constant(2)); var lambda = Expression.Lambda<Func<int,int>>(body, param); var fn = lambda.Compile();"},{"id":"c","text":"Expression.Create<Func<int,int>>(x => x * 2)"},{"id":"d","text":"ExpressionBuilder.Build(\"x * 2\").Compile<int>()"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q24","text":"What is memoization as a functional pattern and how can it be implemented with a dictionary and Func<T,TResult>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Memoization is the same as lazy initialization."},{"id":"b","text":"Memoization caches the result of a function for a given input; it can be implemented by wrapping a Func<T,TResult> to check a dictionary for a cached result before calling the original function."},{"id":"c","text":"Memoization stores intermediate results in a stack."},{"id":"d","text":"Memoization is only applicable to void methods."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q25","text":"What is the purpose of the [Serializable] attribute in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It enables JSON serialization via System.Text.Json."},{"id":"b","text":"It marks a class so the .NET binary formatter can serialize/deserialize its instances; without it, BinaryFormatter will throw a SerializationException."},{"id":"c","text":"It makes all properties of the class auto-serializable to XML."},{"id":"d","text":"It is a compiler hint to optimize field layout."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q26","text":"In LINQ, what is the difference between IQueryable<T> and IEnumerable<T>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IQueryable<T> executes queries in-memory; IEnumerable<T> queries are translated to SQL."},{"id":"b","text":"IEnumerable<T> executes queries in-memory using delegates; IQueryable<T> holds an expression tree that can be translated and executed on a remote data source (e.g., database)."},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical and interchangeable."},{"id":"d","text":"IQueryable<T> does not support deferred execution."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q27","text":"What does the Delegate.Combine() method do, and how does it relate to the += operator on delegates?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Delegate.Combine() removes a delegate from the invocation list; += adds one."},{"id":"b","text":"Delegate.Combine() merges two delegates into a new multicast delegate with both in its invocation list; the += operator on a delegate is syntactic sugar for Delegate.Combine()."},{"id":"c","text":"Delegate.Combine() compiles two delegates into one IL method."},{"id":"d","text":"Delegate.Combine() is only available for event types, not raw delegates."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q28","text":"What does the LINQ Take(n) operator do and what happens when n exceeds the sequence length?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Take(n) returns exactly n elements, throwing an exception if fewer exist."},{"id":"b","text":"Take(n) returns up to n elements from the start of the sequence; if the sequence has fewer than n elements, it returns all available elements without throwing."},{"id":"c","text":"Take(n) removes the first n elements from the sequence."},{"id":"d","text":"Take(n) returns the last n elements."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q29","text":"How does the using declaration (C# 8+) differ from the traditional using statement?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The using declaration wraps code in a try/finally block manually."},{"id":"b","text":"The using declaration (without braces) disposes the resource at the end of the enclosing scope rather than at the end of a block, reducing nesting."},{"id":"c","text":"The using declaration does not call Dispose(); it calls Close() instead."},{"id":"d","text":"The using declaration is only for async contexts."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a03_q30","text":"What is Span<T>.CopyTo() and how does it differ from Array.Copy()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Span<T>.CopyTo() requires the same type as the source; Array.Copy() allows type covariance."},{"id":"b","text":"Span<T>.CopyTo(destination) copies all elements to the destination Span<T>, working over any contiguous memory including stack-allocated memory; Array.Copy() is limited to managed arrays and performs bounds-checked copying."},{"id":"c","text":"They are functionally identical."},{"id":"d","text":"Array.Copy() is faster for all scenarios."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: csharp-advanced-04.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_a04', $${"id":"qp_cs_a04","title":"C# Advanced — Paper 4","description":"Advanced C# covering LINQ Advanced Operators, Lambda & Functional Patterns, Delegates, Events, Async/Await & Task Basics, Reflection, Attributes, Extension Methods, Expression Trees, and Dynamic Types.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":3600,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q1","text":"What does async/await do to a method in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It creates a new thread for each awaited operation."},{"id":"b","text":"It transforms the method into a state machine that suspends execution at each 'await' point, freeing the current thread while waiting, then resuming when the awaited task completes."},{"id":"c","text":"It makes the method execute synchronously on a background thread."},{"id":"d","text":"It blocks the calling thread until the operation completes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q2","text":"What is the purpose of ConfigureAwait(false) in async methods?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It disables the async/await pattern for a specific call."},{"id":"b","text":"It tells the awaiter not to capture the current synchronization context, allowing the continuation to run on any thread pool thread, which avoids deadlocks in library code and improves performance."},{"id":"c","text":"It ensures the continuation always runs on the UI thread."},{"id":"d","text":"It configures the task to not throw exceptions."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q3","text":"What is the difference between LINQ's SelectMany with a collection selector vs a result selector overload?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They produce identical output regardless of overload."},{"id":"b","text":"The collection selector overload flattens sub-sequences into one; the result selector overload additionally projects each (outer, inner) pair through a result function before returning."},{"id":"c","text":"The result selector overload only works with IQueryable<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"The collection selector overload returns a dictionary."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q4","text":"How is a pure function defined in functional programming, and why is it valuable in C# lambdas?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A pure function must be declared static."},{"id":"b","text":"A pure function always returns the same output for the same input and has no side effects, making lambdas that are pure predictable, testable, and safe to compose or parallelize."},{"id":"c","text":"A pure function cannot capture any variables."},{"id":"d","text":"A pure function runs in a sandboxed context with no access to the runtime."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q5","text":"What is delegate covariance in C# return type variance?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A delegate can accept a method returning a more derived type than declared in the delegate signature."},{"id":"b","text":"A delegate can be assigned a method whose return type is a derived class of the delegate's declared return type (covariant return type), since the returned value can always be used as the base type."},{"id":"c","text":"Delegates do not support covariance in any form."},{"id":"d","text":"Covariance applies only to generic delegates, not named delegates."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q6","text":"In C#, what is an event accessor (add/remove), and when would you implement custom accessors?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Event accessors are properties of the EventArgs class."},{"id":"b","text":"Custom add/remove accessors let you control subscription logic (e.g., using a dictionary of handlers, thread-safe locking, or filtering subscriptions) instead of the auto-generated backing delegate field."},{"id":"c","text":"add/remove accessors are required for all events in C#."},{"id":"d","text":"They are used to serialize event handlers to XML."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q7","text":"What does Task.WhenAll() do and how does it differ from awaiting tasks sequentially?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.WhenAll() runs tasks one at a time and awaits each."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.WhenAll() returns a task that completes when all supplied tasks complete, allowing all tasks to run concurrently rather than one-after-another, reducing total elapsed time."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.WhenAll() cancels all tasks if one fails."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.WhenAll() returns as soon as the first task completes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q8","text":"How do you handle exceptions from multiple tasks when using Task.WhenAll()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Each task's exception must be caught with an individual try/catch."},{"id":"b","text":"Awaiting Task.WhenAll() throws an AggregateException (unwrapped by await to the first inner exception); to inspect all exceptions, examine the Task.Exception.InnerExceptions collection on the awaited task."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.WhenAll() swallows all exceptions unless you use .Result."},{"id":"d","text":"You must pass a CancellationToken to capture exceptions from WhenAll."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q9","text":"What is the 'dynamic' keyword in C# and how does it differ from 'object'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"dynamic and object are identical; only the name differs."},{"id":"b","text":"dynamic bypasses static type checking and resolves member access at runtime via the DLR; object requires explicit casting and all member access is checked at compile time."},{"id":"c","text":"dynamic is only available in .NET Framework, not .NET Core."},{"id":"d","text":"dynamic forces boxing of all value types; object does not."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q10","text":"What are expression-bodied members in C# and how do they relate to expression trees?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression-bodied members create expression trees automatically."},{"id":"b","text":"Expression-bodied members use '=>' syntax to provide a single-expression body for methods/properties (compiled to IL). Expression trees (Expression<Func<T>>) represent code as data. They use similar syntax but serve different purposes."},{"id":"c","text":"They are the same concept and interchangeable."},{"id":"d","text":"Expression-bodied members are only for read-only properties."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q11","text":"What does the [StructLayout(LayoutKind.Sequential)] attribute control?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It makes a class behave like a struct."},{"id":"b","text":"It instructs the runtime to lay out struct or class fields in memory in declaration order, which is important for P/Invoke interop with native code."},{"id":"c","text":"It enables automatic struct packing to the smallest alignment."},{"id":"d","text":"It prevents the JIT from reordering struct fields."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q12","text":"What extension method pattern enables type-safe 'Is' and 'As' operations without reflection?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Using dynamic dispatch to test types at runtime."},{"id":"b","text":"Defining extension methods with generic constraints: public static bool Is<T>(this object o) => o is T; and public static T As<T>(this object o) where T : class => o as T;."},{"id":"c","text":"Using Activator.CreateInstance for type testing."},{"id":"d","text":"Extension methods cannot implement type-testing patterns."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q13","text":"How do you use an Expression<Func<T, bool>> as a predicate in Entity Framework LINQ queries?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"You must call .Compile() first to convert it to a delegate."},{"id":"b","text":"Pass it directly to .Where(), since IQueryable<T>.Where() accepts Expression<Func<T,bool>>, allowing the expression tree to be translated to SQL by the EF provider."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression predicates cannot be used with Entity Framework."},{"id":"d","text":"Call .AsEnumerable() first, then pass the compiled delegate."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q14","text":"What is ExpandoObject in C# and what interface does it implement?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ExpandoObject is a static dictionary class; it implements IDictionary."},{"id":"b","text":"ExpandoObject is a dynamic object that allows adding and removing members at runtime; it implements IDynamicMetaObjectProvider and IDictionary<string,object>."},{"id":"c","text":"ExpandoObject is a value type; it implements IStruct."},{"id":"d","text":"ExpandoObject only supports string-typed properties."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q15","text":"What does [CallerMemberName] attribute do when applied to an optional string parameter?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It captures the assembly name at compile time."},{"id":"b","text":"It causes the compiler to automatically supply the name of the calling member (method, property, etc.) as the argument, enabling logging or INotifyPropertyChanged without hard-coded string names."},{"id":"c","text":"It retrieves the member name via reflection at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"It restricts the method to being called only from class members."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q16","text":"When using LINQ's Zip with three sequences (C# 9+), what happens if sequences have different lengths?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It throws an ArgumentException if lengths differ."},{"id":"b","text":"Zip stops producing elements when the shortest sequence is exhausted; remaining elements of longer sequences are ignored."},{"id":"c","text":"It pads shorter sequences with default values."},{"id":"d","text":"Zip requires all sequences to have the same length."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q17","text":"What is the role of the Dynamic Language Runtime (DLR) in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The DLR replaces the CLR for interpreted languages."},{"id":"b","text":"The DLR is a runtime layer that provides dynamic dispatch, late binding, and expression tree evaluation, enabling the 'dynamic' keyword and hosting dynamic languages like IronPython on .NET."},{"id":"c","text":"The DLR compiles C# source code at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"The DLR handles only COM interop scenarios."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q18","text":"What does reflection's Type.GetGenericArguments() return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The generic constraints applied to the type parameters."},{"id":"b","text":"An array of Type objects representing the type arguments of a constructed generic type (e.g., for List<int>, it returns [typeof(int)])."},{"id":"c","text":"The names of the generic parameters as strings."},{"id":"d","text":"The base type of each generic argument."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q19","text":"What is lazy evaluation in LINQ and how can it lead to unexpected re-execution?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"LINQ queries are evaluated once and cached."},{"id":"b","text":"LINQ queries are deferred (lazy) and re-executed every time they are enumerated; if the source data changes between enumerations, different results are returned."},{"id":"c","text":"LINQ queries evaluate eagerly when any operator is appended."},{"id":"d","text":"Lazy evaluation only applies to GroupBy and OrderBy."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q20","text":"What is the Delegate.Remove() method used for?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It removes all delegates from a multicast invocation list."},{"id":"b","text":"It removes the last occurrence of a specified delegate from a multicast delegate's invocation list, returning a new delegate; the -= operator on events uses this."},{"id":"c","text":"It unregisters a delegate from the DLR."},{"id":"d","text":"It disposes the delegate and frees its memory."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q21","text":"What is function composition in C# functional programming?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Calling multiple functions in sequence inside a single method."},{"id":"b","text":"Creating a new function by combining two functions f and g such that compose(f,g)(x) = f(g(x)); each function's output becomes the next function's input."},{"id":"c","text":"Combining method groups into a single multicast delegate."},{"id":"d","text":"Using Aggregate to chain function calls over a collection."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q22","text":"What does Task.WhenAny() return and in what scenario is it most useful?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.WhenAny() returns a Task<Task> that completes when all tasks finish."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.WhenAny() returns a Task<Task> that completes as soon as any one of the supplied tasks completes; useful for timeouts, racing multiple operations, or processing results as they arrive."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.WhenAny() cancels remaining tasks after the first completes."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.WhenAny() throws if the first completing task has faulted."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q23","text":"What is an event memory leak in C# and which common pattern causes it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Event memory leaks are caused by raising events with no subscribers."},{"id":"b","text":"A long-lived publisher holds a reference to subscribers via its event delegate; if a short-lived subscriber subscribes but never unsubscribes, the publisher prevents GC of the subscriber, causing a memory leak."},{"id":"c","text":"Events leak memory when declared as static fields."},{"id":"d","text":"Event memory leaks occur only in .NET Framework, not .NET 6+."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q24","text":"Which overload of Expression.Constant() is used to create a typed constant node?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression.Constant(value) always infers the type."},{"id":"b","text":"Expression.Constant(value, type) lets you specify the type explicitly, which is important when the value is null or needs to be treated as an interface/base type rather than the concrete type."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression.Literal(value) is the correct method for constants."},{"id":"d","text":"Constants are always inlined; Expression.Constant is never needed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q25","text":"How do you add a new property to an ExpandoObject and read it back dynamically?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"expando.AddProperty(\"Name\", \"Alice\"); var name = expando.GetProperty(\"Name\");"},{"id":"b","text":"dynamic expando = new ExpandoObject(); expando.Name = \"Alice\"; string name = expando.Name;"},{"id":"c","text":"ExpandoObject.Declare(\"Name\"); expando[\"Name\"] = \"Alice\";"},{"id":"d","text":"ExpandoObject requires a schema to be defined before setting properties."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q26","text":"What is the purpose of [InternalsVisibleTo] attribute?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It makes all internal members public in the declaring assembly."},{"id":"b","text":"It grants a specified friend assembly access to the declaring assembly's internal types and members, commonly used to expose internals to unit test projects."},{"id":"c","text":"It controls which namespaces are visible within the assembly."},{"id":"d","text":"It merges two assemblies at link time."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q27","text":"What is the Liskov Substitution Principle's implication for delegate parameter variance (contravariance)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A delegate expecting a Base parameter cannot accept a method taking a Derived parameter."},{"id":"b","text":"A delegate declared as Action<Base> can reference a method that takes Action<Derived> is invalid; it's the reverse — Action<object> can be used where Action<string> is expected (contravariance), because a handler for any object can handle a string."},{"id":"c","text":"Contravariance means a derived type can be used where a base type is expected."},{"id":"d","text":"Delegate variance is not related to the Liskov Substitution Principle."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q28","text":"How can reflection be used to find all types in an assembly that implement a specific interface?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains(\"IMyInterface\"))"},{"id":"b","text":"Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => typeof(IMyInterface).IsAssignableFrom(t) && !t.IsInterface)"},{"id":"c","text":"Assembly.Scan(typeof(IMyInterface))"},{"id":"d","text":"You cannot scan an assembly for interface implementations via reflection."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q29","text":"What is a thread-safe event invocation pattern in C# to avoid the 'null check race condition'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Always invoke events inside a lock statement."},{"id":"b","text":"Copy the event delegate to a local variable before null-checking and invoking: var handler = MyEvent; handler?.Invoke(this, args); This avoids a race where the event becomes null between the check and invocation."},{"id":"c","text":"Use Interlocked.Exchange to null the event before invoking."},{"id":"d","text":"Events are inherently thread-safe; no special pattern is needed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a04_q30","text":"What is the difference between Func<T, TResult> and Expression<Func<T, TResult>>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are interchangeable; you can use either in any context."},{"id":"b","text":"Func<T,TResult> is a compiled, directly-invokable delegate; Expression<Func<T,TResult>> is a data structure representing the code as a tree that can be inspected, modified, or translated (e.g., to SQL) before optionally being compiled."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression<Func<T,TResult>> executes faster than Func<T,TResult>."},{"id":"d","text":"Func<T,TResult> can hold expression trees internally."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

a plain async method call?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.Run() and async method calls are identical in behavior."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.Run() offloads synchronous or CPU-bound work to the thread pool; an async method call begins execution synchronously on the current thread until it hits an await, then returns a Task."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.Run() creates a dedicated new thread each time."},{"id":"d","text":"Async methods cannot be called without Task.Run()."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q2","text":"What does Parallel.ForEach do differently from a regular foreach run."},{"id":"d","text":"Parallel.ForEach guarantees iteration order is preserved."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q3","text":"What is advanced pattern matching with a positional pattern in matching parts of the object by their positional components in a switch expression or is expression."},{"id":"c","text":"A pattern that uses index-based access like list[0] in a entirely to runtime via the DLR, operating on values typed as 'dynamic'."},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical; dynamic is just a shorthand for virtual."},{"id":"d","text":"Virtual dispatch is always slower than dynamic dispatch."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q12","text":"What does the 'and' pattern combinator do in C# 9+ switch expressions?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It matches if 100."},{"id":"c","text":"It is used to combine multiple switch arms."},{"id":"d","text":"It concatenates two string patterns."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q13","text":"How do you build in parallel code where each thread needs per-thread state (e.g., per-thread random number generator)."},{"id":"c","text":"ThreadLocal<T> is equivalent to a lock for thread synchronization."},{"id":"d","text":"ThreadLocal<T> stores values in the thread pool's shared queue."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q15","text":"What is the purpose of an extension method with record types."},{"id":"d","text":"Type patterns check at compile time; property patterns check at runtime."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q19","text":"What happens when you call async swallows all exceptions silently."},{"id":"d","text":"The compiler prevents throwing exceptions in async void methods."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q20","text":"How does an operations."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q24","text":"How does dynamic interop with COM objects work in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"COM objects must be wrapped in a managed proxy to identical IL; the only difference is calling syntax."},{"id":"b","text":"An extension method is called with instance syntax (obj.Method()) and participates in IntelliSense autocomplete on the type; a static helper requires explicit class qualification (Helper.Method(obj)) and does not appear in instance member lists."},{"id":"c","text":"Extension methods can access private members; static helpers cannot."},{"id":"d","text":"Static helpers can be inherited; extension methods cannot."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q29","text":"What is the Visitor pattern and how do expression trees embody it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Visitor pattern and expression trees are unrelated."},{"id":"b","text":"The Visitor pattern separates operations on a structure from the structure itself; expression trees embody this via ExpressionVisitor, which lets you walk and transform nodes of an expression tree without modifying the tree classes."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression trees use the Iterator pattern, not Visitor."},{"id":"d","text":"The Visitor pattern is automatically applied when compiling expression trees."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a05_q30","text":"What is the purpose of Parallel.For's localInit and localFinally parameters?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"localInit runs before the loop; localFinally runs after all iterations globally."},{"id":"b","text":"localInit initializes per-thread local state before that thread's first iteration; localFinally aggregates the per-thread result into a shared value after that thread completes its work, enabling thread-safe reduction without locks in the inner loop."},{"id":"c","text":"They configure the thread pool before and after the loop."},{"id":"d","text":"localFinally is called for each iteration, not per thread."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_a06', $${"id":"qp_cs_a06","title":"C# Advanced — Paper 6","description":"Advanced C# covering Delegates, Events, Async/Await, Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Extension Methods, Expression Trees, Dynamic Types, Advanced Pattern Matching, and Records/Tuples/Deconstruction.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":3600,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q1","text":"What is CancellationToken and how is it used to cancel an async operation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CancellationToken directly stops a thread when cancelled."},{"id":"b","text":"CancellationToken is a struct passed to async methods; the method periodically checks IsCancellationRequested or calls ThrowIfCancellationRequested(), and the caller cancels via CancellationTokenSource.Cancel()."},{"id":"c","text":"CancellationToken automatically disposes resources when cancelled."},{"id":"d","text":"CancellationToken is only used with Parallel.For, not async/await."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q2","text":"What is a record type in C# 9+ and what does it provide by default?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A record is a class with a private constructor."},{"id":"b","text":"A record is a reference type with compiler-generated value-based equality, immutable init-only properties, Deconstruct(), ToString(), and a 'with' expression for non-destructive mutation."},{"id":"c","text":"A record is a value type stored on the stack like a struct."},{"id":"d","text":"A record provides thread-safety for all its properties."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q3","text":"What does a multicast delegate's invocation return when all methods return values?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It returns a collection of all return values."},{"id":"b","text":"Only the return value of the last method in the invocation list is returned to the caller; all earlier return values are discarded."},{"id":"c","text":"It throws an exception because multicast delegates cannot have return values."},{"id":"d","text":"It returns the first non-null return value."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q4","text":"What are the differences between event and a public delegate field in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are identical; event is just an alias for a delegate field."},{"id":"b","text":"A public delegate field can be directly assigned (=), cleared (= null), and invoked from outside the class; an event restricts external code to only subscribe (+=) and unsubscribe (-=), preventing external invocation or replacement."},{"id":"c","text":"Events support multicast; delegate fields do not."},{"id":"d","text":"Delegate fields are thread-safe; events are not."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q5","text":"What is ValueTask<T> and when should it be preferred over Task<T>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ValueTask<T> is always faster and should replace Task<T> everywhere."},{"id":"b","text":"ValueTask<T> is a struct that avoids heap allocation when the result is already available synchronously (common in hot paths); use Task<T> for long-running operations that always complete asynchronously."},{"id":"c","text":"ValueTask<T> is used only for CPU-bound work."},{"id":"d","text":"ValueTask<T> does not support cancellation."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q6","text":"What is IAsyncEnumerable<T> and how is it consumed?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IAsyncEnumerable<T> is a synchronous collection that runs on a background thread."},{"id":"b","text":"IAsyncEnumerable<T> represents an async stream of values; it is consumed with 'await foreach', which awaits each item as it becomes available without blocking the thread between items."},{"id":"c","text":"IAsyncEnumerable<T> requires manual Task.WhenAll() to enumerate."},{"id":"d","text":"IAsyncEnumerable<T> materializes all values before enumeration begins."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q7","text":"What happens when you use AsParallel().WithDegreeOfParallelism(n) in PLINQ?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It creates exactly n dedicated threads."},{"id":"b","text":"It limits PLINQ to use at most n concurrent tasks from the thread pool, balancing parallelism with resource constraints."},{"id":"c","text":"It forces sequential execution with n as a batch size."},{"id":"d","text":"It sets the maximum number of elements to process."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q8","text":"What is the 'with' expression in C# records and how does it work?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It modifies the original record in place."},{"id":"b","text":"It creates a copy of a record with specified properties changed and all other properties preserved from the original, enabling non-destructive mutation of immutable records."},{"id":"c","text":"It is a using-like syntax for resource management in records."},{"id":"d","text":"It merges two records into one."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q9","text":"How do you implement an extension method that works on IQueryable<T> for a custom filter?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Extension methods cannot target IQueryable<T>."},{"id":"b","text":"Define a static method in a static class taking 'this IQueryable<T> source' and an Expression<Func<T,bool>> predicate, calling source.Where(predicate) and returning IQueryable<T> to preserve query composition."},{"id":"c","text":"Use a Func<T,bool> parameter instead of Expression<Func<T,bool>> for IQueryable."},{"id":"d","text":"Call .AsEnumerable() first, then apply the extension method."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q10","text":"What is an async stream (C# 8+) and how do you produce one?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An async stream is produced by returning Task<IEnumerable<T>>."},{"id":"b","text":"An async stream is produced by an async iterator method that returns IAsyncEnumerable<T> and uses 'yield return' with 'await' to asynchronously produce each value."},{"id":"c","text":"Async streams require a background Task that pushes values to a queue."},{"id":"d","text":"Async streams are produced by calling Observable.Create() in Rx."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q11","text":"What is a value tuple in C# and how does it differ from System.Tuple?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Value tuples and System.Tuple are identical."},{"id":"b","text":"Value tuples (System.ValueTuple) are value types (structs) with named elements, supporting deconstruction and more concise syntax; System.Tuple is a reference type with positional Item1/Item2 properties and no named element support."},{"id":"c","text":"Value tuples are allocated on the heap; System.Tuple is on the stack."},{"id":"d","text":"System.Tuple supports named elements; value tuples do not."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q12","text":"What is the ExpressionVisitor base class used for in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It is used to traverse the file system like a directory visitor."},{"id":"b","text":"ExpressionVisitor provides a base class for walking and optionally modifying expression trees; override specific Visit* methods to intercept and rewrite nodes of interest."},{"id":"c","text":"It is only useful for compiling expressions, not traversing them."},{"id":"d","text":"ExpressionVisitor executes expression trees without compiling."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q13","text":"What does the 'dynamic' keyword NOT give you compared to using 'object'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"dynamic gives no access to any properties."},{"id":"b","text":"dynamic bypasses compile-time type checking entirely; you lose IntelliSense, compiler errors for typos in member names, and static analysis, unlike 'object' which still enforces compile-time typing before casting."},{"id":"c","text":"dynamic is slower than 'object' in every situation."},{"id":"d","text":"dynamic cannot call methods; it only accesses fields."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q14","text":"What does deconstruction mean for value tuples in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It converts a tuple to a string representation."},{"id":"b","text":"Deconstruction unpacks a tuple (or any type with a Deconstruct method) into individual named variables: var (name, age) = person; assigns the components to name and age directly."},{"id":"c","text":"Deconstruction removes elements from a tuple permanently."},{"id":"d","text":"Deconstruction is only available for System.Tuple, not value tuples."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q15","text":"What is the 'relational' pattern in C# 9+ pattern matching?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A pattern that checks object identity via ReferenceEquals."},{"id":"b","text":"A pattern using comparison operators (< > <= >=) directly in a pattern: e.g., 'x is >= 0 and <= 100' matches when x is in the range [0, 100]."},{"id":"c","text":"A pattern that compares two variables for equality."},{"id":"d","text":"A pattern that matches database relation objects."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q16","text":"How does PLINQ handle ordering and what option preserves it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"PLINQ always preserves order automatically."},{"id":"b","text":"PLINQ does not preserve order by default for performance; call .AsOrdered() to request order preservation, though this introduces synchronization overhead."},{"id":"c","text":"PLINQ preserves order only when using OrderBy."},{"id":"d","text":"PLINQ order is controlled by thread scheduling and cannot be configured."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q17","text":"What is a record struct (C# 10+) and how does it differ from a record class?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Record struct and record class are identical."},{"id":"b","text":"A record struct is a value type (allocated on the stack) with compiler-generated value equality and Deconstruct(); a record class is a reference type. Record structs are mutable by default unless declared readonly."},{"id":"c","text":"Record structs cannot have 'with' expressions."},{"id":"d","text":"Record structs are sealed; record classes are not."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q18","text":"What is the purpose of CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It creates a new token that is independent of all parent tokens."},{"id":"b","text":"It creates a CancellationTokenSource whose token is cancelled when any of the provided tokens is cancelled, enabling composite cancellation from multiple sources."},{"id":"c","text":"It merges two tokens into a single token that requires both to be cancelled."},{"id":"d","text":"It clones a CancellationToken for use in a child task."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q19","text":"What is the difference between Task.Delay() and Thread.Sleep() in async contexts?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are interchangeable in async methods."},{"id":"b","text":"await Task.Delay() suspends the async method without blocking the thread, freeing it for other work; Thread.Sleep() blocks the thread for the duration, preventing it from handling other requests."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.Delay() is less precise than Thread.Sleep()."},{"id":"d","text":"Thread.Sleep() is preferred in async methods for simplicity."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q20","text":"How can you implement Deconstruct() on a custom class to enable tuple-style assignment?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Implement the IDeconstructable interface with a Deconstruct method."},{"id":"b","text":"Define a public void Deconstruct(out T1 a, out T2 b, ...) method on the class; the compiler recognizes it and allows: var (a, b) = myObject; syntax."},{"id":"c","text":"Mark the class with [Deconstruct] attribute."},{"id":"d","text":"Override the implicit conversion operator to ValueTuple."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q21","text":"How does event thread-safety work with the Interlocked class for event subscriptions?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Events are always thread-safe without any explicit synchronization."},{"id":"b","text":"Since delegates are immutable in .NET, subscribing/unsubscribing atomically replaces the backing delegate field; Interlocked.CompareExchange can be used in custom add/remove accessors to implement lock-free, thread-safe event management."},{"id":"c","text":"Interlocked cannot be used with delegates."},{"id":"d","text":"Thread safety for events requires a ReaderWriterLockSlim."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q22","text":"What does the 'switch expression' (C# 8+) enable that switch statements do not?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Switch expressions support fall-through between arms."},{"id":"b","text":"Switch expressions are value-producing: they return a value from each arm, support exhaustiveness checking, use '=>' for concise arms, and integrate pattern matching more cleanly than switch statements."},{"id":"c","text":"Switch expressions require all cases to be constant values."},{"id":"d","text":"Switch expressions automatically handle null values."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q23","text":"What is an anonymous type in C# and how is it related to tuples?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Anonymous types and tuples are identical in C#."},{"id":"b","text":"An anonymous type is a compiler-generated reference type created with 'new { Name = x, Age = y }' and is read-only; tuples are value types supporting deconstruction. Anonymous types are used in LINQ projections; tuples offer named access without a class definition."},{"id":"c","text":"Anonymous types support mutable properties; tuples are always immutable."},{"id":"d","text":"Anonymous types can be returned from public methods; tuples cannot."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q24","text":"How does expression tree manipulation enable runtime code generation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression trees can only be used for read-only code analysis."},{"id":"b","text":"You can build an expression tree programmatically at runtime using Expression.* factory methods, then call .Compile() to generate a delegate backed by IL code, effectively generating and executing code at runtime without Reflection.Emit."},{"id":"c","text":"Runtime code generation requires the Roslyn compiler API, not expression trees."},{"id":"d","text":"Expression tree compilation is only available in .NET Framework."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q25","text":"What is ExpandoObject's relationship to IDictionary<string,object>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ExpandoObject does not implement IDictionary."},{"id":"b","text":"ExpandoObject implements IDictionary<string,object>, so you can add/remove/iterate its dynamic properties via dictionary syntax: ((IDictionary<string,object>)expando)[\"Name\"] = \"Alice\"."},{"id":"c","text":"IDictionary<string,object> is only used for serialization of ExpandoObject."},{"id":"d","text":"Casting ExpandoObject to IDictionary throws an InvalidCastException."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q26","text":"What happens to a CancellationToken after CancellationTokenSource.Cancel() is called?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The token is disposed and cannot be used again."},{"id":"b","text":"IsCancellationRequested becomes true, registered callbacks are invoked synchronously, and any awaiting ThrowIfCancellationRequested() calls will throw OperationCanceledException."},{"id":"c","text":"Existing async operations are immediately terminated."},{"id":"d","text":"The CancellationToken is reset and reusable."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q27","text":"In PLINQ, what is the purpose of .ForAll() vs .ToList() when collecting results?","options":[{"id":"a","text":".ForAll() preserves order; .ToList() does not."},{"id":"b","text":".ForAll() processes results in parallel without merging into a single collection; .ToList() merges all parallel results into a List<T> sequentially, introducing a merge overhead. .ForAll() is preferred when order and collection aren't needed."},{"id":"c","text":".ForAll() is synchronous; .ToList() is asynchronous."},{"id":"d","text":"They are interchangeable with no performance difference."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q28","text":"What is record inheritance in C# 9+ and what constraint applies?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Records cannot participate in inheritance."},{"id":"b","text":"A record can inherit from another record (not from a class); the derived record extends the base record's properties and the compiler-generated equality considers all properties from both."},{"id":"c","text":"Records support multiple inheritance."},{"id":"d","text":"Record inheritance automatically makes the derived record sealed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q29","text":"What is the purpose of the Task.Yield() method in async programming?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.Yield() returns the current result to the caller immediately."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.Yield() creates an awaitable that asynchronously yields control back to the current context, forcing the remainder of the method to run as a continuation, which can prevent stack overflows in async recursion and improve responsiveness."},{"id":"c","text":"Task.Yield() is equivalent to Thread.Yield() for thread scheduling."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.Yield() produces the next value in an async stream."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a06_q30","text":"How do you use a fluent extension method to build a complex pipeline (e.g., query builder)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Each extension method must be in the same class as the type it extends."},{"id":"b","text":"Define extension methods on the builder type, each modifying and returning 'this' (or a new instance); callers chain them like: query.Where(...).OrderBy(...).Limit(10).Execute(), with each method adding to the query state."},{"id":"c","text":"Fluent pipelines require implementing the Builder interface."},{"id":"d","text":"Extension methods cannot be chained in a fluent pattern."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

async Task can."},{"id":"b","text":"async Task represents an async operation with no result; async Task<T> represents one that produces a result of type T when awaited."},{"id":"c","text":"async Task<T> always runs on a background thread; async Task does not."},{"id":"d","text":"They are identical and interchangeable."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q3","text":"What is the purpose of a ConcurrentBag<T> in parallel code?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It provides ordered concurrent access like a queue."},{"id":"b","text":"It is a thread-safe, unordered collection optimized for scenarios where the same thread both produces and consumes items (bag semantics), avoiding lock contention in parallel loops."},{"id":"c","text":"It is a thread-safe sorted set."},{"id":"d","text":"ConcurrentBag<T> guarantees FIFO order."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q4","text":"What does 'await using' do in C# 8+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It disposes the resource on a background thread."},{"id":"b","text":"It asynchronously disposes a resource that implements IAsyncDisposable by calling DisposeAsync() and awaiting it, preventing blocking during async cleanup."},{"id":"c","text":"It is syntactic sugar for await + using that only works with streams."},{"id":"d","text":"It combines await and a using statement for Task objects."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q5","text":"What does the ExpressionVisitor.Visit() method do when you do not override a specific Visit method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It throws NotSupportedException for unhandled node types."},{"id":"b","text":"It returns the node unchanged, effectively acting as an identity transformation for node types you have not overridden."},{"id":"c","text":"It removes the unhandled node from the tree."},{"id":"d","text":"It calls Compile() on the unhandled node."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q6","text":"What is the difference between dynamic and var in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Both defer type checking to runtime."},{"id":"b","text":"var is an implicitly-typed local variable resolved at compile time (still statically typed); dynamic defers all type checking to runtime via the DLR."},{"id":"c","text":"var allows runtime member resolution; dynamic does not."},{"id":"d","text":"They behave identically for reference types."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q7","text":"How does the 'switch expression' with pattern matching handle the default/discard case?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The 'default' keyword is used in switch expressions."},{"id":"b","text":"The discard pattern '_' acts as the catch-all arm in a switch expression, matching anything not handled by earlier arms."},{"id":"c","text":"Switch expressions do not support a default case."},{"id":"d","text":"Use 'else' as the catch-all arm in a switch expression."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q8","text":"What is a primary constructor in C# records and how does it relate to init-only properties?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A primary constructor is a regular constructor that runs initialization logic."},{"id":"b","text":"In records, the primary constructor parameters become init-only auto-implemented properties: 'record Person(string Name, int Age)' generates public string Name { get; init; } and public int Age { get; init; }."},{"id":"c","text":"Primary constructors create mutable properties by default."},{"id":"d","text":"Primary constructors require explicit property declarations."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q9","text":"What does AggregateException.Flatten() do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It converts an AggregateException into a single inner exception."},{"id":"b","text":"It returns a new AggregateException with all nested AggregateExceptions recursively unwrapped into a single flat list of inner exceptions."},{"id":"c","text":"It removes duplicate exceptions from the InnerExceptions list."},{"id":"d","text":"It serializes the AggregateException to a flat string."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q10","text":"What is a weak event pattern and which class helps implement it in .NET?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The weak event pattern uses Interlocked to manage subscriptions."},{"id":"b","text":"The weak event pattern holds only a WeakReference to subscribers so the publisher does not prevent GC; in .NET, WeakEventManager (WPF) or manual WeakReference<T> can implement this."},{"id":"c","text":"A weak event pattern stores event handlers in a HashSet."},{"id":"d","text":"WeakEvent is a built-in C# keyword for managing subscriptions."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q11","text":"What does Task.Run(async () => ...) return and how is it different from Task.Run(() => ...)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Both return Task<T> of the same type."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.Run(async () => ...) returns Task<Task> if you pass an async lambda returning Task; you must await it twice or use Task.Run(Func<Task>) which correctly unwraps to a single Task."},{"id":"c","text":"They behave identically because Task.Run automatically unwraps."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.Run(async () => ...) creates a dedicated thread."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q12","text":"What is the purpose of CancellationToken.Register()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It registers the token with the operating system scheduler."},{"id":"b","text":"It registers a callback to be invoked when cancellation is requested, enabling cleanup actions (like closing a socket) to run when the token is cancelled."},{"id":"c","text":"It adds the token to a global cancellation registry."},{"id":"d","text":"It links the token to a new CancellationTokenSource."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q13","text":"In C# generics, what is an open generic type vs a closed generic type?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An open generic type is public; a closed generic type is internal."},{"id":"b","text":"An open generic type has unbound type parameters (e.g., List<>); a closed generic type has all parameters specified (e.g., List<int>) and can be instantiated."},{"id":"c","text":"Open generics can be instantiated; closed generics cannot."},{"id":"d","text":"Closed generics are generic interfaces; open generics are classes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q14","text":"What are Parallel.For's return value (ParallelLoopResult) LowestBreakIteration and IsCompleted properties?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IsCompleted indicates individual task completion; IsCompleted is always true."},{"id":"d","text":"IsCompleted is always false when using ParallelLoopState."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q15","text":"How does record equality work when records have reference-type properties?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Records use reference equality for all properties."},{"id":"b","text":"Record equality uses structural (value) equality: all properties are compared using their own Equals() methods. For reference-type properties, their Equals() is called, which may be reference equality unless overridden."},{"id":"c","text":"Records only compare value-type properties; reference-type properties are ignored."},{"id":"d","text":"Record equality requires implementing IEquatable<T> manually."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q16","text":"What is the difference between try/catch/finally and try/catch when using 'when' filters?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Exception filters (when) cannot be used with finally blocks."},{"id":"b","text":"Exception filters (catch ... when (condition)) are evaluated before the stack unwinds, while catch without a filter unwinds the stack immediately; finally runs regardless of whether a catch is matched."},{"id":"c","text":"Exception filters replace finally blocks."},{"id":"d","text":"Try/catch/finally and try/catch with 'when' are identical."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q17","text":"How does dynamic interop with IronPython or other DLR languages work in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"C# cannot call code from DLR-hosted languages."},{"id":"b","text":"The DLR provides a hosting API (ScriptEngine, ScriptScope); C# can use 'dynamic' to call methods on DLR objects, and the DLR handles dispatch between the host (C#) and the dynamic language at runtime."},{"id":"c","text":"DLR languages must be compiled to IL before C# can call them."},{"id":"d","text":"Only COM interop is supported via 'dynamic'; DLR languages require P/Invoke."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q18","text":"What is a slice pattern in C# 11+ list patterns?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A slice pattern cuts a sequence at a specific index."},{"id":"b","text":"The '..' slice pattern within a list pattern matches zero or more elements in a sequence: '[first, .., last]' matches a sequence that starts with first and ends with last regardless of what is in between."},{"id":"c","text":"A slice pattern creates a memory slice of the matched sequence."},{"id":"d","text":"Slice patterns only work with Span<T> and Memory<T>."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q19","text":"What is async stream backpressure and how can it be managed with CancellationToken?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Async streams do not support backpressure."},{"id":"b","text":"Backpressure occurs when the consumer cannot keep up with the producer in an async stream; passing a CancellationToken to the async iterator and checking it allows the consumer to stop production, and using await foreach with EnumeratorCancellation enables cooperative cancellation."},{"id":"c","text":"Backpressure in async streams is handled automatically by the runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"CancellationToken cannot be used inside async iterators."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q20","text":"What is the purpose of Expression.Block() in building expression trees?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Expression.Block() groups expressions for parallel execution."},{"id":"b","text":"Expression.Block() creates a block expression that contains multiple sub-expressions executed sequentially, with the value of the block being the last expression's result."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression.Block() is used to wrap exception handling."},{"id":"d","text":"Expression.Block() creates a new variable scope."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q21","text":"What is the purpose of declaring a delegate type as 'delegate void MyHandler(object sender, EventArgs e)' rather than using a generic Func?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Named delegate types cannot be used with += and -=."},{"id":"b","text":"A named delegate type provides clear semantic intent, appears in IntelliSense with a meaningful name, and follows the .NET event design guideline; Func<object,EventArgs,void> is not valid (void cannot be a type argument)."},{"id":"c","text":"Named delegates are faster than Func<> at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"Named delegates are required for multicast; Func<> is not multicastable."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q22","text":"How do you deconstruct a record with additional non-primary-constructor properties in C# 9+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Only primary constructor properties can be deconstructed; extras are ignored."},{"id":"b","text":"The compiler generates Deconstruct() only for primary constructor parameters; you can add a custom Deconstruct() method overload to expose additional properties."},{"id":"c","text":"All record properties are automatically included in deconstruction."},{"id":"d","text":"Records do not support custom Deconstruct overloads."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q23","text":"What exception is thrown when you cancel an async operation via CancellationToken?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CancellationException"},{"id":"b","text":"OperationCanceledException (or its subclass TaskCanceledException when a Task is cancelled)"},{"id":"c","text":"TimeoutException"},{"id":"d","text":"ThreadAbortException"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q24","text":"What is the unmanaged constraint in C# generics and what types satisfy it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It allows T to be any type including managed references."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'unmanaged' constraint restricts T to unmanaged types: value types that contain no managed references (e.g., int, double, structs composed entirely of unmanaged types), enabling unsafe pointer operations and stackalloc with T."},{"id":"c","text":"Unmanaged constraint restricts to types marked with [Unmanaged] attribute."},{"id":"d","text":"It is equivalent to the 'struct' constraint."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q25","text":"What does Parallel.ForEach's ParallelLoopState.Break() do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It immediately terminates the loop and all already-running iterations."},{"id":"b","text":"It signals that no new iterations should start after the current one, but already-started iterations continue; the loop finishes after all iterations with index below the break point complete."},{"id":"c","text":"It is equivalent to 'return' in a sequential foreach."},{"id":"d","text":"Break() causes the loop to throw a BreakException."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q26","text":"What is the 'when' guard in a switch expression arm used for?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It defines an exception filter for errors inside the arm."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'when' guard adds a boolean condition to a switch arm; the arm is selected only if both the pattern matches and the guard condition is true: 'case SomeType x when x.Value > 0 => ...'."},{"id":"c","text":"It specifies when (time) the switch expression should execute."},{"id":"d","text":"The 'when' guard replaces the pattern in a switch arm."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q27","text":"How can ExceptionDispatchInfo be used to marshal exceptions across threads?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ExceptionDispatchInfo serializes exceptions for cross-thread transfer."},{"id":"b","text":"Capture the exception with ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture(ex) on the faulting thread; store the EDI; call edi.Throw() on the target thread to re-throw the exception with its original stack trace preserved."},{"id":"c","text":"ExceptionDispatchInfo automatically propagates exceptions between threads."},{"id":"d","text":"ExceptionDispatchInfo is only used for AggregateException unwrapping."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q28","text":"What happens if an async iterator method does not have any 'yield return' or 'yield break'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The compiler raises an error because IAsyncEnumerable<T> requires at least one yield."},{"id":"b","text":"The async iterator is valid; it returns an IAsyncEnumerable<T> that immediately completes with no elements when enumerated."},{"id":"c","text":"The method returns null instead of IAsyncEnumerable<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"The method runs indefinitely without completing."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q29","text":"What is the difference between an EventHandler and a custom delegate for events?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"EventHandler supports multicast; custom delegates do not."},{"id":"b","text":"EventHandler<TEventArgs> follows the standard (sender, args) pattern and integrates with .NET tooling; a custom delegate can have any signature but loses standardization and interoperability with frameworks expecting EventHandler."},{"id":"c","text":"Custom delegates cannot be used with the event keyword."},{"id":"d","text":"EventHandler cannot carry custom data; custom delegates can."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a07_q30","text":"What is an immutable record and what makes it 'immutable' in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A record is immutable if all its fields are private."},{"id":"b","text":"A record is considered immutable when its properties are declared with 'init' (or generated from primary constructor parameters), meaning they can only be set at construction time; the 'with' expression creates a new copy rather than mutating the original."},{"id":"c","text":"Records are always mutable because C# does not support true immutability."},{"id":"d","text":"A record becomes immutable only when sealed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
