INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_a08', $${"id":"qp_cs_a08","title":"C# Advanced — Paper 8","description":"Advanced C# covering Async/Await & Task Basics, Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Generic Variance, Dynamic Types, Advanced Pattern Matching, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, Advanced Exception Handling, and Memory Management.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":3600,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q1","text":"What is the difference between a sealed class and a sealed record in C# 9+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A sealed record cannot have properties; a sealed class can."},{"id":"b","text":"Both prevent inheritance; for records, sealing also prevents derivation of other records from it and means the compiler-generated equality and ToString are final."},{"id":"c","text":"A sealed record is always a value type."},{"id":"d","text":"Sealing a record disables the 'with' expression."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q2","text":"What is a deadlock in async code and what common mistake causes it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Deadlocks in async code are caused by using too many tasks."},{"id":"b","text":"A common deadlock is calling .Result or .Wait() on a Task inside a synchronization-context-aware environment (e.g., ASP.NET classic or WPF): the continuation needs the context to resume, but the context is blocked waiting for the task."},{"id":"c","text":"Deadlocks only occur in multi-threaded code with locks, not async code."},{"id":"d","text":"Using ConfigureAwait(false) always causes deadlocks."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q3","text":"What is the purpose of the Partitioner class in the Task Parallel Library?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Partitioner manages thread priority in parallel operations."},{"id":"b","text":"Partitioner divides a data source into chunks for parallel processing; custom partitioners allow fine-grained control over how data is distributed across threads in Parallel.For/ForEach and PLINQ."},{"id":"c","text":"Partitioner is used to partition namespaces in assemblies."},{"id":"d","text":"Partitioner provides a thread-safe queue for task distribution."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q4","text":"What does the [EnumeratorCancellation] attribute on a CancellationToken parameter in an async iterator enable?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It makes the iterator automatically retry on cancellation."},{"id":"b","text":"It allows the CancellationToken passed to GetAsyncEnumerator() (via WithCancellation()) to flow into the iterator method, so the iterator method's token parameter receives the consumer's cancellation token."},{"id":"c","text":"It converts the async iterator into a synchronous one."},{"id":"d","text":"It registers the iterator for automatic disposal."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q5","text":"How does a generic type parameter with multiple constraints work? E.g., 'where T : class, IComparable<T>, new()'","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Only one constraint can be active at a time; the others are ignored."},{"id":"b","text":"All constraints must be satisfied simultaneously: T must be a reference type, implement IComparable<T>, and have a public parameterless constructor."},{"id":"c","text":"Multiple constraints create an OR condition; T must satisfy any one."},{"id":"d","text":"The new() constraint is ignored when other constraints are present."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q6","text":"What is generic type variance and why can't it be applied to classes?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Variance applies to classes; interfaces and delegates do not support it."},{"id":"b","text":"Variance (covariant 'out' and contravariant 'in') can only be applied to generic type parameters of interfaces and delegates; classes and structs are invariant because they can be both read from and written to, which would break type safety."},{"id":"c","text":"Variance is not supported in C# at all."},{"id":"d","text":"Classes support covariance but not contravariance."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q7","text":"What is the purpose of using ExpandoObject over a Dictionary<string,object>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ExpandoObject is faster than Dictionary<string,object>."},{"id":"b","text":"ExpandoObject supports dynamic member access syntax (expando.Name) via the DLR, can have event handlers added dynamically, and integrates with the 'dynamic' keyword; Dictionary requires string-key indexer syntax."},{"id":"c","text":"ExpandoObject provides type safety; Dictionary does not."},{"id":"d","text":"ExpandoObject serializes to JSON automatically."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q8","text":"What is a 'var pattern' in C# pattern matching?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A var pattern matches only variables, not constants."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'var' pattern always matches any value (including null) and introduces a new variable of the inferred type: 'x is var y' always succeeds and assigns x to y."},{"id":"c","text":"A var pattern only works with value types."},{"id":"d","text":"A var pattern matches when the variable is uninitialized."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q9","text":"In C# 10+, what is a 'global using' directive?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A using directive that applies only to a single file."},{"id":"b","text":"A 'global using' directive applies to all files in the project automatically, avoiding repetition of common using statements across files."},{"id":"c","text":"A global using makes types available across all assemblies in a solution."},{"id":"d","text":"A global using directive is only valid for static classes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q10","text":"What is a 'try/catch/when' exception filter's key safety benefit over rethrowing?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Exception filters produce shorter code but have identical behavior to catch+rethrow."},{"id":"b","text":"An exception filter evaluates the condition before stack unwinding; if the filter is false, the runtime looks for the next handler without unwinding the stack, preserving the full original call stack for better diagnostics."},{"id":"c","text":"Exception filters allow swallowing exceptions silently."},{"id":"d","text":"They are only available in .NET 5+ and not in .NET Framework."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q11","text":"What is the large object heap (LOH) in .NET and when does an allocation go there?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"All reference types are allocated on the LOH."},{"id":"b","text":"Objects larger than approximately 85,000 bytes are allocated on the large object heap, which is collected less frequently and is not compacted by default, potentially causing fragmentation."},{"id":"c","text":"The LOH is used for all gen 2 objects."},{"id":"d","text":"The LOH only holds string objects."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q12","text":"What does Task.FromResult<T>(value) do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It creates a new Task<T> that runs on the thread pool."},{"id":"b","text":"It creates an already-completed Task<T> with the specified result, useful for returning a synchronous result from an async interface without creating a real async operation."},{"id":"c","text":"It schedules value to be returned when the task is awaited later."},{"id":"d","text":"It converts a synchronous method into an async method."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q13","text":"What is the DataflowBlock in System.Threading.Tasks.Dataflow and how does it relate to TPL?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DataflowBlock is the same as ConcurrentQueue<T>."},{"id":"b","text":"TPL Dataflow provides message-passing blocks (ActionBlock, TransformBlock, etc.) that can be linked into pipelines; each block processes messages concurrently with configurable parallelism, built on top of the TPL."},{"id":"c","text":"DataflowBlock replaces async/await for all pipeline processing."},{"id":"d","text":"DataflowBlock is only available in .NET Framework."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q14","text":"What does IAsyncDisposable provide over IDisposable?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IAsyncDisposable disposes resources on a background thread automatically."},{"id":"b","text":"IAsyncDisposable provides DisposeAsync() which returns a ValueTask, enabling cleanup of resources that require async operations (e.g., flushing a buffer) without blocking."},{"id":"c","text":"IAsyncDisposable is for COM interop only."},{"id":"d","text":"IAsyncDisposable replaces IDisposable in all .NET 6+ types."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q15","text":"What is a generic repository pattern and how does it use generic type constraints?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A generic repository uses Func<T> to define queries."},{"id":"b","text":"A generic repository IRepository<T> where T : class defines CRUD operations for any entity type T, using constraints to ensure T can be managed by the ORM, promoting reuse without duplicating code per entity."},{"id":"c","text":"Generic repositories require reflection to work."},{"id":"d","text":"A generic repository can only be used with database entities."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q16","text":"What is the purpose of IReadOnlyList<T> vs IList<T> from a variance perspective?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are identical; the 'ReadOnly' prefix is just a naming convention."},{"id":"b","text":"IReadOnlyList<T> is covariant (out T) because it only exposes reads; IList<T> is invariant because it supports both reads and writes, making covariance unsafe."},{"id":"c","text":"IReadOnlyList<T> is contravariant; IList<T> is covariant."},{"id":"d","text":"IReadOnlyList<T> does not support indexing; IList<T> does."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q17","text":"How does 'dynamic' interact with operator overloading in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"dynamic ignores operator overloading and uses default arithmetic."},{"id":"b","text":"When operands are 'dynamic', the DLR resolves operator overloads at runtime using the actual runtime types, so custom operators are respected even though the static type is 'dynamic'."},{"id":"c","text":"Operator overloading is disabled for dynamic types."},{"id":"d","text":"Dynamic operands always use boxing and generic arithmetic."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q18","text":"What is extended property patterns in C# 10+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Extended property patterns allow pattern matching on extension methods."},{"id":"b","text":"Extended property patterns allow nested property access in patterns using dot notation: '{ Address.City: \"Paris\" }' instead of requiring nested patterns '{ Address: { City: \"Paris\" } }'."},{"id":"c","text":"Extended property patterns match any number of properties simultaneously."},{"id":"d","text":"They are an extended syntax for initializing property values."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q19","text":"What is the 'init' accessor and how does it differ from 'set'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"init and set are interchangeable."},{"id":"b","text":"'init' allows a property to be set only during object initialization (constructor or object initializer) and not after; 'set' allows mutation at any time after construction."},{"id":"c","text":"'init' makes a property read-only from all contexts including the constructor."},{"id":"d","text":"'init' is only valid on record types."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q20","text":"What does AggregateException.Handle() do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Handle() re-throws all unhandled exceptions."},{"id":"b","text":"Handle() calls a predicate for each inner exception; if the predicate returns true, that exception is considered handled; if any inner exception returns false (unhandled), a new AggregateException containing only the unhandled ones is thrown."},{"id":"c","text":"Handle() logs all exceptions and suppresses them."},{"id":"d","text":"Handle() is equivalent to iterating InnerExceptions and calling catch."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q21","text":"What is a pinned object in .NET GC and when is pinning necessary?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Pinned objects are permanently promoted to gen 2."},{"id":"b","text":"Pinning prevents the GC from moving an object during collection (compaction), which is necessary when passing a managed object's address to unmanaged code via P/Invoke or unsafe pointer operations, ensuring the pointer remains valid."},{"id":"c","text":"Pinning is required for all value types passed to native code."},{"id":"d","text":"The GC never moves objects, so pinning is never needed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q22","text":"How does Task.Run differ from Task.Factory.StartNew in the context of async lambdas?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.Factory.StartNew correctly handles async lambdas; Task.Run does not."},{"id":"b","text":"Task.Run correctly unwraps async lambdas (Func<Task>) returning a Task that represents the async operation; Task.Factory.StartNew returns Task<Task> and requires explicit Unwrap() for async lambdas."},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical for async lambdas."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.Run does not support async lambdas at all."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q23","text":"What is the covariant return type feature in C# 9+ and how does it apply to class hierarchies?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Covariant return types allow overriding with a less derived return type."},{"id":"b","text":"C# 9+ allows overriding a virtual method with a more derived return type: a method returning Animal can be overridden to return Dog, as long as Dog derives from Animal."},{"id":"c","text":"Covariant return types apply only to generic methods."},{"id":"d","text":"Covariant return types are only available on interface methods."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q24","text":"What is MemoryPool<T> in .NET and how does it relate to Memory<T>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"MemoryPool<T> is a static utility class for allocating arrays."},{"id":"b","text":"MemoryPool<T> provides a reusable pool of Memory<T> buffers via Rent(size), returning an IMemoryOwner<T> that wraps a Memory<T>; returning the owner releases the buffer back to the pool, reducing allocations."},{"id":"c","text":"MemoryPool<T> is equivalent to ArrayPool<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"MemoryPool<T> allocates on the large object heap."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q25","text":"What does the 'checked' block do when applied to a floating-point operation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It causes an OverflowException on floating-point overflow."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'checked' keyword has no effect on floating-point arithmetic; it only affects integer arithmetic. Floating-point overflow produces Infinity or NaN, not an exception."},{"id":"c","text":"It converts floating-point results to integers before checking."},{"id":"d","text":"It enables IEEE 754 strict mode for floating-point."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q26","text":"What is an invariant generic type in C# and why is List<T> invariant?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"List<T> is covariant because it can be read from."},{"id":"b","text":"An invariant type does not support variance: List<string> is not assignable to List<object> because List<T> supports both Add(T) (input) and T this[int] (output); allowing covariance would let you add objects to a List<string>, breaking type safety."},{"id":"c","text":"List<T> is contravariant because it only supports Add()."},{"id":"d","text":"Invariant means the type parameter must be a value type."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q27","text":"What is the tuple element name feature in C# 7+ and how does it improve code?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Tuple element names are stored at runtime and accessed via reflection."},{"id":"b","text":"Named tuple elements allow referring to components by name instead of Item1/Item2: '(string Name, int Age) person = (\"Alice\", 30); person.Name' is clearer than 'person.Item1'; names are a compile-time feature encoded in attributes."},{"id":"c","text":"Named elements in tuples create new named types like anonymous types."},{"id":"d","text":"Tuple element names are only available for System.Tuple, not value tuples."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q28","text":"What is the purpose of GC.AddMemoryPressure() in .NET?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It manually triggers a garbage collection."},{"id":"b","text":"It informs the GC of significant unmanaged memory allocations so the GC can adjust its collection frequency, preventing situations where managed memory is low but unmanaged memory is actually exhausted."},{"id":"c","text":"It increases the managed heap size limit."},{"id":"d","text":"It pins objects in memory to prevent GC movement."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q29","text":"How can async streams be used with LINQ-like operators?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Standard LINQ operators work directly on IAsyncEnumerable<T>."},{"id":"b","text":"The System.Linq.Async NuGet package (or similar) provides LINQ operators adapted for IAsyncEnumerable<T> (e.g., WhereAwait, SelectAwait, ToListAsync), enabling LINQ-style composition over async streams."},{"id":"c","text":"You must materialize async streams to a List<T> before using LINQ."},{"id":"d","text":"Async streams cannot be composed; each must be enumerated manually."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a08_q30","text":"What is a recursive pattern in C# 8+ and how does it combine other patterns?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A recursive pattern is a pattern that matches recursively nested objects of the same type."},{"id":"b","text":"A recursive pattern (positional or property) can nest other patterns within it: e.g., '{ Address: { City: { Length: > 3 } } }' nests property patterns; or a positional pattern can contain other positional/property patterns."},{"id":"c","text":"Recursive patterns only work with records."},{"id":"d","text":"Recursive patterns require the object to implement IRecursive."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: csharp-advanced-09.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_a09', $${"id":"qp_cs_a09","title":"C# Advanced — Paper 9","description":"Advanced C# covering Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Generic Variance, LINQ Core Operators, Advanced Pattern Matching, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, Advanced Exception Handling, Memory Management, and Span<T>/Memory<T>.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":3600,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q1","text":"What is a generic factory method pattern and how does it use constraints?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Generic factories require reflection to create instances."},{"id":"b","text":"A generic factory method T Create<T>() where T : class, new() uses the 'new()' constraint to call the parameterless constructor via 'new T()', creating instances of any type that satisfies the constraint."},{"id":"c","text":"Factories with generics can only return interfaces."},{"id":"d","text":"Generic factories use Activator.CreateInstance() because 'new T()' is not possible."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q2","text":"What is the IComparer<T> covariance/contravariance relationship in .NET?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IComparer<T> is covariant (out T)."},{"id":"b","text":"IComparer<T> is contravariant (in T): an IComparer<Animal> can be used where IComparer<Dog> is expected, because a comparer that works for any Animal can certainly compare Dogs."},{"id":"c","text":"IComparer<T> is invariant."},{"id":"d","text":"IComparer<T> is both covariant and contravariant."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q3","text":"What does LINQ's SkipWhile and TakeWhile do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SkipWhile removes matching elements; TakeWhile keeps all elements."},{"id":"b","text":"TakeWhile returns elements as long as a condition is true, stopping at the first false; SkipWhile skips elements while a condition is true, then returns all remaining elements."},{"id":"c","text":"They both filter elements globally based on a predicate."},{"id":"d","text":"SkipWhile and TakeWhile operate only on ordered sequences."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q4","text":"What is the purpose of LINQ's Lookup<TKey, TElement> vs Dictionary<TKey, List<TElement>>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Lookup<TKey,TElement> is mutable; Dictionary is immutable."},{"id":"b","text":"Lookup<TKey,TElement> is an immutable one-to-many mapping returned by ToLookup(); it returns an empty sequence (not throws) for missing keys, unlike a Dictionary which throws KeyNotFoundException."},{"id":"c","text":"Lookup supports more key types than Dictionary."},{"id":"d","text":"Lookup is only available in LINQ to SQL."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q5","text":"How does the 'with' expression work with record structs (C# 10+)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Record structs do not support the 'with' expression."},{"id":"b","text":"'with' on a record struct creates a copy of the struct with the specified properties replaced, exactly like record classes; since structs are value types, the copy is on the stack."},{"id":"c","text":"'with' on a record struct modifies the original struct in place."},{"id":"d","text":"'with' creates a boxed copy of the record struct on the heap."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q6","text":"What is an OperationCanceledException and when should you catch it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It should always be caught and ignored in library code."},{"id":"b","text":"OperationCanceledException signals that an operation was intentionally cancelled via CancellationToken; library code should usually let it propagate; application code may catch it to handle cancellation gracefully (e.g., show a message or take alternative action)."},{"id":"c","text":"OperationCanceledException indicates a timeout, not user cancellation."},{"id":"d","text":"It should always be caught and re-thrown as AggregateException."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q7","text":"What does Span<T>.Fill(value) do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Fill() appends value to the end of the span."},{"id":"b","text":"Fill() sets all elements of the span to the specified value, providing an efficient way to initialize or reset a block of memory."},{"id":"c","text":"Fill() copies another span into this span."},{"id":"d","text":"Fill() is only available for Span<byte>."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q8","text":"What is the thread pool in .NET and how does Task.Run() use it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Task.Run() creates a new OS thread each time."},{"id":"b","text":"The thread pool is a managed pool of reusable threads; Task.Run() queues work to the thread pool, reusing existing threads rather than creating new ones, reducing the overhead of thread creation and destruction."},{"id":"c","text":"The thread pool is only used for I/O-bound operations."},{"id":"d","text":"Task.Run() always uses a dedicated thread, bypassing the pool."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q9","text":"What advanced pattern matching feature allows matching on nested tuple structures in C# 8+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Tuple matching uses a special 'match' keyword."},{"id":"b","text":"Positional patterns can match tuples by structure: '(int x, int y) point = ...; switch(point) { case (0, 0): ...; case (_, 0): ...; }' matches on the deconstructed components."},{"id":"c","text":"Tuples require implementing IMatchable to use in patterns."},{"id":"d","text":"You must deconstruct a tuple manually before pattern matching."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q10","text":"What is the purpose of the 'finally' block when an exception is unhandled?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A finally block only runs when no exception is thrown."},{"id":"b","text":"The finally block runs regardless of whether an exception is caught or unhandled, allowing cleanup code (like closing streams) to execute even if the exception propagates uncaught up the call stack."},{"id":"c","text":"A finally block swallows unhandled exceptions."},{"id":"d","text":"Finally blocks run only when the exception is caught in the same try/catch."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q11","text":"What is memory fragmentation in .NET and how does the LOH contribute to it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The GC always compacts the LOH, so fragmentation is not an issue."},{"id":"b","text":"The large object heap is not compacted by default (unless forced); when large objects are freed and smaller objects are allocated into the gaps, unused pockets of memory remain, causing fragmentation that increases memory consumption."},{"id":"c","text":"Fragmentation only occurs in the small object heap."},{"id":"d","text":"Memory fragmentation only affects unmanaged memory."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q12","text":"What is the difference between stackalloc int[n] and new int[n] in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are equivalent; stackalloc is just a shorter syntax."},{"id":"b","text":"stackalloc int[n] allocates on the stack (no GC pressure, automatically freed on method return, usable with Span<T>); new int[n] allocates on the managed heap (GC-managed, survives method return, accessible as a field or parameter)."},{"id":"c","text":"new int[n] is always faster than stackalloc int[n]."},{"id":"d","text":"stackalloc int[n] requires the unsafe keyword in all cases."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q13","text":"What is the variance issue with arrays in C# (array covariance)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Arrays are invariant and cannot be assigned to base-type arrays."},{"id":"b","text":"C# allows string[] to be assigned to object[] (array covariance is built into the runtime), but writing a non-string to the array throws ArrayTypeMismatchException at runtime, making array covariance unsound."},{"id":"c","text":"Array covariance is fully type-safe in C#."},{"id":"d","text":"Array covariance only applies to value-type arrays."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q14","text":"What is the Enumerable.Range() LINQ method and what does it produce?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Range() produces a random sequence of numbers."},{"id":"b","text":"Enumerable.Range(start, count) produces a sequence of 'count' integers starting from 'start', using deferred execution; useful for generating test data or index sequences."},{"id":"c","text":"Range() returns an IList<int> with all values materialized immediately."},{"id":"d","text":"Range() produces a sequence from start to end inclusive."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q15","text":"What is a CancellationToken.None and when should it be used?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CancellationToken.None throws if passed to an async method."},{"id":"b","text":"CancellationToken.None is a default/empty token that is never cancelled; pass it when you want to satisfy an API that requires a CancellationToken parameter but you have no cancellation intent."},{"id":"c","text":"CancellationToken.None is equivalent to default(CancellationToken)."},{"id":"d","text":"Both b and c are correct."}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q16","text":"What does Parallel.Invoke() do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Parallel.Invoke() schedules actions sequentially on the thread pool."},{"id":"b","text":"Parallel.Invoke() executes an array of Action delegates potentially in parallel and waits for all of them to complete, simplifying parallelizing a fixed set of independent tasks."},{"id":"c","text":"Parallel.Invoke() is equivalent to Task.WhenAll() for synchronous actions."},{"id":"d","text":"Parallel.Invoke() is for fire-and-forget; it does not wait for completion."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q17","text":"What is the purpose of the 'discard' pattern '_' in C# pattern matching?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The discard '_' is invalid in patterns; it is only for out parameter discards."},{"id":"b","text":"The discard pattern '_' matches any value without binding it to a variable, used as a wildcard in switch arms or positional patterns when you do not need the matched value."},{"id":"c","text":"The discard pattern matches only null values."},{"id":"d","text":"The discard pattern '_' matches only the default enum value."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q18","text":"How does C# 12's primary constructors (non-record) work?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Primary constructors in non-record classes generate init-only properties."},{"id":"b","text":"In C# 12, classes and structs can have a primary constructor (parameters in the class declaration); the parameters are scoped to the class body but do NOT automatically become properties — you must declare properties manually or use them in initializers."},{"id":"c","text":"Primary constructors on regular classes behave identically to records."},{"id":"d","text":"Primary constructors are only for struct types in C# 12."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q19","text":"What is a re-throw vs throw inside a catch block in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Both 'throw ex' and 'throw' rethrow with the same stack trace."},{"id":"b","text":"'throw' (without argument) rethrows the current exception preserving its original stack trace; 'throw ex' rethrows a specific exception but resets the stack trace to the current location, losing the original call stack."},{"id":"c","text":"'throw ex' is always preferred over bare 'throw'."},{"id":"d","text":"Both 'throw' and 'throw ex' clear the stack trace."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q20","text":"What is ReadOnlySpan<T> and how does it differ from Span<T>?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ReadOnlySpan<T> can be stored on the heap; Span<T> cannot."},{"id":"b","text":"ReadOnlySpan<T> is a ref struct like Span<T> but provides read-only access to a memory region; you cannot modify elements through it, making it suitable for reading string data or immutable buffers."},{"id":"c","text":"ReadOnlySpan<T> supports async methods; Span<T> does not."},{"id":"d","text":"ReadOnlySpan<T> allocates a copy of the data."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q21","text":"In PLINQ, what is the difference between AsParallel() and AsSequential()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"AsParallel() makes LINQ sequential; AsSequential() makes it parallel."},{"id":"b","text":"AsParallel() marks a query for parallel execution using multiple threads; AsSequential() can be inserted mid-pipeline to force subsequent operators to run sequentially, useful when an operator is not thread-safe."},{"id":"c","text":"AsSequential() materializes the query immediately."},{"id":"d","text":"They are interchangeable; order of execution is up to the scheduler."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q22","text":"What is the purpose of the 'base' record member specification in C# 9+ record inheritance?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It calls the base class constructor at compile time."},{"id":"b","text":"In derived record declarations, the positional parameters include base parameters passed to the base record's primary constructor: 'record Dog(string Name, string Breed) : Animal(Name);' passes Name to Animal."},{"id":"c","text":"'base' in a record is used to access overridden properties."},{"id":"d","text":"Derived records cannot pass parameters to the base record."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q23","text":"How does Func<T> covariance work in C# and what is an example?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Func<T> is contravariant; it cannot be assigned to Func<base>."},{"id":"b","text":"Func<TResult> is covariant in TResult: a Func<string> can be assigned to Func<object> because string is an object. This works because Func only produces (returns) a value."},{"id":"c","text":"Func<T> is invariant because it can both accept and return T."},{"id":"d","text":"Func<T> covariance requires explicit casting."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q24","text":"What is the Span<T>.SequenceEqual() method used for?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It checks if two spans point to the same memory location."},{"id":"b","text":"SequenceEqual() compares two Span<T> instances element-by-element and returns true if they have the same length and all corresponding elements are equal."},{"id":"c","text":"SequenceEqual() is only available for Span<byte>."},{"id":"d","text":"SequenceEqual() checks if the spans are both empty."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q25","text":"What is an async method state machine and what does the compiler generate?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The compiler generates a new Thread class for each async method."},{"id":"b","text":"The compiler transforms an async method into a struct-based state machine with MoveNext() and fields storing local variables and the current state; each 'await' is a state transition point."},{"id":"c","text":"The state machine runs on a dedicated thread pool thread."},{"id":"d","text":"The compiler only transforms async methods that have more than one await."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q26","text":"What does LINQ's Contains() method do and what does it use for equality?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Contains() always uses reference equality."},{"id":"b","text":"Contains() returns true if any element in the sequence equals the specified value, using the default equality comparer (EqualityComparer<T>.Default) or a provided IEqualityComparer<T>."},{"id":"c","text":"Contains() only works on IList<T>, not IEnumerable<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"Contains() counts occurrences rather than checking for membership."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q27","text":"What is the Safe Handle pattern in .NET and why is it preferred over raw IntPtr?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SafeHandle is only used for file handles."},{"id":"b","text":"SafeHandle (and derived classes like SafeFileHandle) wraps a native handle with proper finalization, ensures exactly-once release, handles edge cases like handle recycling attacks, and participates in the IDisposable pattern correctly, unlike raw IntPtr."},{"id":"c","text":"SafeHandle boxes the IntPtr for GC tracking."},{"id":"d","text":"SafeHandle prevents the handle from being passed to native code."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q28","text":"What is value tuple projection in LINQ (C# 7+)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Value tuples cannot be used in LINQ projections."},{"id":"b","text":"You can project LINQ results to value tuples: 'source.Select(x => (x.Name, x.Age))' produces IEnumerable<(string Name, int Age)>, giving named access to projected components without defining an anonymous type or separate class."},{"id":"c","text":"Value tuple projections require calling ToList() first."},{"id":"d","text":"Value tuples in LINQ projections only work with query syntax."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q29","text":"How do you properly implement the async-safe Dispose pattern with both IDisposable and IAsyncDisposable?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Implement only IAsyncDisposable; IDisposable is obsolete in async code."},{"id":"b","text":"Implement both interfaces; in Dispose() provide synchronous cleanup (calling DisposeAsync().GetAwaiter().GetResult() or equivalent); in DisposeAsync() provide async cleanup; both should be idempotent with a disposed flag."},{"id":"c","text":"DisposeAsync() automatically calls Dispose() as a fallback."},{"id":"d","text":"Only one can be implemented per class; choose based on whether the resource is managed or unmanaged."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a09_q30","text":"What is C# 11's required modifier on properties and how does it work?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"required makes a property non-nullable."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'required' modifier forces callers to set a property in the object initializer; the compiler raises an error if a required property is not initialized, even when using object initializers without a parameterized constructor."},{"id":"c","text":"required makes the property a primary constructor parameter."},{"id":"d","text":"required prevents a property from having a default value."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_a10', $${"id":"qp_cs_a10","title":"C# Advanced — Paper 10","description":"Advanced C# covering Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Generic Variance, LINQ Core & Advanced Operators, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, Advanced Exception Handling, Memory Management, Span<T>/Memory<T>, and Reflection.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":3600,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q1","text":"What is the difference between a positional record and a nominal record in C# 9+?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Positional records are value types; nominal records are reference types."},{"id":"b","text":"A positional record uses a primary constructor to declare properties concisely: 'record Point(int X, int Y)'; a nominal record uses standard property declarations with init accessors. Both are functionally equivalent but positional syntax is more concise."},{"id":"c","text":"Nominal records support inheritance; positional records do not."},{"id":"d","text":"Positional records are immutable; nominal records are always mutable."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q2","text":"What is the purpose of the CancellationTokenSource.CancelAfter() method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It cancels all registered tokens after the source is disposed."},{"id":"b","text":"CancelAfter() schedules automatic cancellation after a specified delay (TimeSpan or milliseconds), enabling timeout-based cancellation without manually creating a timer."},{"id":"c","text":"CancelAfter() is equivalent to Thread.Sleep() followed by Cancel()."},{"id":"d","text":"CancelAfter() cancels any downstream linked tokens only."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q3","text":"What is the 'notnull' generic constraint in C# 8+ and what does it enforce?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"notnull constrains T to value types only."},{"id":"b","text":"The 'notnull' constraint ensures T is a non-nullable type (either a non-nullable reference type or a value type); with nullable reference types enabled, it prevents nullable reference types from being used as T."},{"id":"c","text":"notnull automatically adds null checks to all method calls on T."},{"id":"d","text":"notnull is equivalent to the 'struct' constraint."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q4","text":"What is the Func<in T, out TResult> contravariant input and covariant output in practice?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Func<T,TResult> is invariant in both T and TResult."},{"id":"b","text":"Func<T,TResult> is contravariant in T (in) and covariant in TResult (out): Func<object,string> can be assigned to Func<string,object> because it accepts any object (handles strings) and returns a string (which is an object)."},{"id":"c","text":"Func<T,TResult> is covariant in T and contravariant in TResult."},{"id":"d","text":"Variance does not apply to Func<T,TResult>."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q5","text":"What is LINQ's Except() operator and what does it return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Except() returns elements present in both sequences."},{"id":"b","text":"Except() returns elements from the first sequence that are not present in the second sequence (set difference), using the default equality comparer or a provided one."},{"id":"c","text":"Except() excludes the first N elements of a sequence."},{"id":"d","text":"Except() removes duplicates from a single sequence."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q6","text":"What does LINQ's Intersect() operator do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Intersect() returns elements that are in the first sequence but not the second."},{"id":"b","text":"Intersect() returns the set intersection: elements that appear in both sequences, using the default equality comparer or a provided one."},{"id":"c","text":"Intersect() concatenates two sequences and removes duplicates."},{"id":"d","text":"Intersect() returns the union of both sequences."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q7","text":"How does C# 12 collection expressions work and how do they relate to existing collection supports it (arrays, List<T>, Span<T>, ImmutableArray<T>, etc.); the compiler picks the optimal initialization based on the target type."},{"id":"c","text":"Collection expressions always create a new List<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"Collection expressions require the CollectionBuilder attribute."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q8","text":"What is the checked/unchecked context for arithmetic in C# and what is the default?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The default context is checked; overflow throws OverflowException by default."},{"id":"b","text":"The default arithmetic context is unchecked; integer overflow silently wraps. The 'checked' keyword/block enables overflow detection, throwing OverflowException on integer overflow."},{"id":"c","text":"Checked is the default for floating-point; unchecked for integers."},{"id":"d","text":"Both checked and unchecked are compile-time only; there is no runtime difference."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q9","text":"What is GC.Collect() used for and when is it appropriate to call it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"GC.Collect() should be called frequently to optimize memory usage."},{"id":"b","text":"GC.Collect() triggers an explicit garbage collection; it should rarely be called in production code as it interrupts normal GC scheduling. Legitimate uses include after a known large allocation release or in test/diagnostic scenarios."},{"id":"c","text":"GC.Collect() compacts the managed heap."},{"id":"d","text":"GC.Collect() is required to free objects that implement IDisposable."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q10","text":"What is the purpose of ArrayPool<T>.Shared in .NET?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ArrayPool<T>.Shared provides a single shared array of type T."},{"id":"b","text":"ArrayPool<T>.Shared is a shared pool of reusable arrays; you Rent(size) to get an array (may be larger than requested) and Return() it when done, dramatically reducing GC pressure for short-lived large arrays."},{"id":"c","text":"ArrayPool<T>.Shared is only safe to use on a single thread."},{"id":"d","text":"ArrayPool<T>.Shared always returns zero-initialized arrays."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q11","text":"What is the difference between Memory<T>.Span and Memory<T> itself?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Memory<T>.Span is a different type stored on the heap."},{"id":"b","text":"Memory<T>.Span returns a Span<T> over the same memory region; Span<T> is a ref struct (stack-only) while Memory<T> is a regular struct that can be stored as a field, passed to async methods, and used across await points."},{"id":"c","text":"Memory<T>.Span copies the data to a new stack allocation."},{"id":"d","text":"They are the same type; .Span is just an alias."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q12","text":"What is reflection emit and how does it differ from expression tree compilation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They produce identical IL code and are interchangeable."},{"id":"b","text":"Reflection.Emit (System.Reflection.Emit) allows generating full IL byte code for dynamic types, methods, and assemblies at runtime with fine-grained control; expression tree compilation is higher-level, limited to expressions (not arbitrary statements), but easier to use."},{"id":"c","text":"Expression trees are lower-level than Reflection.Emit."},{"id":"d","text":"Reflection.Emit is deprecated; expression trees replace it entirely."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q13","text":"What is a generic interface default implementation in C# 8+ and what restrictions apply?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Default interface methods are only allowed on abstract interfaces."},{"id":"b","text":"C# 8+ allows interface methods to have a default implementation body; classes implementing the interface inherit the default if not overridden. Restrictions: default implementations require the CLR >=.NET 5 (or compatible), and they are accessed via the interface type, not the class type."},{"id":"c","text":"Default interface implementations replace abstract classes entirely."},{"id":"d","text":"Default interface methods can be sealed by the implementing class."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q14","text":"How does reflection handle generic methods and how do you invoke a generic method with a specific type argument?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Generic methods cannot be invoked via reflection."},{"id":"b","text":"Get the MethodInfo for the open generic method, then call MakeGenericMethod(typeof(T)) to get a closed MethodInfo, then call .Invoke(instance, args) with the appropriate arguments."},{"id":"c","text":"You must specify generic arguments in GetMethod() directly."},{"id":"d","text":"Generic methods are invoked without specifying type arguments; the runtime infers them."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q15","text":"What is the IAsyncEnumerable<T> cancellation pattern with WithCancellation()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"WithCancellation() is called on IAsyncEnumerable<T> to replace the source."},{"id":"b","text":"Calling asyncEnumerable.WithCancellation(token) returns a configured IAsyncEnumerable<T> that passes the token to the underlying GetAsyncEnumerator(token), enabling the producer to observe the token and stop early."},{"id":"c","text":"WithCancellation() converts an IAsyncEnumerable into a Task<List<T>>."},{"id":"d","text":"WithCancellation() automatically cancels after a fixed timeout."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q16","text":"What is a covariant return type on a record clone method ('protected virtual T Clone()') in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Records do not have a Clone method."},{"id":"b","text":"The compiler generates a 'protected virtual T Clone()' method on records used for 'with' expressions; in derived records, this is overridden with a covariant return type returning the derived record type, ensuring 'with' produces the correct derived type."},{"id":"c","text":"Clone() on records is a public static method."},{"id":"d","text":"The Clone method uses MemberwiseClone() and returns object."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q17","text":"What is the LINQ Union() operator and how does it differ from Concat()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Union() and Concat() return identical results."},{"id":"b","text":"Union() returns distinct elements from both sequences (set union, no duplicates); Concat() appends the second sequence to the first without removing duplicates."},{"id":"c","text":"Concat() removes duplicates; Union() keeps all duplicates."},{"id":"d","text":"Union() requires both sequences to be of the same concrete type."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q18","text":"What is the difference between Marshal.AllocHGlobal() and stackalloc in unsafe C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are interchangeable; both allocate unmanaged memory."},{"id":"b","text":"Marshal.AllocHGlobal() allocates unmanaged heap memory that persists until Marshal.FreeHGlobal() is called; stackalloc allocates on the stack and is automatically freed when the method exits. Stack allocations are faster but size-limited."},{"id":"c","text":"stackalloc allocates on the heap; Marshal.AllocHGlobal() allocates on the stack."},{"id":"d","text":"Marshal.AllocHGlobal() is managed by the GC; stackalloc is not."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q19","text":"What is a ValueTask<T> backed by IValueTaskSource<T> and when is it used?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IValueTaskSource<T> is only used in the .NET runtime itself, not in user code."},{"id":"b","text":"IValueTaskSource<T> allows pooling the ValueTask<T>'s backing state (instead of allocating a new Task<T> each time), enabling zero-allocation async patterns for high-performance scenarios like I/O on hot paths."},{"id":"c","text":"IValueTaskSource<T> is equivalent to TaskCompletionSource<T>."},{"id":"d","text":"ValueTask<T> backed by IValueTaskSource<T> cannot be awaited multiple times."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q20","text":"What is the purpose of MemoryMarshal.Cast<TFrom, TTo>() in .NET?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"MemoryMarshal.Cast reinterprets the type of elements in a span."},{"id":"b","text":"MemoryMarshal.Cast<TFrom,TTo>(Span<TFrom>) reinterprets the raw bytes of the span as a different element type without copying, e.g., treating a Span<byte> as Span<int> to read integer data from a byte buffer."},{"id":"c","text":"MemoryMarshal.Cast converts managed memory to unmanaged memory."},{"id":"d","text":"Both a and b are correct."}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q21","text":"What is a generic variance constraint combining 'in' and 'out' on different type parameters?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A single interface cannot have both in and out type parameters."},{"id":"b","text":"An interface can have both: e.g., interface IConverter<in TInput, out TOutput> { TOutput Convert(TInput input); } TInput is contravariant (input only), TOutput is covariant (output only), so IConverter<Animal,string> can be used as IConverter<Dog,object>."},{"id":"c","text":"Combining in and out cancels both, making the parameters invariant."},{"id":"d","text":"Both in and out make the type parameter bidirectional."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q22","text":"What is a LINQ query continuation using 'into' in query syntax?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"'into' in LINQ terminates the query early."},{"id":"b","text":"'into' is used after 'select' or 'group' to introduce a new range variable for a query continuation, allowing further operations (where, select, orderby) on the projected results without starting a new query."},{"id":"c","text":"'into' is used for inserting items into a collection via LINQ."},{"id":"d","text":"'into' is only valid with the join clause."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q23","text":"What is the difference between NullReferenceException and ArgumentNullException and when should each be thrown?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are equivalent; use either for null-related errors."},{"id":"b","text":"NullReferenceException is thrown by the runtime when dereferencing a null; ArgumentNullException should be thrown explicitly by library code to validate that a required argument is not null, giving a clearer error message with the parameter name."},{"id":"c","text":"ArgumentNullException is thrown by the runtime; NullReferenceException is user-thrown."},{"id":"d","text":"Neither should ever be thrown manually; use Debug.Assert instead."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q24","text":"What is a Span<T> over a string (via AsSpan()) and what does it enable?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"string.AsSpan() copies the string into a new char array."},{"id":"b","text":"string.AsSpan() returns a ReadOnlySpan<char> over the string's underlying character buffer without copying, enabling efficient substring operations, character-level parsing, and zero-allocation string manipulation."},{"id":"c","text":"string.AsSpan() enables mutating the string's characters in place."},{"id":"d","text":"string.AsSpan() is only available for StringBuilder."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q25","text":"What is Type.MakeGenericType() used for in reflection?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It creates an instance of a generic type."},{"id":"b","text":"Type.MakeGenericType(typeof(T)) constructs a closed generic type from an open generic type definition at runtime: e.g., typeof(List<>).MakeGenericType(typeof(int)) returns typeof(List<int>), which can then be used with Activator.CreateInstance."},{"id":"c","text":"It defines a new generic type at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"MakeGenericType is used to add type parameters to an existing closed type."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q26","text":"What is an async enumerable producer that uses ConfigureAwait(false) and why does it matter?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ConfigureAwait(false) cannot be used inside async iterators."},{"id":"b","text":"Inside an async iterator, each 'await' can use ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the synchronization context; this is important for library code producers that should not marshal continuations back to a specific context (e.g., UI thread)."},{"id":"c","text":"ConfigureAwait(false) on an async iterator makes it run synchronously."},{"id":"d","text":"ConfigureAwait(false) only matters when the consumer calls GetAsyncEnumerator()."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q27","text":"What is the difference between an exception filter (when) and catching then re-throwing?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"They are semantically identical; the filter is only a style preference."},{"id":"b","text":"An exception filter (when) evaluates before stack unwinding; if the condition is false the stack is not unwound and debuggers show the original throw site. Catch-then-rethrow unwinds the stack before the check, losing the original call context if the exception is re-thrown via 'throw ex'."},{"id":"c","text":"Exception filters only work in async methods."},{"id":"d","text":"Re-throwing with bare 'throw' and using a filter have identical debugger behavior."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q28","text":"What is the purpose of the Span<T>.TryCopyTo() method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"TryCopyTo() copies elements one-by-one with error recovery."},{"id":"b","text":"TryCopyTo(destination) attempts to copy all elements to the destination Span<T>; it returns true on success and false if the destination is too short, without throwing an exception, enabling safe copy operations with a capacity check."},{"id":"c","text":"TryCopyTo() copies as many elements as fit and returns the count."},{"id":"d","text":"TryCopyTo() is only available on Memory<T>, not Span<T>."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q29","text":"What is the tuple swap idiom in C# 7+ using deconstruction?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Tuples cannot be used to swap variables."},{"id":"b","text":"(a, b) = (b, a); uses tuple deconstruction to swap two variables atomically without a temporary variable, as the right-hand side tuple is created before the left-hand side assignment."},{"id":"c","text":"The swap requires creating a named tuple type first."},{"id":"d","text":"(a, b) = (b, a) is only valid for value types."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_a10_q30","text":"What does GC.KeepAlive() do and when is it necessary?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"GC.KeepAlive() pins the object in memory permanently."},{"id":"b","text":"GC.KeepAlive(obj) ensures the GC considers the object reachable until the call point, preventing premature collection in scenarios where the only live reference is used before this point (e.g., in P/Invoke when a delegate's lifetime must extend to cover the native callback)."},{"id":"c","text":"GC.KeepAlive() increments the object's reference count."},{"id":"d","text":"GC.KeepAlive() adds the object to gen 2 permanently."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

constants that don't require instance state; cannot be instantiated"},{"id":"d","text":"To mark all members as thread-safe"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_cs_b05_q30","text":"How do you call a b);"},{"id":"c","text":"MathHelper.GetMax(a, b);"},{"id":"d","text":"static MathHelper.GetMax(a, b);"}],"correctOptionId":"c"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

in arithmetic in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Rounds floating-point results"},{"id":"b","text":"Enables overflow checking so OverflowException is thrown on integer overflow"},{"id":"c","text":"Converts to checked type automatically"},{"id":"d","text":"Forces compile-time constant evaluation"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q15","text":"What happens when you cast a `long` with a value greater than `int.MaxValue` to `int` in unchecked context?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An OverflowException is thrown"},{"id":"b","text":"The value wraps around (overflow), giving an unexpected result"},{"id":"c","text":"The value is clamped to int.MaxValue"},{"id":"d","text":"A compile error occurs"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q16","text":"What is `virtual` dispatch in C# and how does the CLR resolve it at runtime?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The CLR calls the method based on the declared (compile-time) type of the variable"},{"id":"b","text":"The CLR resolves the call based on the actual (runtime) type of the object, calling the most-derived override"},{"id":"c","text":"The CLR randomly selects which override to call for performance"},{"id":"d","text":"The CLR always calls the base class version first"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q17","text":"What does `sealed` do when applied to an method"},{"id":"c","text":"Makes the method static"},{"id":"d","text":"Removes the virtual dispatch overhead"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q18","text":"Which statement about interfaces in interface"},{"id":"c","text":"Interfaces can have private instance constructors"},{"id":"d","text":"Interfaces cannot have properties"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q19","text":"What is the difference between `is` and `as` for type checking in C#?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"`is` returns bool; `as` attempts a reference cast and returns null on failure"},{"id":"b","text":"`as` returns bool; `is` attempts a cast"},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical"},{"id":"d","text":"`is` throws on mismatch; `as` returns false"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q20","text":"What structs"},{"id":"d","text":"A struct with only static members"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q24","text":"What is the main advantage of using a struct over a class for a `Point` type (x, y pressure"},{"id":"c","text":"Structs can have more methods than classes"},{"id":"d","text":"Structs support interface implementation unlike classes"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_b10_q25","text":"What does `void Swap(ref int a, ref int b)` allow when called?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Returns two values via out parameters"},{"id":"b","text":"Swaps the values of the two caller variables directly, since both are passed by reference"},{"id":"c","text":"Swaps only if both values are What LSP fix is most appropriate?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Make Rectangle a sealed class."},{"id":"b","text":"Remove the Width and Height properties from Rectangle."},{"id":"c","text":"Remove the inheritance relationship and treat Square and Rectangle as separate classes sharing an IShape interface."},{"id":"d","text":"Add a flag property IsSquare to Rectangle."}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q13","text":"ST05-5: According to LSP, which of the following is a valid behaviour for a subclass method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Throwing a new exception type not documented by the base class method."},{"id":"b","text":"Accepting a wider range of input values than the base class method."},{"id":"c","text":"Returning a narrower range of output values than the base class method."},{"id":"d","text":"Requiring more conditions to be satisfied before it can be called."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q14","text":"ST03-5: A developer must add a new payment provider to a checkout system. The system currently has a `ProcessPayment(string providerName)` method with a switch statement. What is the OCP-compliant fix?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Add the new case to the existing switch statement."},{"id":"b","text":"Refactor to use an IPaymentProvider interface and register the new provider without touching existing code."},{"id":"c","text":"Create a new ProcessPaymentV2 method alongside the old one."},{"id":"d","text":"Use reflection to discover and call the new provider class automatically."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q15","text":"ST16-2: How does the Repository pattern support the Dependency Inversion Principle?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"By coupling domain services directly to Entity Framework DbContext."},{"id":"b","text":"By defining an IRepository interface that domain services depend on, allowing the actual storage mechanism to be swapped."},{"id":"c","text":"By storing domain logic inside repository methods."},{"id":"d","text":"By making all repository methods static."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q16","text":"ST12-5: In a C# Strategy pattern, replacing a large if-else block with strategies relates most directly to which refactoring technique?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Extract Method"},{"id":"b","text":"Introduce Null Object"},{"id":"c","text":"Replace Conditional with Polymorphism"},{"id":"d","text":"Collapse Hierarchy"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q17","text":"ST04-5: Which keyword in C# prevents a class from being further subclassed, effectively closing it for extension via inheritance?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"abstract"},{"id":"b","text":"virtual"},{"id":"c","text":"sealed"},{"id":"d","text":"readonly"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q18","text":"ST13-5: An Abstract Factory is used in a cross-platform UI toolkit. factory class implementing the abstract factory interface for the new OS."},{"id":"c","text":"Add a new OS parameter to every existing product creation method."},{"id":"d","text":"Change the interface to include the new OS type as a concrete method."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q19","text":"ST06-3: Which of the following best describes an LSP-compliant inheritance without callers needing to know the difference."},{"id":"c","text":"Subclasses may throw new unchecked exceptions from any overridden method."},{"id":"d","text":"Subclasses always use `new` (hiding) rather than `override`."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q20","text":"ST15-5: Which C# feature allows subscribing to an event without being able to raise it or unsubscribe other file I/O."},{"id":"c","text":"When a class has only one public method."},{"id":"d","text":"When two classes share the same base class."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s02_q22","text":"ST16-3: INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_cs_s04', $${"id":"qp_cs_s04","title":"C# SOLID — Paper 4","description":"Covers OCP refactoring, LSP concepts, LSP refactoring, ISP concepts, ISP refactoring, Decorator Pattern, Observer Pattern, Repository Pattern, Code Smells & SOLID Violations, and Refactoring Techniques.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q1","text":"ST08-1: Which of the following best describes the ISP refactoring approach of 'splitting interfaces'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Merging two small interfaces into one larger interface for simplicity."},{"id":"b","text":"Breaking a fat interface into multiple smaller, role-specific interfaces so clients only depend on what they use."},{"id":"c","text":"Removing all interface abstractions and using concrete classes directly."},{"id":"d","text":"Replacing interfaces with abstract classes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q2","text":"ST15-9: In C#, which syntax is used to subscribe a method to an event?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"event.Subscribe(handler)"},{"id":"b","text":"event += handler"},{"id":"c","text":"event.Add(handler)"},{"id":"d","text":"event.Attach(handler)"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q3","text":"ST04-10: A notification system must support Email, SMS, and Push notifications. Applying OCP, what is the best design?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A NotificationService class with methods SendEmail(), SendSms(), and SendPush(), updated each time a new type appears."},{"id":"b","text":"An INotificationChannel interface implemented by EmailChannel, SmsChannel, and PushChannel, with new channels added as new classes."},{"id":"c","text":"A single SendNotification(string type, string message) method with a switch on type."},{"id":"d","text":"An enum NotificationType with values for each channel type."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q4","text":"ST18-1: The 'Extract Method' refactoring technique is used to:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Move a method from one class to another."},{"id":"b","text":"Turn a code fragment in a long method into a separate named method."},{"id":"c","text":"Replace a method body with a call to a strategy object."},{"id":"d","text":"Remove a method that is no longer needed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q5","text":"ST16-7: A repository method `GetOrdersByCustomer(int customerId)` returns a `List<Order>`. Which concern does this violate if business rules are also placed inside the repository method?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ISP — the repository interface has too many methods."},{"id":"b","text":"SRP — the repository mixes data-access logic with business logic."},{"id":"c","text":"OCP — the repository cannot be extended."},{"id":"d","text":"LSP — the return type is too specific."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q6","text":"ST06-8: Which of the following is a valid technique for fixing an LSP violation caused by an incorrect inheritance hierarchy?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Add null checks throughout the codebase that uses the base type."},{"id":"b","text":"Flatten the hierarchy and use composition instead of inheriting from the problematic base class."},{"id":"c","text":"Mark the violating override method as sealed."},{"id":"d","text":"Add a type-check (is/as) before each polymorphic call."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q7","text":"ST17-5: A 'Long Method' code smell indicates a violation of which SOLID principle?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"OCP — the method cannot be extended."},{"id":"b","text":"SRP — the method is doing too many things and should be broken up."},{"id":"c","text":"DIP — the method depends on concrete types."},{"id":"d","text":"ISP — the method is part of a fat interface."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q8","text":"ST05-10: Which of the following statements about LSP and type hierarchies is correct?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An 'is-a' relationship in domain terms always guarantees LSP compliance in code."},{"id":"b","text":"An 'is-a' relationship in domain terms does not guarantee LSP compliance; behavioral substitutability must also hold."},{"id":"c","text":"LSP only applies to abstract classes, not interfaces."},{"id":"d","text":"LSP compliance is only required for sealed classes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q9","text":"ST07-5: An `IMultiFunctionDevice` interface declares Print(), Scan(), Fax(), and Copy(). A SimplePrinter class implements it but throws NotSupportedException for Scan(), Fax(), and Copy(). Which principle is violated?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SRP"},{"id":"b","text":"OCP"},{"id":"c","text":"LSP"},{"id":"d","text":"ISP"}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q10","text":"ST14-8: A `CachingRepository` wraps a real `IRepository` and returns cached results. This is an example of which pattern?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Proxy (or Decorator)"},{"id":"b","text":"Singleton"},{"id":"c","text":"Adapter"},{"id":"d","text":"Facade"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q11","text":"ST08-2: After splitting a fat IWorker interface into ITaskWorker and IBreakWorker, a Robot class should:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Implement both ITaskWorker and IBreakWorker regardless of capability."},{"id":"b","text":"Implement only ITaskWorker, since robots do not take breaks."},{"id":"c","text":"Implement neither interface and use a concrete base class."},{"id":"d","text":"Implement ITaskWorker and throw an exception in IBreakWorker methods."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q12","text":"ST18-2: 'Replace Conditional with Polymorphism' is a refactoring that:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Removes all if/else and switch statements from a codebase."},{"id":"b","text":"Replaces type-checking conditional branches with overridden methods in subclasses or strategy implementations."},{"id":"c","text":"Converts all enum-based logic into lambda expressions."},{"id":"d","text":"Merges multiple conditional methods into one large method."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q13","text":"ST04-11: OCP is often said to be achieved 'parameterically' in functional programming through:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Switch expressions"},{"id":"b","text":"Higher-order functions (passing behaviour as parameters)"},{"id":"c","text":"Immutable data structures"},{"id":"d","text":"Pattern matching"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q14","text":"ST05-11: A unit test expects that calling `Calculate(animal)` returns a positive number for any `Animal`. A `VirtualAnimal` subclass always returns -1. This test failure demonstrates:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A DIP violation — the test depends on a concrete type."},{"id":"b","text":"An LSP violation — VirtualAnimal breaks the behavioral contract of Animal."},{"id":"c","text":"An SRP violation — the test tests two things at once."},{"id":"d","text":"An OCP violation — the test was not extended for VirtualAnimal."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q15","text":"ST07-6: Which of the following is an ISP-compliant refactoring of a fat IAnimal interface?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Combine IAnimal with IVehicle for maximum reuse."},{"id":"b","text":"Split IAnimal into IFlyable, ISwimmable, and IWalkable, each implemented only by animals that perform those actions."},{"id":"c","text":"Add default implementations to IAnimal so implementors don't need to override everything."},{"id":"d","text":"Replace IAnimal with an abstract class Animal."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q16","text":"ST16-8: In a Domain-Driven Design context, which layer is responsible for calling repository methods?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The presentation layer (controllers/views)."},{"id":"b","text":"The application/domain service layer."},{"id":"c","text":"The infrastructure layer directly."},{"id":"d","text":"The database migration scripts."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q17","text":"ST06-9: In C#, the `IEnumerable<T>` and `ICollection<T>` hierarchy is an example of a partial LSP concern. Why might code passing `IEnumerable<T>` be safer than passing `ICollection<T>`?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IEnumerable<T> has more methods, giving callers more options."},{"id":"b","text":"IEnumerable<T> is read-only; passing it avoids mutations that callers may not expect."},{"id":"c","text":"ICollection<T> implementations always violate LSP."},{"id":"d","text":"IEnumerable<T> is always faster at runtime."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q18","text":"ST17-6: Which of the following refactoring patterns directly addresses a 'God Class' code smell?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Inline Class"},{"id":"b","text":"Extract Class and Move Method to redistribute responsibilities"},{"id":"c","text":"Introduce Explaining Variable"},{"id":"d","text":"Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q19","text":"ST18-3: 'Extract Class' is appropriate when:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A class is too small and has fewer than three fields."},{"id":"b","text":"A subset of a class's fields and methods naturally cluster together and form a cohesive concept of their own."},{"id":"c","text":"A class has too many tests."},{"id":"d","text":"Two classes share an identical method that should be centralised."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q20","text":"ST15-10: When using EventHandler in C#, what is the purpose of the `sender` parameter?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It holds the event data payload."},{"id":"b","text":"It provides a reference to the object that raised the event, allowing subscribers to act on or query the publisher."},{"id":"c","text":"It determines the priority of the event handler."},{"id":"d","text":"It specifies whether the event is synchronous or asynchronous."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q21","text":"ST08-3: In C#, a class may implement multiple interfaces. How does this support ISP?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It allows the class to be registered as multiple service types in a DI container."},{"id":"b","text":"It enables a class to satisfy multiple role-specific contracts without forcing clients to depend on a single fat interface."},{"id":"c","text":"It causes C# to generate efficient vtable entries for each interface."},{"id":"d","text":"It reduces the number of assemblies needed."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q22","text":"ST14-9: Applying the Decorator pattern to a stream in .NET — e.g., `new GZipStream(new BufferedStream(new FileStream(...)))` — demonstrates:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The Composite pattern, because streams form a tree structure."},{"id":"b","text":"The Decorator pattern, where each stream wraps another to add behaviour (buffering, compression) without modifying Stream."},{"id":"c","text":"The Builder pattern, constructing a complex stream step by step."},{"id":"d","text":"The Adapter pattern, converting one stream interface to another."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q23","text":"ST04-12: Robert Martin's OCP states that a module should be both 'open' and 'closed' simultaneously. How is this paradox resolved?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"By having two versions of each class: an open version and a closed version."},{"id":"b","text":"By separating the stable abstraction (interface/abstract class) that is closed from the extension points (implementations) that are open."},{"id":"c","text":"By using feature flags to enable or disable behavior at runtime."},{"id":"d","text":"By keeping source code private and only distributing compiled binaries."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q24","text":"ST06-10: The Design by Contract concept of 'class invariant' in the context of LSP means:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The class must never change at runtime."},{"id":"b","text":"Conditions that must hold true for all instances of a class throughout their lifetime; subclasses must preserve base class invariants."},{"id":"c","text":"The class must have an invariant number of properties."},{"id":"d","text":"Constants defined within the class."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q25","text":"ST17-7: 'Primitive Obsession' is a code smell describing:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Using too many loops in a single method."},{"id":"b","text":"Overusing primitive types (int, string) for domain concepts that should be represented as small value objects or classes."},{"id":"c","text":"A class that has only primitive fields."},{"id":"d","text":"Using bitwise operations for business logic."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q26","text":"ST07-7: Which principle is most directly supported by ISP?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"High cohesion — interfaces should group only related operations that clients actually need."},{"id":"b","text":"Low coupling — every class should depend on every other class."},{"id":"c","text":"High coupling — interfaces should aggregate all possible operations."},{"id":"d","text":"Low cohesion — interfaces should group unrelated operations for convenience."}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q27","text":"ST16-9: Which of the following is a disadvantage of the Repository pattern?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It couples domain logic directly to the ORM."},{"id":"b","text":"It can add unnecessary abstraction layers for simple CRUD applications, increasing boilerplate code."},{"id":"c","text":"It prevents the use of dependency injection."},{"id":"d","text":"It makes unit testing harder."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q28","text":"ST18-4: 'Move Method' refactoring is appropriate when:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A method is called from many different classes."},{"id":"b","text":"A method uses more features of another class than it does of its own class, suggesting it belongs there."},{"id":"c","text":"A method is too long and needs to be split."},{"id":"d","text":"A method has too many parameters."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q29","text":"ST08-4: In C#, the ISP is often applied when defining service contracts. Which approach best applies ISP to a command/query split?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"One IService interface with all read and write methods."},{"id":"b","text":"Separate IReadRepository<T> and IWriteRepository<T> interfaces so read-only consumers don't depend on mutation methods."},{"id":"c","text":"A single abstract class with virtual read and write methods."},{"id":"d","text":"An enum-based service locator choosing read or write operations."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s04_q30","text":"ST05-12: An interface `IShape` defines `Area()`. A class `Line` implements `IShape` and returns 0 for `Area()`. Is this LSP compliant?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"No — returning 0 is always an LSP violation."},{"id":"b","text":"Yes — if the contract of Area() allows returning 0 for degenerate shapes and callers are prepared for this."},{"id":"c","text":"Yes — but only if Line is a sealed class."},{"id":"d","text":"No — Line should not implement IShape because it is not a 2D shape."}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

that:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"High-level modules should depend directly on low-level modules for efficiency."},{"id":"b","text":"Both high-level and low-level modules should depend on abstractions; abstractions should not depend on details."},{"id":"c","text":"Low-level modules should control high-level modules."},{"id":"d","text":"All dependencies should flow from interfaces to concrete classes."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s05_q2","text":"ST18-5: The 'Extract Method' refactoring primarily improves code by:","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Reducing runtime memory allocation."},{"id":"b","text":"Improving readability, enabling reuse, and supporting SRP by giving the extracted logic a descriptive name."},{"id":"c","text":"Removing the need for unit tests on the original method."},{"id":"d","text":"Making the method run faster through inlining."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s05_q3","text":"ST05-13: Which of the following correctly distinguishes 'behavioral subtyping' from simple 'syntactic subtyping'?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Behavioral subtyping requires the subtype to have more methods."},{"id":"b","text":"Behavioral subtyping requires that the subtype's observable behavior fulfils the same contract as the supertype, not just that method signatures match."},{"id":"c","text":"Syntactic subtyping is stricter than behavioral subtyping."},{"id":"d","text":"Behavioral subtyping only applies to generic types."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s05_q4","text":"ST16-10: Specification pattern is sometimes combined with reflection."}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_cs_s05_q5","text":"ST06-11: Which of the following behaviors in a subclass represents 'weakening a postcondition', violating
