Which should they choose and why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"VARCHAR(255) because it uses less storage and all characters are supported via code pages"},{"id":"b","text":"NVARCHAR(255) because it stores Unicode (UTF-16) and can represent characters from any language without data loss"},{"id":"c","text":"CHAR(255) because fixed-length columns are faster to read"},{"id":"d","text":"VARCHAR(MAX) to ensure no truncation occurs"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q18","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that accepts @ProductId INT and @Price DECIMAL(10,2) as parameters and runs: UPDATE Products SET Price = @Price WHERE ProductId = @ProductId. After the update, they need to check whether any row was actually modified. What is the correct approach?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Check @@ERROR immediately after the UPDATE"},{"id":"b","text":"Use SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Products WHERE ProductId = @ProductId after the UPDATE"},{"id":"c","text":"Check @@ROWCOUNT immediately after the UPDATE statement; it reflects the number of rows affected by the most recent statement"},{"id":"d","text":"Use TRY...CATCH — if no row matched, an exception is thrown automatically"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q19","text":"A developer wants to produce a running total of daily sales ordered by SaleDate. Which window function expression achieves this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SUM(DailySales) OVER (ORDER BY SaleDate ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)"},{"id":"b","text":"SUM(DailySales) OVER (PARTITION BY SaleDate)"},{"id":"c","text":"CUMULATIVE_SUM(DailySales) ORDER BY SaleDate"},{"id":"d","text":"SUM(DailySales) GROUP BY SaleDate ORDER BY SaleDate"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q20","text":"A developer reviews a slow query and sees the execution plan shows a Key Lookup operator after a non-clustered index seek. What does this indicate and how should it be resolved?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query has a missing JOIN condition; add the correct ON clause"},{"id":"b","text":"The non-clustered index satisfied the WHERE clause but the query also needs columns not in the index, forcing extra lookups to the clustered index; adding those columns as INCLUDE columns creates a covering index"},{"id":"c","text":"The clustered index is fragmented and needs to be rebuilt"},{"id":"d","text":"Key Lookup means the table has no primary key; one must be added"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q21","text":"A developer uses EXISTS instead of IN for a subquery check: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Orders o WHERE o.CustomerId = c.CustomerId). What is the key advantage of EXISTS over IN in this context when Orders is a large table?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"EXISTS returns multiple rows; IN returns only one row"},{"id":"b","text":"EXISTS short-circuits as soon as one matching row is found, avoiding a full scan of the subquery result; IN must evaluate the complete subquery result set first"},{"id":"c","text":"IN cannot be used with subqueries, only with literal lists"},{"id":"d","text":"EXISTS ignores NULL values automatically; IN does not"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q22","text":"A developer needs to alias a calculated column and then filter on it in the same SELECT. They write: SELECT SalePrice * Quantity AS LineTotal FROM OrderItems WHERE LineTotal > 500. What will happen?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query works because SQL Server resolves aliases before WHERE evaluation"},{"id":"b","text":"The query fails because column aliases defined in SELECT cannot be referenced in the WHERE clause of the same query; the expression must be repeated or wrapped in a subquery"},{"id":"c","text":"LineTotal will be evaluated as 0 in the WHERE clause"},{"id":"d","text":"The query works only if LineTotal is an existing column name in the table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q23","text":"Two developers are debating soft delete vs hard delete for a customer records table. Soft delete adds an IsDeleted BIT column. What is the primary ongoing maintenance cost introduced by soft delete?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Soft delete requires a trigger on every table"},{"id":"b","text":"Every query against the table must include WHERE IsDeleted = 0 to exclude logically deleted rows, increasing query complexity and risk of accidentally exposing deleted data"},{"id":"c","text":"Soft delete prevents the use of foreign keys"},{"id":"d","text":"The IsDeleted column adds significant storage overhead"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q24","text":"A developer writes a self-join to find employees who share the same manager: SELECT a.EmpName, b.EmpName AS Colleague FROM Employees a JOIN Employees b ON a.ManagerId = b.ManagerId WHERE a.EmpId <> b.EmpId. What problem does this query have?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Self-joins are not allowed in SQL Server"},{"id":"b","text":"The query will return each pair twice (A with B and B with A); adding AND a.EmpId < b.EmpId eliminates duplicates"},{"id":"c","text":"The WHERE clause should use NOT IN instead of <>"},{"id":"d","text":"The query will fail because both aliases reference the same table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q25","text":"A developer applies DISTINCT to remove duplicate rows: SELECT DISTINCT CustomerId, OrderDate FROM Orders. A colleague suggests GROUP BY CustomerId, OrderDate instead. What is the practical difference in this scenario?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DISTINCT cannot be used with multiple columns"},{"id":"b","text":"GROUP BY requires an aggregate function; DISTINCT does not"},{"id":"c","text":"For this scenario they produce identical results; DISTINCT is more readable while GROUP BY is preferred when aggregate functions are also needed"},{"id":"d","text":"DISTINCT always performs better than GROUP BY"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q26","text":"A developer encounters a deadlock between two transactions. T1 locks Table A then tries to lock Table B; T2 locks Table B then tries to lock Table A. What is the standard developer-side approach to preventing this class of deadlock?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Use NOLOCK hints on all queries"},{"id":"b","text":"Ensure all transactions acquire locks in a consistent order — both T1 and T2 should lock Table A before Table B"},{"id":"c","text":"Increase transaction isolation to SERIALIZABLE"},{"id":"d","text":"Wrap every statement in a separate transaction"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q27","text":"A developer needs the previous month's revenue next to each month's revenue to calculate month-over-month growth. Which window function retrieves the previous row's value?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"FIRST_VALUE(Revenue) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth)"},{"id":"b","text":"LAG(Revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth)"},{"id":"c","text":"LEAD(Revenue, -1) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth)"},{"id":"d","text":"PREV(Revenue) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth)"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q28","text":"A developer needs to use a CHECK constraint to ensure that a product's SalePrice is always greater than its CostPrice. Which constraint definition achieves this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CONSTRAINT chk_price CHECK (SalePrice > 0 AND CostPrice > 0)"},{"id":"b","text":"CONSTRAINT chk_price CHECK (SalePrice > CostPrice)"},{"id":"c","text":"CHECK constraints cannot compare two columns in the same table"},{"id":"d","text":"CONSTRAINT chk_price FOREIGN KEY (SalePrice) REFERENCES CostPrice"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q29","text":"A developer wants to find all product categories that exist in a current catalog table but NOT in a legacy catalog table. Which set operation is most appropriate?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"UNION ALL"},{"id":"b","text":"INTERSECT"},{"id":"c","text":"EXCEPT"},{"id":"d","text":"CROSS JOIN"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d01_q30","text":"A developer adds a non-clustered index on a table that receives hundreds of INSERT and UPDATE operations per second. After adding the index, write throughput drops noticeably. What is the cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Non-clustered indexes lock the table exclusively during all writes"},{"id":"b","text":"Every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also maintain the index structure, adding write overhead; indexes improve read performance at the cost of write performance"},{"id":"c","text":"The index causes SQL Server to switch to a single-threaded execution plan"},{"id":"d","text":"Non-clustered indexes are only supported on read-only tables"}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: sql-dev-02.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_sql_d02', $${"id":"qp_sql_d02","title":"SQL Developer — Paper 2","description":"Developer-focused SQL scenarios covering query writing, joins, filtering, aggregations, CTEs, indexes, transactions, window functions, stored procedures, and data safety.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q1","text":"A developer rewrites SELECT * FROM Invoices to SELECT InvoiceId, CustomerId, Amount, InvoiceDate FROM Invoices. Which benefit is NOT directly gained by this change?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query can now benefit from a covering index that includes exactly those four columns"},{"id":"b","text":"Less data is transferred over the network to the application"},{"id":"c","text":"The query automatically runs in parallel across multiple CPU cores"},{"id":"d","text":"Adding a new column to Invoices will not break application code that expects a fixed column set"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q2","text":"A developer is asked to list all products that have never been ordered. The Products table has a ProductId primary key and the OrderItems table has a ProductId foreign key. Which approach most efficiently finds products with no orders when OrderItems is very large?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SELECT p.ProductId FROM Products p WHERE p.ProductId NOT IN (SELECT ProductId FROM OrderItems)"},{"id":"b","text":"SELECT p.ProductId FROM Products p WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM OrderItems oi WHERE oi.ProductId = p.ProductId)"},{"id":"c","text":"SELECT p.ProductId FROM Products p INNER JOIN OrderItems oi ON p.ProductId = oi.ProductId WHERE oi.ProductId IS NULL"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT ProductId FROM Products EXCEPT ALL SELECT ProductId FROM OrderItems"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q3","text":"A developer writes: SELECT DeptName, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSalary FROM Employees GROUP BY DeptName HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000 ORDER BY AvgSalary DESC. A colleague asks why AVG(Salary) is repeated in HAVING rather than using the alias. What is the correct explanation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"HAVING does not support aggregate functions; only column references are allowed"},{"id":"b","text":"Column aliases defined in SELECT are not available in HAVING because HAVING is logically evaluated before SELECT in the SQL processing order"},{"id":"c","text":"Using an alias in HAVING would produce a different result than repeating the aggregate"},{"id":"d","text":"SQL Server does not permit aliases in any clause other than SELECT"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q4","text":"A developer needs to number rows for pagination: page 3 with 20 rows per page. They write: SELECT * FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC) AS RN FROM Articles) t WHERE t.RN BETWEEN 41 AND 60. What is the main drawback of this approach on a large dataset?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ROW_NUMBER() is not supported in subqueries"},{"id":"b","text":"BETWEEN cannot be used with window function results"},{"id":"c","text":"SQL Server must scan and number all preceding rows before returning the requested page, making later pages increasingly expensive"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT * inside a subquery causes a syntax error"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q5","text":"A developer builds a stored procedure that accepts a table name as a parameter and constructs dynamic SQL: EXEC('SELECT * FROM ' + @TableName). Beyond SQL injection, what additional risk exists with this pattern?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"EXEC cannot run SELECT statements"},{"id":"b","text":"The procedure execution plan cannot be cached and reused, and any caller with EXECUTE permission on the SP gains implicit access to any table they can name"},{"id":"c","text":"Dynamic SQL inside a stored procedure is automatically encrypted"},{"id":"d","text":"Table names passed as parameters are automatically validated against sys.tables"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q6","text":"A developer notices that the following query performs a full table scan despite an index on OrderDate: SELECT OrderId, CustomerId FROM Orders WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024. How should the WHERE clause be rewritten to use the index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"WHERE OrderDate LIKE '2024%'"},{"id":"b","text":"WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01' AND OrderDate < '2025-01-01'"},{"id":"c","text":"WHERE CAST(OrderDate AS VARCHAR) LIKE '2024%'"},{"id":"d","text":"WHERE DATEPART(YEAR, OrderDate) = 2024"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q7","text":"A developer sets SET XACT_ABORT ON at the top of a stored procedure that contains a multi-statement transaction. During execution a constraint violation occurs. What does SET XACT_ABORT ON guarantee?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The violating statement is silently skipped and execution continues"},{"id":"b","text":"SQL Server automatically terminates and rolls back the entire transaction when any statement raises a runtime error, even if no explicit ROLLBACK is coded"},{"id":"c","text":"Only the current statement is rolled back; previous statements remain committed"},{"id":"d","text":"SET XACT_ABORT ON only applies to DDL statements inside transactions"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q8","text":"A developer writes: SELECT COUNT(ManagerId) FROM Employees. The ManagerId column is NULL for the CEO row. What value does COUNT(ManagerId) return compared to COUNT(*)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Both return the same value because COUNT ignores NULLs in all forms"},{"id":"b","text":"COUNT(ManagerId) excludes NULL rows and returns one less than COUNT(*) in this case"},{"id":"c","text":"COUNT(ManagerId) returns NULL because one of the values is NULL"},{"id":"d","text":"COUNT(*) excludes NULL rows; COUNT(ManagerId) includes them"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q9","text":"A developer wants to use a recursive CTE to traverse an employee hierarchy starting from a given manager and returning all subordinates at any depth. Which part of the recursive CTE syntax is essential to prevent infinite recursion?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The anchor member must SELECT from the recursive member"},{"id":"b","text":"A MAXRECURSION option or a WHERE condition in the recursive member that ensures rows are eventually excluded, terminating the recursion"},{"id":"c","text":"The CTE must use UNION instead of UNION ALL"},{"id":"d","text":"Recursive CTEs must always have an ORDER BY clause"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q10","text":"A developer creates a non-clustered index on a Transactions table: CREATE INDEX IX_Trans_Status ON Transactions(Status). The column Status has only 3 distinct values: 'Pending', 'Complete', 'Cancelled'. After 2 million rows, the index is rarely chosen by the query optimiser. Why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Non-clustered indexes require at least 10 distinct values to be effective"},{"id":"b","text":"Low cardinality columns make an index ineffective because each value matches a large fraction of rows, making a table scan cheaper than random index lookups"},{"id":"c","text":"The index should have been created as a clustered index to work on low-cardinality columns"},{"id":"d","text":"SQL Server does not create indexes on columns with fewer than 10 distinct values"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q11","text":"A developer uses DENSE_RANK() instead of RANK() for a leaderboard query. Two players have the same top score. What is the key difference in the rank assigned to the third-highest score?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DENSE_RANK assigns 3 to the third-highest; RANK also assigns 3"},{"id":"b","text":"DENSE_RANK assigns 2 to the third-highest (no gap); RANK assigns 3 (skips rank 2 because two rows tied at rank 1)"},{"id":"c","text":"DENSE_RANK and RANK produce identical results when there is only one tie"},{"id":"d","text":"DENSE_RANK assigns 1 to all tied rows and 2 to all subsequent rows"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q12","text":"Before running a large UPDATE on a production table, a developer wants to verify which rows will be affected without making any changes. What is the safest technique?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Run the UPDATE with NOLOCK to preview changes without locking"},{"id":"b","text":"Wrap the UPDATE in BEGIN TRANSACTION, run SELECT with the same WHERE clause to confirm affected rows, then ROLLBACK"},{"id":"c","text":"Use UPDATE with OUTPUT clause to see affected rows before committing"},{"id":"d","text":"Run the UPDATE in a stored procedure with TRY...CATCH so errors auto-rollback"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q13","text":"A developer joins three tables: Orders, OrderItems, and Products. They notice the result has far more rows than expected. After investigation, they find that one OrderItems record has a NULL ProductId. How does this affect an INNER JOIN between OrderItems and Products?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The NULL row causes a Cartesian product with all Products rows"},{"id":"b","text":"The row with NULL ProductId is excluded from the INNER JOIN result because NULL does not match any ProductId value"},{"id":"c","text":"SQL Server replaces NULL with 0 for JOIN evaluation"},{"id":"d","text":"The JOIN raises a foreign key violation error"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q14","text":"A developer is debating whether to use a subquery or a JOIN to retrieve the most recent order date for each customer alongside their profile data. Which statement accurately reflects the trade-off?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Subqueries are always faster than JOINs for aggregation tasks"},{"id":"b","text":"A correlated subquery in SELECT runs once per customer row which can be slow; joining to a derived table or CTE that pre-aggregates MAX(OrderDate) per customer is usually more efficient"},{"id":"c","text":"JOINs cannot be used with aggregate functions"},{"id":"d","text":"Subqueries cannot reference outer query columns in SQL Server"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q15","text":"A developer needs to prevent phantom reads in a financial reporting transaction where the same range query must return identical results if run multiple times within one transaction. Which isolation level guarantees this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"READ UNCOMMITTED"},{"id":"b","text":"READ COMMITTED"},{"id":"c","text":"REPEATABLE READ"},{"id":"d","text":"SERIALIZABLE"}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q16","text":"A developer writes: SELECT ProductId, ProductName FROM Products WHERE Category = NULL. The query returns 0 rows even though there are products with no category. What is wrong?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"NULL values are stored differently and require the ISNULL() function to detect"},{"id":"b","text":"Comparing any value to NULL using = always returns UNKNOWN, which is treated as false; the correct syntax is WHERE Category IS NULL"},{"id":"c","text":"The column Category must be cast to VARCHAR before comparing to NULL"},{"id":"d","text":"NULL comparison works fine; the products simply have an empty string not NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q17","text":"A developer is designing a table to store financial transaction amounts and is choosing between FLOAT and DECIMAL(18,4). Which should they choose for monetary values and why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"FLOAT because it supports a larger range of values"},{"id":"b","text":"DECIMAL(18,4) because it stores exact values; FLOAT is an approximate type that can introduce rounding errors in calculations like summing thousands of transactions"},{"id":"c","text":"Either type works because SQL Server rounds results automatically"},{"id":"d","text":"FLOAT because monetary amounts always have exactly 2 decimal places"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q18","text":"A stored procedure has an OUTPUT parameter @NewId INT. Inside the procedure, an INSERT is performed and the new identity value is captured. Which statement correctly retrieves the newly inserted identity value into @NewId?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SET @NewId = @@IDENTITY"},{"id":"b","text":"SET @NewId = SCOPE_IDENTITY()"},{"id":"c","text":"SET @NewId = IDENT_CURRENT('TableName')"},{"id":"d","text":"SET @NewId = LAST_INSERT_ID()"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q19","text":"A developer uses LEAD() to compare the current month's sales with the next month's sales in a report. For December (the last row), there is no next month. What value does LEAD() return by default for the last row?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"0"},{"id":"b","text":"The same value as the current row"},{"id":"c","text":"NULL, unless a default value is specified as the third argument to LEAD()"},{"id":"d","text":"An error is raised for the last row"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q20","text":"A developer is deciding whether to add an index to a column used only in SELECT queries that run once per day, while the table receives 50,000 inserts per hour. What is the right decision?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Always add an index to every column used in a WHERE clause"},{"id":"b","text":"The index would degrade high-frequency insert performance significantly for minimal read gain; it should only be added if the once-daily query has demonstrably unacceptable performance"},{"id":"c","text":"Indexes have no impact on INSERT performance"},{"id":"d","text":"Add the index and also add FILLFACTOR=100 to minimise overhead"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q21","text":"A developer writes the following WHERE clause: WHERE Status = 'Active' OR Department = 'Sales' AND StartDate > '2024-01-01'. A bug report says too many rows are returned. What is the operator precedence issue?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"OR has higher precedence than AND, so the condition is evaluated as (Status = 'Active' OR Department = 'Sales') AND StartDate > '2024-01-01'"},{"id":"b","text":"AND has higher precedence than OR, so the condition is evaluated as Status = 'Active' OR (Department = 'Sales' AND StartDate > '2024-01-01'), returning all Active rows regardless of department or date"},{"id":"c","text":"AND and OR have equal precedence and are evaluated left to right"},{"id":"d","text":"String comparisons are evaluated before boolean operators"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q22","text":"A developer needs to avoid repeating a complex filtered dataset across multiple aggregations in the same query. They consider a CTE vs an inline subquery. What does a CTE improve in this scenario beyond just performance?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CTEs are always faster than inline subqueries"},{"id":"b","text":"A CTE improves readability by naming the intermediate result set and separating it from the main aggregation logic, making the query easier to understand and maintain"},{"id":"c","text":"CTEs automatically create a temporary index on the result set"},{"id":"d","text":"Inline subqueries cannot be used alongside GROUP BY"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q23","text":"A developer adds a FK constraint: FOREIGN KEY (DepartmentId) REFERENCES Departments(DepartmentId). An attempt to INSERT an employee with a DepartmentId that does not exist in Departments fails. What is the benefit of this enforcement at the database level compared to enforcing it only in application code?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"FK constraints are optional and only matter during migrations"},{"id":"b","text":"Enforcement in the database ensures referential integrity regardless of which application, script, or direct connection accesses the data, not just through the main application path"},{"id":"c","text":"Application code enforcement is always faster than database constraint enforcement"},{"id":"d","text":"FK constraints prevent NULLs in the foreign key column automatically"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q24","text":"A developer is asked to query an employee table and join it to itself to display each employee's manager name. The Employees table has EmployeeId and ManagerId columns. Which JOIN type should be used so that the CEO (who has NULL ManagerId) is also included in results?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"INNER JOIN — emp e JOIN Employees m ON e.ManagerId = m.EmployeeId"},{"id":"b","text":"LEFT JOIN — emp e LEFT JOIN Employees m ON e.ManagerId = m.EmployeeId"},{"id":"c","text":"RIGHT JOIN — emp e RIGHT JOIN Employees m ON e.ManagerId = m.EmployeeId"},{"id":"d","text":"FULL OUTER JOIN to include unmatched rows from both sides"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q25","text":"A developer uses SELECT DISTINCT ProductId, CategoryId FROM OrderItems. The query is slow on 10 million rows. An index on (ProductId, CategoryId) exists. Why might DISTINCT still cause a performance issue?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DISTINCT cannot use non-clustered indexes"},{"id":"b","text":"DISTINCT requires SQL Server to sort or hash all rows to identify duplicates, which is expensive even with an index when the combination of ProductId and CategoryId has high cardinality"},{"id":"c","text":"DISTINCT is only slow when used with JOINs"},{"id":"d","text":"The index on (ProductId, CategoryId) would be used only if those columns appear in a WHERE clause"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q26","text":"A developer uses READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT ISOLATION (RCSI) on a database. How does this differ from standard READ COMMITTED in terms of reader-writer blocking?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"RCSI increases locking and blocking compared to standard READ COMMITTED"},{"id":"b","text":"Under RCSI, readers do not block writers and writers do not block readers because readers access a row version from tempdb instead of waiting for a write lock to be released"},{"id":"c","text":"RCSI eliminates all anomalies including phantom reads"},{"id":"d","text":"RCSI is a query hint applied per statement, not a database-level setting"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q27","text":"A developer applies SUM(Sales) OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY SaleMonth ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) in a report. What does this expression compute?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The total sales for the entire region regardless of month"},{"id":"b","text":"A cumulative (running) total of sales within each region, accumulating from the first month up to and including the current month"},{"id":"c","text":"The average sales per region per month"},{"id":"d","text":"The sales difference between the current month and the previous month"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q28","text":"A developer discovers that a column storing UK postcodes is defined as VARCHAR(10), but some application paths can insert 20-character strings. What data integrity mechanism at the database level would silently prevent this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A CHECK constraint: CHECK (LEN(Postcode) <= 8)"},{"id":"b","text":"Changing the column to VARCHAR(10) truncates values at the database level when ANSI warnings are off, but this can cause silent data corruption; a CHECK constraint on length is safer"},{"id":"c","text":"A UNIQUE constraint on Postcode"},{"id":"d","text":"Defining the column as CHAR(10) automatically trims excess characters"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q29","text":"A developer has two queries: Query A uses IN with a subquery returning 500k rows; Query B uses EXISTS with the same subquery. On a large table, which typically performs better and why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IN always outperforms EXISTS because IN evaluates the full result once"},{"id":"b","text":"They always produce identical query plans in SQL Server 2019 and later"},{"id":"c","text":"EXISTS often performs better because it stops evaluating the subquery as soon as one matching row is found; IN may require materialising the full subquery result, especially when it contains NULLs which can cause IN to behave unexpectedly"},{"id":"d","text":"EXISTS cannot be used with subqueries that return more than 1000 rows"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d02_q30","text":"A developer needs to assign sequential page numbers within each chapter of a document stored in a Documents table (ChapterId, PageContent, InsertedAt). Which query correctly numbers pages starting at 1 for each chapter?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SELECT ChapterId, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY InsertedAt) AS PageNum FROM Documents"},{"id":"b","text":"SELECT ChapterId, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ChapterId ORDER BY InsertedAt) AS PageNum FROM Documents"},{"id":"c","text":"SELECT ChapterId, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY InsertedAt) AS PageNum FROM Documents"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT ChapterId, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY ChapterId) AS PageNum FROM Documents"}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: sql-dev-03.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_sql_d03', $${"id":"qp_sql_d03","title":"SQL Developer — Paper 3","description":"Developer-focused SQL scenarios covering query writing, joins, filtering, aggregations, CTEs, indexes, transactions, window functions, stored procedures, and data safety.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q1","text":"A developer writes a report query that joins five tables and selects 40 columns with SELECT *. The DBA notices the query causes excessive memory grant warnings. Which refactoring addresses this most directly?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Add a MAXDOP hint to limit parallelism"},{"id":"b","text":"Select only the columns needed by the report, reducing the row width so sort and hash operations require less memory"},{"id":"c","text":"Convert all INNER JOINs to LEFT JOINs"},{"id":"d","text":"Add ORDER BY to every subquery in the joins"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q2","text":"A developer writes the following to count active users who have placed at least one order: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users u JOIN Orders o ON u.UserId = o.UserId WHERE u.IsActive = 1. What is a potential overcounting issue?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"COUNT(*) counts NULL rows which inflates the total"},{"id":"b","text":"The JOIN multiplies user rows — a user with 5 orders is counted 5 times; COUNT(DISTINCT u.UserId) is needed"},{"id":"c","text":"WHERE must appear before JOIN in standard SQL"},{"id":"d","text":"COUNT(*) cannot be used with a JOIN"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q3","text":"A developer uses COALESCE in an ORDER BY clause: ORDER BY COALESCE(PreferredName, FirstName). What does this accomplish and when is it useful?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"COALESCE cannot be used in ORDER BY; a CASE expression must be used instead"},{"id":"b","text":"It sorts by PreferredName when it is not NULL, falling back to FirstName for rows where PreferredName is NULL, ensuring all rows sort by the most relevant available name"},{"id":"c","text":"It sorts NULL values to the top of the result set regardless of column value"},{"id":"d","text":"COALESCE in ORDER BY causes a full table scan because it wraps the column in a function"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q4","text":"A developer wants to use NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY Revenue DESC) to bucket customers into quartiles. The result set has 101 rows. How will NTILE distribute the extra row?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The extra row is discarded"},{"id":"b","text":"NTILE raises an error when the row count is not evenly divisible"},{"id":"c","text":"NTILE distributes the remainder rows one each to the first buckets — bucket 1 gets 26 rows, buckets 2-4 get 25 rows each"},{"id":"d","text":"All four buckets receive 25 rows and the extra row is assigned to a fifth bucket"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q5","text":"A developer uses sp_executesql with a parameterised query: EXEC sp_executesql N'SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @cid', N'@cid INT', @cid = @InputId. How does this protect against SQL injection compared to string concatenation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"sp_executesql encrypts the query before execution"},{"id":"b","text":"The parameter @cid is passed separately from the query string; user input is never interpreted as SQL syntax, so malicious strings cannot modify query structure"},{"id":"c","text":"sp_executesql validates that @InputId is a numeric value before execution"},{"id":"d","text":"sp_executesql runs in a sandboxed context with no table access"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q6","text":"A query on a Logs table with 50M rows uses: WHERE CONVERT(DATE, LogTimestamp) = '2025-03-15'. An index exists on LogTimestamp (DATETIME). Why does this query not use the index efficiently?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DATE is not compatible with DATETIME indexes"},{"id":"b","text":"Wrapping LogTimestamp in CONVERT() makes the expression non-sargable — the optimiser cannot seek into the index because each row must be converted at scan time"},{"id":"c","text":"The index on DATETIME columns is never used for equality filters"},{"id":"d","text":"CONVERT requires a CAST hint to enable index seeks"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q7","text":"A developer handles an order placement transaction. The code inserts an order header, then inserts order lines. A constraint violation occurs on the second order line insert. The developer catches the error but does NOT explicitly ROLLBACK. What state is the transaction in?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The entire transaction is automatically rolled back by SQL Server"},{"id":"b","text":"The transaction remains open and uncommitted; subsequent commits would commit the partial data unless a ROLLBACK is issued"},{"id":"c","text":"Only the failed statement is undone; all prior inserts are committed"},{"id":"d","text":"The connection is automatically closed after a constraint violation in a transaction"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q8","text":"A developer writes: SELECT Department, SUM(Salary) AS TotalPay, COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) AS HeadCount FROM Employees GROUP BY Department. Which statement about COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) is true?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"COUNT(DISTINCT col) is invalid inside GROUP BY queries"},{"id":"b","text":"COUNT(DISTINCT EmployeeId) counts unique EmployeeId values within each department group, useful when joins might introduce duplicate rows"},{"id":"c","text":"COUNT(DISTINCT col) is identical to COUNT(col) when the column has no duplicates in the raw table"},{"id":"d","text":"DISTINCT inside COUNT() is only supported in SQL Server 2019 and later"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q9","text":"A developer writes a CTE to find the second-highest salary in a company. They write: WITH Ranked AS (SELECT Salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk FROM Employees) SELECT Salary FROM Ranked WHERE Rnk = 2. Why is DENSE_RANK preferred over RANK for this use case?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DENSE_RANK is faster than RANK on large tables"},{"id":"b","text":"If multiple employees share the highest salary, RANK would skip rank 2 entirely; DENSE_RANK ensures there is always a rank 2 value representing the second distinct salary level"},{"id":"c","text":"RANK cannot be used inside CTEs"},{"id":"d","text":"DENSE_RANK supports NULL salaries while RANK does not"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q10","text":"A developer sees that SQL Server's Missing Index DMV suggests an index on (EmailAddress) INCLUDE (FirstName, LastName, CreatedDate) for a Users table. What does the INCLUDE clause mean and why is it suggested?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"INCLUDE columns become part of the index key and are used for filtering"},{"id":"b","text":"INCLUDE columns are stored at the leaf level of the index but not in the key; they allow the query to be satisfied entirely from the index without a Key Lookup back to the clustered index"},{"id":"c","text":"INCLUDE prevents the index from being used for sorting operations"},{"id":"d","text":"INCLUDE columns are only stored in memory and are not persisted to disk"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q11","text":"A developer uses ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerId ORDER BY OrderDate DESC) AS RowNum and filters WHERE RowNum = 1. What does this return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The first order ever placed by each customer"},{"id":"b","text":"The most recent order per customer, with exactly one row returned per customer"},{"id":"c","text":"All orders where the customer placed exactly one order total"},{"id":"d","text":"The order with the lowest OrderDate per customer"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q12","text":"A developer issues an UPDATE without a WHERE clause on a 200,000 row Prices table in a production database. After the statement executes, they realise the mistake. What is the correct immediate action assuming the session has not been closed?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Run DELETE to remove the incorrectly updated rows"},{"id":"b","text":"If the UPDATE was inside an explicit transaction that has not been committed, issue ROLLBACK TRANSACTION immediately"},{"id":"c","text":"Restore the entire database from backup before doing anything else"},{"id":"d","text":"Run UPDATE again with the original values from memory"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q13","text":"A developer queries Employees with a LEFT JOIN to Departments: SELECT e.Name, d.DeptName FROM Employees e LEFT JOIN Departments d ON e.DeptId = d.DeptId. Three employees have a DeptId that does not exist in Departments. What appears in the DeptName column for those rows?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An empty string ''"},{"id":"b","text":"NULL"},{"id":"c","text":"The word 'UNKNOWN'"},{"id":"d","text":"Those rows are excluded from the result"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q14","text":"A developer is querying two tables: SalesQ1 and SalesQ2 with identical column structures and wants to combine results including duplicates across both quarters. Which set operation should be used?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"UNION, which removes duplicates"},{"id":"b","text":"UNION ALL, which retains all rows including duplicates across both result sets"},{"id":"c","text":"INTERSECT, which returns rows common to both"},{"id":"d","text":"EXCEPT, which returns rows in Q1 not in Q2"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q15","text":"An e-commerce application uses SNAPSHOT isolation to allow readers and writers to work concurrently. A developer notices that a long-running transaction is causing tempdb to grow rapidly. What is the cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SNAPSHOT isolation locks all rows in tempdb"},{"id":"b","text":"SNAPSHOT isolation stores row versions in tempdb; the longer a transaction runs, the more versions accumulate as other transactions modify the data, growing tempdb"},{"id":"c","text":"tempdb growth is unrelated to isolation level"},{"id":"d","text":"SNAPSHOT isolation disables auto-shrink on tempdb"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q16","text":"A developer writes: SELECT ProductId, CASE WHEN Stock > 100 THEN 'High' WHEN Stock > 10 THEN 'Medium' ELSE 'Low' END AS StockLevel FROM Products WHERE StockLevel = 'High'. What is wrong?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CASE WHEN cannot be used in SELECT alongside WHERE"},{"id":"b","text":"The alias StockLevel cannot be referenced in the WHERE clause of the same query level; the CASE expression must be repeated in WHERE or the query wrapped in a subquery"},{"id":"c","text":"ELSE is not valid in a searched CASE expression"},{"id":"d","text":"CASE WHEN with a string result requires a CAST in the WHERE clause"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q17","text":"A developer stores a customer's date of birth as VARCHAR(10) in the format 'DD-MM-YYYY'. When they try to sort customers by age they get incorrect results. What is the root cause and solution?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"VARCHAR columns cannot be sorted; a CAST is always required"},{"id":"b","text":"Storing dates as VARCHAR means sorting is alphabetical (by day first) rather than chronological; dates should be stored as DATE or DATETIME2 data types for correct temporal ordering"},{"id":"c","text":"The format 'DD-MM-YYYY' must be converted to 'YYYY-MM-DD' using REPLACE before sorting"},{"id":"d","text":"DATE columns sort differently to DATETIME columns, causing discrepancies"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q18","text":"A developer adds TRY...CATCH to a stored procedure that runs multiple INSERTs. Inside the CATCH block they want to surface the original error to the caller. Which statement achieves this in SQL Server?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"RETURN -1"},{"id":"b","text":"THROW; (re-throws the original error with its original error number, severity, and state)"},{"id":"c","text":"RAISERROR('An error occurred', 16, 1)"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT ERROR_MESSAGE()"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q19","text":"A developer writes: SELECT ProductId, SaleMonth, SUM(Revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY ProductId ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS RunningRevenue FROM MonthlySales. What does this query produce for each row?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The total revenue for the product across all months"},{"id":"b","text":"A cumulative sum of Revenue from the first month up to the current month, reset for each product"},{"id":"c","text":"The revenue for the current month only"},{"id":"d","text":"The average monthly revenue per product"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q20","text":"A developer queries an Orders table frequently with: WHERE ShippedDate IS NULL AND OrderStatus = 'Processing'. Which composite index definition best supports this query?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(ShippedDate)"},{"id":"b","text":"CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(OrderStatus, ShippedDate) WHERE ShippedDate IS NULL AND OrderStatus = 'Processing'"},{"id":"c","text":"CREATE INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(OrderId) INCLUDE (ShippedDate, OrderStatus)"},{"id":"d","text":"CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX IX_Orders ON Orders(ShippedDate, OrderStatus)"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q21","text":"A developer writes a correlated subquery: SELECT c.CustomerId, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Orders o WHERE o.CustomerId = c.CustomerId) AS OrderCount FROM Customers c. On 200k customers, the query is very slow. What is the most efficient rewrite?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Replace the subquery with EXISTS"},{"id":"b","text":"LEFT JOIN Customers to a pre-aggregated subquery: (SELECT CustomerId, COUNT(*) AS OrderCount FROM Orders GROUP BY CustomerId) and use COALESCE to handle NULLs for customers with no orders"},{"id":"c","text":"Use CROSS APPLY instead of a correlated subquery"},{"id":"d","text":"Add an index on Customers.CustomerId"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q22","text":"A developer must write a CASE expression that labels a product's margin: 'High' if margin > 30%, 'Medium' if 10-30%, and 'Low' otherwise. The margin is calculated as (SalePrice - CostPrice) / SalePrice. What risk exists when CostPrice equals SalePrice?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The CASE expression cannot handle negative results"},{"id":"b","text":"If SalePrice is 0, the division produces a divide-by-zero error; this must be guarded with NULLIF(SalePrice, 0) or a CASE WHEN SalePrice = 0 THEN NULL ELSE ... END"},{"id":"c","text":"CASE expressions automatically return 0 for division by zero"},{"id":"d","text":"SQL Server converts 0/0 to NULL silently"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q23","text":"A developer normalises a customer address into a separate Addresses table linked by CustomerId. Later, a report queries customer name and address for 100k customers requiring a JOIN every time. A colleague suggests denormalising. What is the main trade-off?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Denormalisation increases the number of tables and JOINs required"},{"id":"b","text":"Denormalisation improves read performance by eliminating the JOIN but risks data anomalies — updating a customer's address must be done in multiple places or inconsistencies arise"},{"id":"c","text":"Normalisation always performs better than denormalisation for reads"},{"id":"d","text":"Denormalisation violates SQL Server constraints and cannot be implemented"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q24","text":"A developer needs to find rows in a Products table where the Description column contains the word 'organic' regardless of case. Which approach is efficient on a case-insensitive collation database?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"WHERE LOWER(Description) = 'organic'"},{"id":"b","text":"WHERE Description LIKE '%organic%'"},{"id":"c","text":"WHERE CHARINDEX('organic', Description) > 0"},{"id":"d","text":"Both b and c achieve the same result on a case-insensitive collation; neither enables an index seek since the leading wildcard in LIKE prevents it"}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q25","text":"A developer writes a query that returns product sales grouped by year. They use GROUP BY YEAR(SaleDate), ProductId. A colleague points out the query could be written with GROUP BY SaleYear, ProductId where SaleYear is a calculated column. What is the issue with using an alias in GROUP BY in SQL Server?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SQL Server allows column aliases in GROUP BY without restriction"},{"id":"b","text":"SQL Server does not allow SELECT aliases to be used in GROUP BY; the expression YEAR(SaleDate) must be repeated in GROUP BY"},{"id":"c","text":"Aliases can be used in GROUP BY only if defined in a subquery"},{"id":"d","text":"Aliases in GROUP BY are supported only in SQL Server 2022"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q26","text":"A developer encounters a deadlock and the SQL Server deadlock graph shows two SPIDs waiting on each other. The DBA recommends adding a READPAST hint. What does READPAST do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"READPAST reads uncommitted data to avoid waiting for locks"},{"id":"b","text":"READPAST skips locked rows rather than waiting for the lock to be released, which can reduce deadlocks but means some rows may be temporarily invisible to the reader"},{"id":"c","text":"READPAST upgrades the query to SERIALIZABLE isolation"},{"id":"d","text":"READPAST forces SQL Server to choose a different execution plan"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q27","text":"A developer uses FIRST_VALUE(Revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY ProductId ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS FirstMonthRevenue. What does this return for each row?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The revenue from the row immediately before the current row"},{"id":"b","text":"The revenue from the first month in the ordered partition for the same product, repeated on every row for that product"},{"id":"c","text":"The minimum revenue within the product partition"},{"id":"d","text":"NULL for all rows except the first row of each partition"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q28","text":"A developer designs a table for a multi-tenant SaaS app where each tenant's data must be isolated. They add a TenantId column with a foreign key to a Tenants table and ensure all queries include WHERE TenantId = @TenantId. What additional database-level safety layer could enforce this at the row level automatically?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A CHECK constraint on TenantId > 0"},{"id":"b","text":"Row-Level Security (RLS) using a security policy and predicate function that filters rows based on the current session's tenant context"},{"id":"c","text":"A UNIQUE constraint on (TenantId, UserId)"},{"id":"d","text":"Separate databases per tenant"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q29","text":"A developer uses INTERSECT to compare two sets of CustomerIds — one from an email campaign list and one from the purchases table. What does the result contain?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"All CustomerIds from both lists combined with duplicates removed"},{"id":"b","text":"CustomerIds that appear in both lists — customers who were on the email list AND made a purchase"},{"id":"c","text":"CustomerIds that appear in the email list but not in the purchases table"},{"id":"d","text":"CustomerIds unique to each list, excluding those appearing in both"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d03_q30","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that reads a large dataset and processes rows in a CURSOR loop, issuing an UPDATE per row. With 50,000 rows this takes 4 minutes. What is the primary reason and how should it be rewritten?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CURSORs are not supported inside stored procedures"},{"id":"b","text":"A CURSOR issues one round-trip per row, accumulating transaction and lock overhead for each row; replacing the cursor with a single set-based UPDATE statement processes all rows in one operation and is typically orders of magnitude faster"},{"id":"c","text":"The procedure should use BULK INSERT instead"},{"id":"d","text":"The CURSOR should be changed from FAST_FORWARD to STATIC to improve speed"}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: sql-dev-04.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_sql_d04', $${"id":"qp_sql_d04","title":"SQL Developer — Paper 4","description":"Developer-focused SQL scenarios covering query writing, joins, filtering, aggregations, CTEs, indexes, transactions, window functions, stored procedures, and data safety.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q1","text":"A developer writes a query that joins Customers, Orders, OrderItems, Products, and Inventory. The result is used to populate a dashboard. The query takes 8 seconds. A senior developer recommends splitting it into two queries. Which query-writing principle supports this advice?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SQL Server limits the number of JOINs to four per query"},{"id":"b","text":"Complex multi-join queries produce larger intermediate result sets; breaking into smaller, targeted queries each returning only the columns needed can improve plan quality and reduce total data movement"},{"id":"c","text":"Two queries always run faster than one because they use parallel threads"},{"id":"d","text":"Dashboard queries must always use stored procedures, not inline SQL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q2","text":"A developer writes: SELECT o.OrderId, COUNT(oi.ItemId) AS ItemCount FROM Orders o LEFT JOIN OrderItems oi ON o.OrderId = oi.OrderId GROUP BY o.OrderId. For an order with no items, what does COUNT(oi.ItemId) return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"NULL"},{"id":"b","text":"1, because the LEFT JOIN produces one row for the order even with no items"},{"id":"c","text":"0, because COUNT(column) excludes NULL values, so the NULL from the unmatched LEFT JOIN is not counted"},{"id":"d","text":"An error because COUNT cannot be used on a LEFT JOIN result"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q3","text":"A developer writes: WHERE Region IN ('North', 'South') AND Revenue > 5000 OR Category = 'Premium'. A bug is filed that Premium products from all regions are included. What is the fix?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Replace OR with AND NOT"},{"id":"b","text":"Add parentheses: WHERE (Region IN ('North', 'South') AND Revenue > 5000) OR (Category = 'Premium' AND Region IN ('North', 'South')) to restrict premium to the desired regions as well"},{"id":"c","text":"Add parentheses: WHERE Region IN ('North', 'South') AND (Revenue > 5000 OR Category = 'Premium')"},{"id":"d","text":"Both b and c could be correct depending on the intended business logic; the developer must clarify the requirement before changing the parentheses"}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q4","text":"A developer uses PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY TestScore DESC) AS PctRank to show where each student stands. A student has PERCENT_RANK = 0. What does this mean?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The student scored 0 on the test"},{"id":"b","text":"The student has the highest score — PERCENT_RANK is 0 for the first row and 1 for the last row in the ordered set"},{"id":"c","text":"PERCENT_RANK = 0 means the student is at the 50th percentile"},{"id":"d","text":"It means the student's score could not be ranked due to NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q5","text":"A stored procedure accepts @SearchTerm NVARCHAR(100) and runs: SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ProductName LIKE @SearchTerm. A developer passes '%' + @SearchTerm + '%' from the application layer. Is there a SQL injection risk?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Yes, because LIKE with parameters always allows injection"},{"id":"b","text":"No — the value is passed as a parameter, not concatenated into the SQL string; the user input is treated as a data value, not executable SQL, so injection is not possible through the parameter itself"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes, because % characters in the parameter bypass parameterisation"},{"id":"d","text":"Yes, because LIKE patterns are parsed by the SQL engine and can inject commands"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q6","text":"A developer reviews an execution plan and sees a Clustered Index Scan on a 10M-row table despite a WHERE clause that filters on CustomerId. The CustomerId column is the primary key. What is likely causing the scan instead of a seek?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Clustered index scans are always used for primary key lookups"},{"id":"b","text":"The WHERE clause likely uses an expression or implicit conversion on CustomerId — for example, WHERE CustomerId = '12345' when CustomerId is INT causes an implicit cast that prevents a seek"},{"id":"c","text":"Primary key clustered indexes cannot be used for seeks in SQL Server"},{"id":"d","text":"The table statistics are up to date so SQL Server chose a scan intentionally"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q7","text":"A developer writes a transaction that updates a customer balance and then inserts an audit log entry. The procedure uses TRY...CATCH with ROLLBACK in the CATCH block. What should be checked before issuing ROLLBACK in the CATCH block to avoid an error when no transaction is active?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Check @@ERROR > 0 before rolling back"},{"id":"b","text":"Check @@TRANCOUNT > 0 before issuing ROLLBACK; if @@TRANCOUNT is 0, there is no active transaction and calling ROLLBACK would raise an error"},{"id":"c","text":"Check XACT_STATE() = 1 and only rollback if exactly equal to 1"},{"id":"d","text":"Check SET XACT_ABORT is ON before rolling back"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q8","text":"A developer groups sales data by SalesRepId and Year, and a manager asks why some reps appear with NULL in the Year column in the GROUP BY output. The query uses: GROUP BY SalesRepId, YEAR(SaleDate). What is the cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"YEAR() returns NULL when SaleDate is NULL because applying any function to NULL returns NULL, and these NULL groups appear as a separate group"},{"id":"b","text":"GROUP BY ignores NULL values and they are dropped from results"},{"id":"c","text":"NULL in SaleDate causes a GROUP BY error in SQL Server"},{"id":"d","text":"YEAR() converts NULL to 0, not NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q9","text":"A developer uses EXCEPT to find customer IDs in Table A that are not in Table B. Table B has a column with some NULL CustomerIds. Will the EXCEPT correctly exclude rows where CustomerIds in A match non-NULL rows in B?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"No, EXCEPT cannot handle NULL values and returns an error"},{"id":"b","text":"Yes — EXCEPT treats NULL as equal to NULL for comparison purposes (unlike standard equality), so it correctly identifies rows absent from B, and NULL CustomerIds in B match NULL CustomerIds in A"},{"id":"c","text":"EXCEPT ignores NULL rows in both tables"},{"id":"d","text":"EXCEPT and NOT IN behave identically with NULLs"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q10","text":"A developer drops and recreates an index every night as part of a maintenance job, claiming this refreshes the statistics. A DBA objects. What is the correct approach and why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Dropping and recreating indexes is the only way to update statistics"},{"id":"b","text":"Dropping and recreating indexes is unnecessary and risks data availability; the correct approach is to use ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD or REORGANIZE for fragmentation, and UPDATE STATISTICS for statistics — these operations are less disruptive"},{"id":"c","text":"Index statistics update automatically after every INSERT and never need manual updates"},{"id":"d","text":"Dropping indexes before nightly batch jobs and recreating them afterwards is a recommended best practice"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q11","text":"A developer applies CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY Salary) to an employee list. An employee with salary 50000 gets a value of 0.6. What does this mean?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"60% of employees have a higher salary than this employee"},{"id":"b","text":"60% of employees have a salary less than or equal to this employee's salary of 50000"},{"id":"c","text":"This employee earns 60% of the maximum salary"},{"id":"d","text":"The employee is in the 40th percentile"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q12","text":"A developer needs to soft-delete a record by setting DeletedAt = GETUTCDATE() rather than removing it. Later, a report accidentally includes these soft-deleted rows. What is the root cause and correct preventive measure?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Soft delete should use a BIT column, not a DATETIME column"},{"id":"b","text":"The report query lacks a WHERE DeletedAt IS NULL filter; this can be systematically prevented by creating filtered views that always exclude soft-deleted rows and having all queries use the view"},{"id":"c","text":"Soft delete is incompatible with reporting queries"},{"id":"d","text":"The report should use a LEFT JOIN to the deleted records table instead"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q13","text":"A developer writes: SELECT p.ProductId, p.Name, s.SupplierName FROM Products p FULL OUTER JOIN Suppliers s ON p.SupplierId = s.SupplierId. What rows appear in the result that would NOT appear in an INNER JOIN?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Rows where both sides have matching values"},{"id":"b","text":"Products with no supplier (SupplierId is NULL or unmatched) AND suppliers with no products — unmatched rows from both sides, with NULLs for the missing side's columns"},{"id":"c","text":"Only suppliers with no matching products"},{"id":"d","text":"FULL OUTER JOIN produces the same result as INNER JOIN plus an extra NULL row"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q14","text":"A developer wants to return the total order count and the count of orders over $1000 in a single query, without using subqueries or CTEs. Which technique achieves this efficiently?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Use two separate GROUP BY queries joined with UNION ALL"},{"id":"b","text":"Use conditional aggregation: SELECT COUNT(*) AS TotalOrders, COUNT(CASE WHEN Amount > 1000 THEN 1 END) AS HighValueOrders FROM Orders"},{"id":"c","text":"Use PIVOT to transform the order amounts into columns"},{"id":"d","text":"Use a CROSS JOIN to the same table filtered differently"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q15","text":"A developer uses REPEATABLE READ isolation for a stock reservation transaction. Between two reads of the same row, another session tries to INSERT a new row for the same product in a related table. Can the new INSERT succeed?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"No — REPEATABLE READ blocks all INSERTs from other sessions"},{"id":"b","text":"Yes — REPEATABLE READ only prevents modification of rows already read; it does not prevent new rows from being inserted, which means phantom reads are still possible at this level"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes — REPEATABLE READ allows all concurrent operations without restriction"},{"id":"d","text":"No — INSERTs are blocked by shared locks held under REPEATABLE READ"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q16","text":"A developer stores international phone numbers in a column defined as INT. A phone number starting with +44 is rejected by the application. What is the data type issue and correct fix?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"INT supports + prefix when ANSI_PADDING is enabled"},{"id":"b","text":"INT cannot store leading zeros, plus signs, or country code prefixes; phone numbers should be stored as VARCHAR(20) or NVARCHAR(20) to preserve formatting and international formats"},{"id":"c","text":"Use BIGINT to support longer phone numbers with country codes"},{"id":"d","text":"Store phone numbers as DECIMAL(15,0) to allow numeric-only international numbers"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q17","text":"A developer is designing a stored procedure that accepts a list of OrderIds to process. SQL Server does not natively support array parameters. What is the recommended pattern to pass multiple values to a stored procedure?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Pass values as a comma-separated NVARCHAR and split with STRING_SPLIT() inside the procedure, or use a User-Defined Table Type parameter (table-valued parameter)"},{"id":"b","text":"Create a separate stored procedure for each OrderId and call them in a loop from the application"},{"id":"c","text":"Use a global temp table ##Orders to pass data between the application and procedure"},{"id":"d","text":"Encode values in JSON and parse with OPENJSON inside the procedure"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q18","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that calculates year-end bonuses and updates an Employees table. The procedure is called by a scheduled job. A bug causes some employees to receive double updates. Which coding pattern would make the procedure idempotent (safe to run multiple times)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Add a TRY...CATCH to ignore duplicate updates"},{"id":"b","text":"Add a condition to only update employees where BonusAppliedYear <> YEAR(GETDATE()), preventing the same year's bonus being applied again"},{"id":"c","text":"Use a UNIQUE constraint on EmployeeId"},{"id":"d","text":"Use SET NOCOUNT ON at the top of the procedure"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q19","text":"A developer needs to calculate a 3-month moving average of sales. Which window frame clause achieves this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"AVG(Sales) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)"},{"id":"b","text":"AVG(Sales) OVER (PARTITION BY SaleMonth)"},{"id":"c","text":"AVG(Sales) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '3' MONTH PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)"},{"id":"d","text":"AVG(Sales) OVER (ORDER BY SaleMonth ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q20","text":"A developer creates a filtered index: CREATE INDEX IX_ActiveUsers ON Users(LastLogin) WHERE IsActive = 1. Which query will benefit most from this index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE IsActive = 0 ORDER BY LastLogin"},{"id":"b","text":"SELECT UserId, LastLogin FROM Users WHERE IsActive = 1 AND LastLogin > '2025-01-01'"},{"id":"c","text":"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Users"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT * FROM Users WHERE LastLogin IS NOT NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q21","text":"A developer writes a query using NOT IN with a subquery: WHERE ProductId NOT IN (SELECT ProductId FROM Discontinued). The Discontinued table has one row where ProductId is NULL. What unexpected result occurs?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query returns all products because NULL means the subquery is empty"},{"id":"b","text":"The query returns no rows because NOT IN with a NULL in the subquery causes every comparison to return UNKNOWN, making the WHERE condition UNKNOWN for all rows"},{"id":"c","text":"The NULL row is ignored and NOT IN works correctly for non-NULL values"},{"id":"d","text":"SQL Server raises an error when NOT IN encounters a NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q22","text":"A developer writes a CTE that builds a date series using a recursive query. The CTE generates one row per day for a year (365 iterations). By default, SQL Server limits recursion to how many levels, and how is this changed?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Default limit is 100 levels; it can be increased with OPTION (MAXRECURSION n) where n can be up to 32767 or 0 for unlimited"},{"id":"b","text":"Default limit is 1000 levels; it cannot be changed"},{"id":"c","text":"Default limit is 32767 levels; lower values improve performance"},{"id":"d","text":"There is no default recursion limit in SQL Server CTEs"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q23","text":"A developer adds a UNIQUE constraint to the Email column in a Users table that already has data. The constraint creation fails. What is the most likely cause and resolution?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"UNIQUE constraints cannot be applied to columns that already contain data"},{"id":"b","text":"The existing data contains duplicate email values or NULLs in multiple rows (SQL Server allows one NULL in a UNIQUE constraint, but multiple NULLs violate it); duplicates must be resolved before the constraint can be added"},{"id":"c","text":"UNIQUE constraints require the column to be a primary key first"},{"id":"d","text":"The Email column must be NOT NULL before a UNIQUE constraint can be applied"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q24","text":"A developer needs to return products along with their category name, where some products have no category (NULL CategoryId). The requirement says uncategorised products must appear in results with 'Uncategorised' shown in the CategoryName column. Which JOIN and expression achieves this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"INNER JOIN Categories c ON p.CategoryId = c.CategoryId; SELECT ISNULL(c.CategoryName, 'Uncategorised')"},{"id":"b","text":"LEFT JOIN Categories c ON p.CategoryId = c.CategoryId; SELECT COALESCE(c.CategoryName, 'Uncategorised') AS CategoryName"},{"id":"c","text":"RIGHT JOIN Categories c ON p.CategoryId = c.CategoryId; SELECT c.CategoryName"},{"id":"d","text":"CROSS JOIN Categories c; SELECT c.CategoryName"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q25","text":"A developer adds GROUP BY ProductId, CategoryId, SubcategoryId, SupplierName to a reporting query but the product manager complains that rows are not being aggregated at the product level as expected. What is the problem?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"GROUP BY with more than 3 columns is not supported"},{"id":"b","text":"If SupplierName varies per product (e.g., a product can have multiple suppliers), adding SupplierName to GROUP BY creates a separate group per supplier, preventing aggregation at the product level as intended"},{"id":"c","text":"CategoryId and SubcategoryId should not be grouped together"},{"id":"d","text":"GROUP BY on VARCHAR columns like SupplierName is case-sensitive and creates duplicate groups"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q26","text":"A developer receives a report that a transaction involving a bank transfer was partially committed — the debit happened but the credit did not. The code uses TRY...CATCH. On investigation, they find XACT_ABORT was OFF and the CATCH block contained only a PRINT statement. What should the CATCH block have done?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The CATCH block should have used RETURN 0 to exit the procedure"},{"id":"b","text":"The CATCH block must issue ROLLBACK TRANSACTION (or check XACT_STATE) to undo the debit when the credit fails; a PRINT statement does not undo any changes"},{"id":"c","text":"The CATCH block should have retried the credit operation"},{"id":"d","text":"SET XACT_ABORT ON would have prevented the CATCH block from executing"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q27","text":"A developer uses LAG(Revenue, 2) OVER (ORDER BY QuarterDate) to get the revenue from two periods ago. For the second row in the result set, what value does LAG return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The revenue from the first row"},{"id":"b","text":"NULL, because there is no row two positions before the second row"},{"id":"c","text":"0, the default for missing LAG values"},{"id":"d","text":"The same value as the current row"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q28","text":"A developer creates a table with an IDENTITY column as the primary key and also needs a business key (OrderNumber VARCHAR(20)) that must be unique. Which combination of constraints is most appropriate?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"PRIMARY KEY on OrderId (identity); no constraint on OrderNumber — enforce uniqueness in application code"},{"id":"b","text":"PRIMARY KEY on OrderId (identity) and a UNIQUE constraint on OrderNumber — this enforces both surrogate and business key integrity at the database level"},{"id":"c","text":"PRIMARY KEY on both OrderId and OrderNumber as a composite key"},{"id":"d","text":"UNIQUE constraint on OrderId; PRIMARY KEY on OrderNumber"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q29","text":"A developer uses CROSS APPLY to call a table-valued function for each row in a Customers query. A colleague asks why OUTER APPLY wasn't used. What is the difference?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"OUTER APPLY and CROSS APPLY are identical in SQL Server"},{"id":"b","text":"CROSS APPLY excludes customer rows where the TVF returns no rows (like an INNER JOIN); OUTER APPLY keeps customer rows even when the TVF returns no rows, placing NULLs in the TVF columns (like a LEFT JOIN)"},{"id":"c","text":"OUTER APPLY returns the inverse of CROSS APPLY — only rows where the TVF returns nothing"},{"id":"d","text":"CROSS APPLY is for correlated subqueries; OUTER APPLY is for uncorrelated ones"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d04_q30","text":"A developer needs to write a query that gives each product a rank by revenue within each category, but wants no gaps in ranks when there are ties. Which window function is correct?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY CategoryId ORDER BY Revenue DESC)"},{"id":"b","text":"ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY CategoryId ORDER BY Revenue DESC)"},{"id":"c","text":"DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY CategoryId ORDER BY Revenue DESC)"},{"id":"d","text":"NTILE(1) OVER (PARTITION BY CategoryId ORDER BY Revenue DESC)"}],"correctOptionId":"c"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: sql-dev-05.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_sql_d05', $${"id":"qp_sql_d05","title":"SQL Developer — Paper 5","description":"Developer-focused SQL scenarios covering query writing, joins, filtering, aggregations, CTEs, indexes, transactions, window functions, stored procedures, and data safety.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q1","text":"A developer writes a query that applies several scalar UDFs in the SELECT list across 2 million rows. The query runs for 10 minutes. A senior developer says the UDFs are the bottleneck. Why do scalar UDFs typically cause performance problems when applied to large result sets?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Scalar UDFs are deprecated in modern SQL Server versions"},{"id":"b","text":"Scalar UDFs execute row-by-row, preventing batch execution and parallelism; in SQL Server versions prior to 2019, they also prevent the query from going parallel and hide their cost from the execution plan"},{"id":"c","text":"Scalar UDFs hold exclusive locks on tables they reference"},{"id":"d","text":"Scalar UDFs always perform a full table scan on the tables they access"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q2","text":"A developer queries: SELECT CategoryId, AVG(Price) AS AvgPrice FROM Products GROUP BY CategoryId HAVING COUNT(*) > 5. For a category with 3 products, will its average price appear in results?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Yes, HAVING filters after AVG is calculated so the average is always shown"},{"id":"b","text":"No, the HAVING clause filters out any category group that does not have more than 5 products; this category with 3 products is excluded entirely"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes, but AvgPrice will be NULL for categories with fewer than 5 products"},{"id":"d","text":"No, but only if WHERE COUNT(*) > 5 is also added"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q3","text":"A developer writes: WHERE Discount IS NOT NULL AND Discount != 0 AND TaxCode IN ('A', 'B') OR VipFlag = 1. A product manager reports VIP customers are getting results even when discount and tax conditions fail. What is the issue?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"IS NOT NULL cannot be combined with != in the same expression"},{"id":"b","text":"Due to AND having higher precedence than OR, the condition is: (Discount IS NOT NULL AND Discount != 0 AND TaxCode IN ('A','B')) OR VipFlag = 1 — VIP rows bypass all other conditions; parentheses must wrap the entire filter correctly per business intent"},{"id":"c","text":"IN cannot be combined with AND in WHERE clauses"},{"id":"d","text":"OR always takes precedence over AND in SQL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q4","text":"A developer writes a query using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId ORDER BY HireDate ASC) AS RowNum to find the longest-serving employee per department. The WHERE clause filters RowNum = 1. Two employees in the same department have the same HireDate. What happens?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Both employees are assigned RowNum = 1 and both are returned"},{"id":"b","text":"ROW_NUMBER() assigns unique sequential numbers even for ties, so only one of the tied employees gets RowNum = 1 — which one is non-deterministic without a tiebreaker in the ORDER BY"},{"id":"c","text":"An error is raised for tied ROW_NUMBER values"},{"id":"d","text":"Both employees get RowNum = 1 and RowNum = 2, both are filtered out"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q5","text":"A developer discovers a stored procedure in production that builds SQL using: SET @SQL = 'SELECT * FROM ' + @TableName + ' WHERE Status = ' + @Status. A security review flags this. Which refactoring best mitigates both SQL injection risks in this statement?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Use QUOTENAME(@TableName) to safely delimit the table name and pass @Status as a parameter via sp_executesql rather than concatenation"},{"id":"b","text":"Validate that @TableName exists in sys.tables using IF EXISTS before running"},{"id":"c","text":"Convert the SP to use an ORM layer"},{"id":"d","text":"Add EXECUTE AS OWNER to the stored procedure"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q6","text":"A developer creates a composite index on (AccountId, TransactionDate, Amount) to support a query that filters on AccountId and TransactionDate only. A DBA suggests the Amount column is unnecessary in the key. Why?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Indexes cannot contain more than 2 key columns"},{"id":"b","text":"If Amount is not used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY but only in SELECT, it should be placed in the INCLUDE clause rather than the key; key columns increase index size and sort order, while INCLUDE columns add data at the leaf level with less overhead"},{"id":"c","text":"Amount columns cannot be part of non-clustered indexes"},{"id":"d","text":"Adding Amount to the key improves performance for the filter on AccountId"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q7","text":"A developer opens a transaction, inserts a record, then another developer runs a SELECT on the same table under READ COMMITTED isolation. The first transaction has not committed yet. What does the second developer's SELECT see?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The uncommitted row inserted by the first transaction (dirty read)"},{"id":"b","text":"Only committed rows; the uncommitted insert is not visible because READ COMMITTED does not permit dirty reads"},{"id":"c","text":"A deadlock error because both sessions access the same table"},{"id":"d","text":"An empty result set until the first transaction commits"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q8","text":"A developer writes: SELECT SupplierId, SUM(OrderAmount) AS Total FROM PurchaseOrders WHERE SupplierId IS NOT NULL GROUP BY SupplierId HAVING SUM(OrderAmount) > 50000. A colleague asks whether the WHERE or HAVING clause executes first. What is the correct SQL logical processing order?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"HAVING executes first to reduce rows before grouping"},{"id":"b","text":"WHERE executes before grouping, filtering individual rows; GROUP BY then aggregates; HAVING filters the groups after aggregation — so WHERE reduces the dataset before aggregation, making it more efficient than HAVING for row-level filters"},{"id":"c","text":"WHERE and HAVING execute simultaneously in parallel"},{"id":"d","text":"ORDER BY executes before HAVING in SQL Server's processing order"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q9","text":"A developer needs to find orders placed in 2024 that have no corresponding shipment record in a Shipments table. They write: SELECT o.OrderId FROM Orders o WHERE YEAR(o.OrderDate) = 2024 AND o.OrderId NOT IN (SELECT OrderId FROM Shipments). A QA engineer says some orders are wrongly excluded. What is likely the cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"YEAR() cannot be used in WHERE with subqueries"},{"id":"b","text":"The Shipments table may contain rows where OrderId IS NULL; NOT IN returns UNKNOWN for all comparisons when any subquery value is NULL, causing all rows to be excluded"},{"id":"c","text":"NOT IN cannot reference a table different from the outer query"},{"id":"d","text":"YEAR(o.OrderDate) = 2024 conflicts with the NOT IN subquery logic"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q10","text":"A developer runs a query and the execution plan shows an estimated 1 row but the actual row count is 500,000. What is the most likely root cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query uses a non-clustered index"},{"id":"b","text":"The table statistics are out of date or missing, causing the optimiser to grossly underestimate row counts and choose a suboptimal plan"},{"id":"c","text":"The developer has not run DBCC CHECKDB recently"},{"id":"d","text":"The query uses NOLOCK which disables statistics"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q11","text":"A developer uses NTH_VALUE(Salary, 3) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId ORDER BY Salary DESC) to find the third-highest salary per department. For a department with only 2 employees, what does NTH_VALUE return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The salary of the second employee (the last available)"},{"id":"b","text":"NULL, because there is no third row in that partition"},{"id":"c","text":"0"},{"id":"d","text":"An error is raised when NTH_VALUE offset exceeds partition size"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q12","text":"A developer needs to delete all orders older than 5 years from a table with 8M rows, foreign-keyed to an OrderItems table.
