What is the safest sequence of operations?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"DELETE FROM Orders WHERE OrderDate < DATEADD(YEAR, -5, GETDATE()) — FK cascade will handle child rows automatically"},{"id":"b","text":"First delete child rows from OrderItems matching the old orders, then delete the old Orders rows — in batches if the dataset is large — to respect FK constraints and avoid large transaction log growth"},{"id":"c","text":"TRUNCATE both tables and re-import recent data"},{"id":"d","text":"Disable the FK constraint, delete from Orders, then re-enable the FK"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q13","text":"A developer writes a query joining four tables. The execution plan shows a Hash Join between two large tables instead of a Nested Loops join. Under what circumstance does SQL Server typically choose a Hash Join?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"When both input sets are small and indexed"},{"id":"b","text":"When one or both inputs are large and there is no useful index on the join columns; Hash Join builds an in-memory hash table from the smaller input and probes it with the larger — it scales well for large unindexed datasets"},{"id":"c","text":"When the ON clause uses inequality operators like > or <"},{"id":"d","text":"Hash Join is only used for self-joins"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q14","text":"A developer needs to retrieve each employee's salary along with the minimum and maximum salary in their department without using a subquery or JOIN. Which approach achieves this in a single SELECT?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Use GROUP BY DeptId and aggregate MIN/MAX in the same query"},{"id":"b","text":"Use window functions: MIN(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId) AS DeptMin, MAX(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId) AS DeptMax alongside the regular SELECT columns"},{"id":"c","text":"Use a PIVOT to transform salary rows into min/max columns"},{"id":"d","text":"Use CROSS JOIN to a department aggregate table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q15","text":"Two concurrent transactions T1 and T2 both run under READ COMMITTED. T1 reads an account balance and decides to withdraw if balance > 100. T2 reads the same balance and makes the same decision. Both commit, causing the balance to go negative. What concurrency problem is this?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Dirty read"},{"id":"b","text":"Lost update / write skew — both transactions read the same value, make a decision independently, and update without awareness of the other's update, leading to inconsistent state"},{"id":"c","text":"Phantom read"},{"id":"d","text":"Cascading abort"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q16","text":"A developer stores product weights as REAL (single-precision float). When summing 10,000 product weights in a report, the total is off by a small amount each time. What is the cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"REAL columns round values on INSERT to 2 decimal places"},{"id":"b","text":"REAL is an approximate numeric type that cannot represent all decimal fractions exactly in binary floating point; rounding errors accumulate across many additions, producing imprecise totals"},{"id":"c","text":"SUM() is incompatible with REAL data types"},{"id":"d","text":"The column should be FLOAT(53) which has no rounding errors"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q17","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that is called by a web API for every HTTP request. Each call runs the same parameterised SELECT query. After a deployment, query performance degrades for one specific parameter value despite fast execution for all others. What SQL Server phenomenon is most likely responsible?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The stored procedure is being recompiled on every execution"},{"id":"b","text":"Parameter sniffing — SQL Server compiled and cached the execution plan based on the first parameter value seen, which may be atypical (e.g., a rare ID returning few rows); this plan performs poorly when a high-volume parameter is used later"},{"id":"c","text":"The web API is passing NULL for the parameter on degraded calls"},{"id":"d","text":"The procedure lacks a primary key lookup"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q18","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that calls another stored procedure (nested SP). The inner SP has its own BEGIN/COMMIT TRANSACTION block. The outer SP also has BEGIN/COMMIT TRANSACTION. How does SQL Server handle nested transactions?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Each COMMIT in the inner SP fully commits the inner transaction independently of the outer"},{"id":"b","text":"SQL Server increments @@TRANCOUNT with each BEGIN TRANSACTION; inner COMMITs decrement @@TRANCOUNT but do not physically commit — only the outermost COMMIT (when @@TRANCOUNT reaches 1) physically commits; a ROLLBACK at any level rolls back everything"},{"id":"c","text":"Nested transactions are not supported in SQL Server stored procedures"},{"id":"d","text":"The inner transaction is treated as a savepoint automatically"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q19","text":"A developer writes: SELECT DeptId, EmpName, Salary, MAX(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId) AS DeptMax, Salary - MAX(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptId) AS DiffFromMax FROM Employees. What does the DiffFromMax column represent for each row?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The salary difference between the current employee and the department average"},{"id":"b","text":"How much below the highest salary in the department the current employee's salary is — a negative value for all non-top earners, 0 for the top earner(s)"},{"id":"c","text":"The total payroll minus the current employee's salary"},{"id":"d","text":"The salary gap between adjacent employees when sorted by salary"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q20","text":"A developer queries a large EventLog table (100M rows) frequently with: WHERE EventType = 'Error' AND EventDate >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, GETDATE()). An index on (EventDate) exists but the query is still slow. What would significantly improve this specific query?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Add a second index on (EventType) so both indexes can be merged"},{"id":"b","text":"Create a composite index on (EventType, EventDate) — this allows the index to seek on EventType first, then range-scan on EventDate within that partition, dramatically reducing rows examined"},{"id":"c","text":"Partition the table by EventType"},{"id":"d","text":"Convert the non-clustered index on EventDate to a clustered index"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q21","text":"A developer writes a WHERE clause: WHERE LastName LIKE 'Sm%'. Will a non-clustered index on LastName be used for this query?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"No, LIKE never uses indexes"},{"id":"b","text":"Yes — a leading character pattern (no leading wildcard) is sargable; the index can seek to the range of values starting with 'Sm' without scanning the entire index"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes, but only if LastName is also the clustered index key"},{"id":"d","text":"No, string columns require a full-text index for LIKE queries"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q22","text":"A developer writes a CTE that calculates department headcount and then uses it in a DELETE statement to remove departments with zero employees. A colleague warns that CTEs in DML statements have a caveat. What is it?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"CTEs cannot be used in DELETE statements in SQL Server"},{"id":"b","text":"The CTE is re-evaluated during the DML execution and is not materialised; if the underlying data changes between the CTE definition and the DML operation (e.g., in a concurrent system), the DELETE may affect different rows than anticipated"},{"id":"c","text":"CTEs used in DELETE require the WITH SCHEMABINDING clause"},{"id":"d","text":"The CTE must be placed inside the DELETE statement's FROM clause using a subquery instead"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q23","text":"A developer designs an Orders table and debates between storing CustomerName as a column in Orders vs using a CustomerId foreign key linked to a Customers table. A year later, a customer changes their name. What is the consequence of the denormalised approach (CustomerName stored directly in Orders)?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Orders cannot be queried without joining to Customers"},{"id":"b","text":"Historical order records will show the old name unless every order row is updated; the normalised approach stores the name once in Customers, so updating it automatically reflects in all order queries via the FK relationship"},{"id":"c","text":"The denormalised approach is always preferred for historical data integrity"},{"id":"d","text":"Storing CustomerName in Orders violates the PRIMARY KEY constraint"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q24","text":"A developer needs to combine results from a Customers query and a Suppliers query where both return Id and Name. They use UNION. After running, they notice fewer rows than expected. What is the most likely reason?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"UNION requires matching data types which caused some rows to be cast to NULL"},{"id":"b","text":"UNION removes duplicate rows — if a customer and a supplier share the same Id and Name values, UNION deduplicates them; UNION ALL should be used to retain all rows regardless of duplicates"},{"id":"c","text":"UNION cannot combine more than 100 rows from each table"},{"id":"d","text":"UNION applies a TOP filter automatically when no ORDER BY is specified"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q25","text":"A developer needs to report on products grouped by year and category, with subtotals per year and a grand total. Which GROUP BY extension enables subtotals and grand totals in a single query?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"GROUP BY CUBE(Year, Category) — produces all possible combinations of subtotals including per-year, per-category, and grand total"},{"id":"b","text":"GROUP BY ROLLUP(Year, Category) — produces subtotals hierarchically: detail by Year+Category, subtotals by Year, and a grand total; GROUPING() can identify which rows are subtotals"},{"id":"c","text":"GROUP BY GROUPING SETS — must list every grouping set manually to achieve the same result as ROLLUP"},{"id":"d","text":"All three options (CUBE, ROLLUP, GROUPING SETS) produce identical output for two columns"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q26","text":"A developer sets transaction isolation to SERIALIZABLE for a batch job that reads and then updates account balances. The batch runs for 20 minutes and user complaints about the application being unresponsive increase. What is the likely cause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SERIALIZABLE automatically cancels other transactions after 5 minutes"},{"id":"b","text":"SERIALIZABLE holds range locks for the entire batch duration, blocking concurrent inserts, updates, and reads on the affected tables; this eliminates phantom reads but severely limits concurrent access"},{"id":"c","text":"SERIALIZABLE requires the database to be in single-user mode"},{"id":"d","text":"SERIALIZABLE prevents the batch from using parallelism, slowing it down"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q27","text":"A developer uses: SELECT ProductId, SaleMonth, Revenue, Revenue - LAG(Revenue, 1, 0) OVER (PARTITION BY ProductId ORDER BY SaleMonth) AS MonthlyGrowth FROM Sales. For the first month of each product (where there is no prior month), what value does LAG return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"NULL, causing MonthlyGrowth to be NULL for the first row"},{"id":"b","text":"0, because the third argument to LAG() specifies the default value when no prior row exists; MonthlyGrowth equals Revenue - 0 = Revenue for the first month"},{"id":"c","text":"The revenue from the last row of the partition"},{"id":"d","text":"An error because LAG cannot have a default value"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q28","text":"A developer needs to store a product's status that can only ever be one of: 'Draft', 'Active', 'Discontinued', 'Archived'. Which database-level mechanism enforces this constraint most directly?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A FOREIGN KEY referencing a lookup table of valid statuses — this allows the list to be extended without schema changes and is the most maintainable approach"},{"id":"b","text":"A CHECK constraint: CHECK (Status IN ('Draft', 'Active', 'Discontinued', 'Archived'))"},{"id":"c","text":"A UNIQUE constraint on the Status column"},{"id":"d","text":"Both a and b are valid; a FK to a lookup table is more flexible, while a CHECK constraint is simpler for static lists — the best choice depends on whether the list needs to be user-configurable"}],"correctOptionId":"d"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q29","text":"A developer writes a stored procedure that accepts an @OrderStatus NVARCHAR(20) parameter and uses it in a WHERE clause via sp_executesql parameterisation. However, the query plan changes dramatically based on the value passed. The DBA suggests adding OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN). What does this hint do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It forces SQL Server to recompile the query on every execution"},{"id":"b","text":"It instructs SQL Server to compile the plan using average statistical estimates rather than the specific sniffed parameter value, producing a more stable plan that avoids extreme choices made for atypical first-run values"},{"id":"c","text":"It disables parameterisation and treats all values as literals"},{"id":"d","text":"It causes SQL Server to use a full table scan regardless of indexes"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_d05_q30","text":"A developer creates a view that joins Orders and Customers and exposes it to the reporting team. A report writer accidentally runs SELECT * FROM vw_OrderSummary WHERE 1=1 with no other filters, returning all 5M rows. Which view option would allow the DBA to prevent the view from ever returning more rows than the underlying indexed view's query predicate allows?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"WITH ENCRYPTION on the view definition"},{"id":"b","text":"WITH CHECK OPTION — this ensures that data written through the view satisfies the view's WHERE clause, but does not restrict reads; to restrict reads, Row-Level Security or application-level row limits are required"},{"id":"c","text":"WITH SCHEMABINDING — this prevents schema changes to underlying tables but does not filter rows"},{"id":"d","text":"There is no view option that restricts SELECT output; query filtering must be enforced through RLS policies, permission restrictions, or application-layer mandatory WHERE clauses"}],"correctOptionId":"d"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

-- paper: sql-indexes-performance.json
INSERT INTO papers (id, data) VALUES ('qp_sql_06', $${"id":"qp_sql_06","title":"SQL Indexes & Query Performance","description":"Index types, query performance optimization, execution plans, and index maintenance.","createdAt":"2026-02-26T09:00:00Z","timer":{"enabled":true,"durationSeconds":2700,"autoSubmit":true},"questions":[{"id":"qp_sql_06_q1","text":"What is the primary difference between a clustered and a non-clustered index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A clustered index sorts and stores the actual table data rows; a non-clustered index has a separate structure with pointers to the data rows"},{"id":"b","text":"A non-clustered index stores data rows; a clustered index stores pointers"},{"id":"c","text":"Clustered indexes are only on primary keys; non-clustered can be on any column"},{"id":"d","text":"There is no performance difference between them"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q2","text":"How many clustered indexes can a SQL Server table have?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Unlimited"},{"id":"b","text":"Up to 999"},{"id":"c","text":"Exactly 1"},{"id":"d","text":"2 — one for primary key and one user-defined"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q3","text":"What is a composite index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An index that combines two or more tables"},{"id":"b","text":"An index built on two or more columns"},{"id":"c","text":"An index on computed columns"},{"id":"d","text":"An index that covers all columns in a table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q4","text":"What is a covering index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An index that covers all tables in the database"},{"id":"b","text":"An index that contains all the columns needed to satisfy a query without accessing the base table"},{"id":"c","text":"An index on every column of a table"},{"id":"d","text":"A clustered index that includes non-key columns"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q5","text":"What are INCLUDE columns in a non-clustered index used for?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"To define the sort order of the index"},{"id":"b","text":"To add non-key columns to the leaf level of the index, making it a covering index without increasing the index key size"},{"id":"c","text":"To filter which rows are included in the index"},{"id":"d","text":"To include columns from other tables"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q6","text":"What is an index seek?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Reading every page of the index from start to finish"},{"id":"b","text":"Navigating directly to a specific portion of the index using the B-tree structure"},{"id":"c","text":"Searching the index using a table scan"},{"id":"d","text":"Looking for a missing index in the execution plan"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q7","text":"Which is generally more efficient: an index seek or an index scan?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Index scan is always faster"},{"id":"b","text":"Index seek is generally more efficient as it goes directly to matching rows"},{"id":"c","text":"They are always equally efficient"},{"id":"d","text":"Index scan is more efficient for large tables"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q8","text":"When can adding an index HURT performance?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Indexes always improve performance"},{"id":"b","text":"On INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations, indexes add overhead as they must be maintained"},{"id":"c","text":"Indexes only hurt performance on SELECT queries"},{"id":"d","text":"Only when the index is on a text column"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q9","text":"Which SQL Server command is used to view the execution plan of a query?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SHOW PLAN"},{"id":"b","text":"DISPLAY PLAN"},{"id":"c","text":"SET STATISTICS IO ON or using SSMS with 'Include Actual Execution Plan'"},{"id":"d","text":"EXPLAIN SELECT"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q10","text":"What does SET STATISTICS IO ON show in SQL Server?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The query execution time only"},{"id":"b","text":"The number of logical reads, physical reads, and read-ahead reads per table accessed"},{"id":"c","text":"The index names used in the query"},{"id":"d","text":"The memory usage of the query"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q11","text":"What does the EXPLAIN command do in PostgreSQL or MySQL?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Executes the query and returns results"},{"id":"b","text":"Shows the query execution plan without actually executing the query"},{"id":"c","text":"Explains the SQL syntax used"},{"id":"d","text":"Lists all indexes on a table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q12","text":"In an execution plan, what does a 'Missing Index' warning suggest?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An existing index has been corrupted"},{"id":"b","text":"The query optimizer has identified an index that could improve query performance"},{"id":"c","text":"The table has no primary key"},{"id":"d","text":"A required system index is missing"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q13","text":"What is index fragmentation?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"When an index has too many columns"},{"id":"b","text":"When index pages become logically or physically disordered due to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations, reducing efficiency"},{"id":"c","text":"When two indexes overlap on the same columns"},{"id":"d","text":"When an index is spread across multiple databases"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q14","text":"What is the difference between ALTER INDEX REBUILD and ALTER INDEX REORGANIZE?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"REBUILD drops and recreates the index (offline by default); REORGANIZE defragments in place and is always online"},{"id":"b","text":"REORGANIZE is more aggressive; REBUILD is lighter"},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical operations"},{"id":"d","text":"REBUILD only works on clustered indexes; REORGANIZE only on non-clustered"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q15","text":"What is a filtered index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"An index that excludes certain columns"},{"id":"b","text":"A non-clustered index with a WHERE clause that indexes only a subset of rows"},{"id":"c","text":"An index that automatically filters NULLs"},{"id":"d","text":"An index built only on filtered queries"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q16","text":"What is a columnstore index best suited for?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"OLTP workloads with many small transactions"},{"id":"b","text":"Data warehouse and analytical queries that scan large amounts of data across few columns"},{"id":"c","text":"Indexing text columns for full-text search"},{"id":"d","text":"Indexing foreign key columns for join performance"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q17","text":"How does a columnstore index differ from a traditional rowstore index?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Columnstore stores data column-by-column allowing high compression and batch processing; rowstore stores data row-by-row"},{"id":"b","text":"Columnstore is only available on primary key columns"},{"id":"c","text":"Rowstore is column-oriented; columnstore is row-oriented"},{"id":"d","text":"Columnstore indexes cannot be updated"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q18","text":"Which of the following query patterns would MOST benefit from a composite index on (LastName, columns not in the index"},{"id":"c","text":"The query is missing an index entirely"},{"id":"d","text":"The query is accessing a foreign key"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q20","text":"How query"},{"id":"c","text":"By converting the query to use SELECT *"},{"id":"d","text":"By adding more WHERE conditions"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q21","text":"What does index performance"},{"id":"c","text":"The index should be dropped"},{"id":"d","text":"The table is corrupt"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q22","text":"What is the 'leading column' rule for when the query filters on the leftmost (leading) columns of the index"},{"id":"c","text":"The leading column must always be the primary key"},{"id":"d","text":"There is no leading column rule in SQL access)"},{"id":"c","text":"The table is scanned using a non-clustered index"},{"id":"d","text":"A temporary table is being created"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_06_q25","text":"Which DMV in no clustered index — data pages are not ordered"},{"id":"c","text":"A temporary table stored in memory"},{"id":"d","text":"A table used only for bulk permissions"},{"id":"c","text":"Renames the stored procedure"},{"id":"d","text":"Copies the stored procedure to another database"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_08_q22","text":"Which function the last COMMIT or BEGIN TRANSACTION"},{"id":"c","text":"Starts a new transaction"},{"id":"d","text":"Locks a table"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_07_q4","text":"What does ACID stand for in back"},{"id":"c","text":"The database stores data in atomic units of storage"},{"id":"d","text":"The transaction cannot be split into smaller transactions"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_07_q6","text":"What does Consistency in ACID mean?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"All transactions run at the same speed"},{"id":"b","text":"A always return the same results"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_07_q7","text":"What does Durability in ACID guarantee?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The transaction will complete in a fixed that can never resolve"},{"id":"c","text":"When a transaction is waiting for a lock for more than 30 seconds"},{"id":"d","text":"When too many connections are open serial — intermediate states are not visible to other transactions"},{"id":"c","text":"Each transaction runs on isolated hardware"},{"id":"d","text":"Transactions cannot read each other's data under started"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_07_q27","text":"What type of lock does a SELECT statement typically acquire under READ COMMITTED?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Exclusive (X) independent transaction"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_07_q30","text":"What is the purpose of using TRY...CATCH with transactions?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"To speed up transaction statement"},{"id":"d","text":"To log the transaction to a file"}],"correctOptionId":"b"}]}$$::jsonb) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
-- paper: sql-window-functions.json

GROUP BY does not"},{"id":"b","text":"Window functions compute values across related rows while KEEPING all individual rows; GROUP BY reduces rows to one per group"},{"id":"c","text":"GROUP BY can use OVER(); window functions cannot"},{"id":"d","text":"Window functions are always faster than GROUP BY"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q4","text":"What does ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) assign?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A rank with gaps for tied values"},{"id":"b","text":"A unique sequential integer to each row in the specified order, with no gaps or ties"},{"id":"c","text":"The same number to rows with equal salary"},{"id":"d","text":"A random number"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q5","text":"Employees have salaries: 5000, 5000, 3000. What does RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) assign?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"1, 2, 3"},{"id":"b","text":"1, 1, 2"},{"id":"c","text":"1, 1, 3"},{"id":"d","text":"1, 2, 2"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q6","text":"What does DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) assign for salaries 5000, 5000, 3000?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"1, 1, 3"},{"id":"b","text":"1, 2, 3"},{"id":"c","text":"1, 1, 2"},{"id":"d","text":"0, 0, 1"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q7","text":"What is the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"RANK() assigns unique integers; DENSE_RANK() allows ties"},{"id":"b","text":"RANK() skips numbers after a tie; DENSE_RANK() does not skip numbers"},{"id":"c","text":"DENSE_RANK() skips numbers; RANK() does not"},{"id":"d","text":"They are identical"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q8","text":"What does NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY Salary) do?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Returns the 4th highest salary"},{"id":"b","text":"Divides the result set into 4 equal buckets (quartiles) and assigns each row a bucket number"},{"id":"c","text":"Returns the top 4 rows"},{"id":"d","text":"Assigns a tile number from 1 to 4 randomly"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q9","text":"What does LAG(Salary, 1) OVER (ORDER BY HireDate) return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The next row's salary in the ordered window"},{"id":"b","text":"The previous row's salary (1 row behind) in the ordered window"},{"id":"c","text":"The salary lagged by 1 second in time"},{"id":"d","text":"A cumulative lag calculation"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q10","text":"What does LEAD(Salary, 1) OVER (ORDER BY HireDate) return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The previous row's salary"},{"id":"b","text":"The next row's salary (1 row ahead) in the ordered window"},{"id":"c","text":"The leading (maximum) salary"},{"id":"d","text":"NULL always"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q11","text":"What does FIRST_VALUE(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptID ORDER BY Salary DESC) return?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The minimum salary in the department"},{"id":"b","text":"The salary of the first row in the ordered partition — the highest salary in each department"},{"id":"c","text":"The first salary value inserted into the table"},{"id":"d","text":"Always NULL"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q12","text":"What does LAST_VALUE() return and why might it behave unexpectedly without a frame specification?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"It always returns the last row of the entire table"},{"id":"b","text":"Without a ROWS/RANGE frame, the default frame ends at the current row, so LAST_VALUE returns the current row's value, not the partition's last row"},{"id":"c","text":"It returns NULL for all rows except the last"},{"id":"d","text":"It only works with PARTITION BY"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q13","text":"Which query computes a running total of sales ordered by SaleDate?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SELECT SaleDate, Amount, SUM(Amount) OVER (ORDER BY SaleDate) AS RunningTotal FROM Sales"},{"id":"b","text":"SELECT SaleDate, Amount, RUNNING_SUM(Amount) FROM Sales ORDER BY SaleDate"},{"id":"c","text":"SELECT SaleDate, SUM(Amount) FROM Sales GROUP BY SaleDate"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT SaleDate, CUMSUM(Amount) OVER SaleDate FROM Sales"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q14","text":"What does AVG(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptID) compute?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"The overall average salary across the entire table"},{"id":"b","text":"The average salary within each department, displayed on every row (without reducing rows)"},{"id":"c","text":"The cumulative average salary"},{"id":"d","text":"The average salary for each row individually"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q15","text":"What does the frame specification ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW define?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A window covering all rows after the current row"},{"id":"b","text":"A window from the first row of the partition to the current row — used for running totals"},{"id":"c","text":"A window covering only the current row"},{"id":"d","text":"A window covering a fixed number of rows before the current row"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q16","text":"What is the difference between ROWS and RANGE frame specifications?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"ROWS operates on a physical number of rows; RANGE operates on logical value ranges (all rows with the same ORDER BY value are included in the same peer group)"},{"id":"b","text":"RANGE operates on physical rows; ROWS on logical groups"},{"id":"c","text":"They are identical"},{"id":"d","text":"ROWS allows UNBOUNDED; RANGE does not"}],"correctOptionId":"a"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q17","text":"Can you filter results based on a window function result directly in the WHERE clause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"Yes, you can use WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER(...) = 1 directly"},{"id":"b","text":"No — window functions cannot be in WHERE; wrap the query in a subquery or CTE and filter there"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes, but only with RANK() and DENSE_RANK()"},{"id":"d","text":"Yes, in the HAVING clause only"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q18","text":"How do you use ROW_NUMBER() to return only the top 1 salary per department?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY DeptID ORDER BY Salary DESC) = 1"},{"id":"b","text":"WITH ranked AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY DeptID ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS rn FROM Employees) SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1"},{"id":"c","text":"SELECT TOP 1 * FROM Employees GROUP BY DeptID ORDER BY Salary DESC"},{"id":"d","text":"SELECT MAX(Salary) OVER (PARTITION BY DeptID) FROM Employees"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q19","text":"What does LAG(Salary, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY HireDate) return for the first row where there is no previous salary"}],"correctOptionId":"c"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q20","text":"What does ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW define in a window frame?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A window covering the current row and all rows after it"},{"id":"b","text":"A sliding window of 3 rows — the 2 rows before the current row plus the current row"},{"id":"c","text":"A window covering 2 rows after the current numbers"},{"id":"b","text":"Dividing data into quartiles, deciles, or percentiles for reporting and analysis"},{"id":"c","text":"Finding the previous row's value"},{"id":"d","text":"Computing cumulative sums"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q22","text":"Can a query contain multiple different window functions each with its own OVER() clause?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"No, only one OVER() clause is allowed per query"},{"id":"b","text":"Yes — multiple window functions with different OVER() definitions can exist in the same SELECT"},{"id":"c","text":"Yes, but all must share the same PARTITION BY"},{"id":"d","text":"Only if they use the same ORDER BY"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q23","text":"What does ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING define?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A window from the start of the partition to the current row"},{"id":"b","text":"A window from the current row to the last row of the partition"},{"id":"c","text":"A window covering only the current row"},{"id":"d","text":"An invalid frame specification"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q24","text":"Which window function bucket number as a percentage"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q27","text":"In what order does SQL Server process window functions relative to WHERE, GROUP BY, and filtered/grouped result set"},{"id":"c","text":"Window functions are evaluated before GROUP BY"},{"id":"d","text":"Window functions are evaluated at the same time as WHERE"}],"correctOptionId":"b"},{"id":"qp_sql_09_q28","text":"What does CUME_DIST() compute?","options":[{"id":"a","text":"A cumulative distribution value between 0 and 1, representing the fraction of
